title: Baseline retest for cores/bench
Iterative shift-subtract divider to shorten EX-stage critical path
motivation:
Current fitness is 301.04 iter/s with Fmax=135.21 MHz on Tang Nano 20K.
Fitness = Fmax × CoreMark iter/cycle, so raising Fmax lifts fitness
one-for-one. Profiling by inspection points to a single dominant
combinational critical path in the EX stage: alu.sv maps DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU
to SystemVerilog `/` and `%` operators on 32-bit operands. GOWIN LUT4
fabric has no division primitives, so Yosys must synthesize a ~30-level
LUT cascade of restoring-division compare/subtract/mux stages for each
of the four divide ops, feeding a mux that selects between them and
the other 15 ALU ops. This dwarfs the multiplier path (which lands on
DSP blocks) and the adder/shift paths, and is almost certainly what
caps Fmax at 135 MHz — well below the 150–170 MHz that a well-floor-
planned 5-stage core reaches on Tang Nano 20K when divide is iterative.
CoreMark itself issues essentially zero divide ops in its hot loops
(matrix, list, state machines use MUL/shifts/compares, not DIV/REM),
so the IPC cost of making divide multi-cycle is negligible — a few
dozen cycles per 449,148-cycle iteration at most, i.e. <0.01% IPC
regression against an 8–15% Fmax gain.

hypothesis.motivation:
Current fitness is 301.04 iter/s with Fmax=135.21 MHz on Tang Nano 20K.
Fitness = Fmax × CoreMark iter/cycle, so raising Fmax lifts fitness
one-for-one. Profiling by inspection points to a single dominant
combinational critical path in the EX stage: alu.sv maps DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU
to SystemVerilog `/` and `%` operators on 32-bit operands. GOWIN LUT4
fabric has no division primitives, so Yosys must synthesize a ~30-level
LUT cascade of restoring-division compare/subtract/mux stages for each
of the four divide ops, feeding a mux that selects between them and
the other 15 ALU ops. This dwarfs the multiplier path (which lands on
DSP blocks) and the adder/shift paths, and is almost certainly what
caps Fmax at 135 MHz — well below the 150–170 MHz that a well-floor-
planned 5-stage core reaches on Tang Nano 20K when divide is iterative.
CoreMark itself issues essentially zero divide ops in its hot loops
(matrix, list, state machines use MUL/shifts/compares, not DIV/REM),

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Extract the four divide operations (DIV, DIVU, REM, REMU) from alu.sv's
combinational case and implement them in a new `div_unit.sv` module as
an iterative 33-cycle non-restoring / shift-subtract divider:

- div_unit owns a small FSM: IDLE -> BUSY (32 iterations) -> DONE.
- On a DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU reaching EX with !busy, ex_stage asserts
  `div_start` with the op and operands; div_unit latches them, masks
  the sign for DIV/REM, and iterates shift-subtract on a 32-bit
  partial remainder. On the 33rd cycle it re-applies the sign to
  produce quotient/remainder and asserts `div_done` for one cycle.
- RV32M edge cases (divide-by-zero, INT_MIN/-1 signed overflow) are
  handled as single-cycle early-exits from IDLE before starting the
  iteration, matching the current ALU semantics bit-for-bit.
- ex_stage drives a new `div_busy` output that the hazard unit ORs
  into `stall_if`, `stall_id`, and `stall_ex_mem` — the LOAD/STORE
  stall path is identical structurally, so the existing forwarding
  logic, EX/MEM hold, and MEM/WB clear-valid behaviour are reused
  verbatim.
- alu.sv loses the DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU arms of its real (non-ALTOPS)
  case; the ALU.output for a divide op is now driven by div_unit's
  result register, muxed in ex_stage before the EX/MEM register.
- The ALTOPS branch in alu.sv (XOR-of-add/sub stand-ins) stays
  unchanged so `make formal` continues to prove the M-ext contract
  at PREUNSAT depth. The CLAUDE.md caveat about `make formal-deep`
  is what covers bit-accurate divide — and the cocotb `test_alu.py`
  vectors plus cosim against reference.py already exercise every
  edge case end-to-end.

Expected effect on Fmax: removing a ~30-level combinational LUT4
cascade from EX should lift the critical path enough for nextpnr
to route at 145–155 MHz (median across 3 seeds), a +8 to +14% gain.
Expected effect on IPC: CoreMark stall budget from DIV/REM is under
100 cycles per iteration (far under 0.1% of 449k cycles), so IPC is
effectively flat. Net fitness delta: +5 to +10%.

changes.description: New module. 33-cycle shift-subtract divider FSM producing DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU results. Single-cycle early-exit for div-by-zero and INT_MIN/-1 overflow matching current alu.sv semantics. Exposes start/busy/done/result ports.

Remove DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU arms from the non-ALTOPS case (divider now external). Keep ALTOPS branch untouched so `make formal` still proves via XOR stand-ins. ALU remains combinational for all other ops.
Instantiate div_unit. On divide ops, assert div_start, mux div_unit.result into the EX/MEM register's alu_result field when div_done, and expose div_busy to the hazard unit.
Add div_busy input. OR it into stall_if/stall_id/stall_ex_mem (same shape as dmem_stall path), and clear MEM/WB valid while busy so rvfi_order doesn't tick during the iteration.
Wire new div_busy signal from ex_stage to hazard_unit. No change to RVFI port set — divide instructions simply retire N cycles later through channel 0.

Verify existing 30+ M-ext bit-vector cases still pass bit-for-bit against the iterative divider (cocotb waits div_busy to deassert before sampling result). Add explicit vectors for div-by-zero and INT_MIN/-1 early-exit paths.

Add regression: back-to-back DIV/REM with RAW dependency on the quotient; LOAD followed immediately by DIV consuming the loaded value; branch that consumes DIV's result — all must retire in-order with correct rvfi_order and no channel-1 retirement.

New module. 33-cycle shift-subtract divider FSM producing DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU results. Single-cycle early-exit for div-by-zero and INT_MIN/-1 overflow matching current alu.sv semantics. Exposes start/busy/done/result ports.

Remove DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU arms from the non-ALTOPS case (divider now external). Keep ALTOPS branch untouched so `make formal` still proves via XOR stand-ins. ALU remains combinational for all other ops.
Instantiate div_unit. On divide ops, assert div_start, mux div_unit.result into the EX/MEM register's alu_result field when div_done, and expose div_busy to the hazard unit.
Add div_busy input. OR it into stall_if/stall_id/stall_ex_mem (same shape as dmem_stall path), and clear MEM/WB valid while busy so rvfi_order doesn't tick during the iteration.
Wire new div_busy signal from ex_stage to hazard_unit. No change to RVFI port set — divide instructions simply retire N cycles later through channel 0.

Verify existing 30+ M-ext bit-vector cases still pass bit-for-bit against the iterative divider (cocotb waits div_busy to deassert before sampling result). Add explicit vectors for div-by-zero and INT_MIN/-1 early-exit paths.

Add regression: back-to-back DIV/REM with RAW dependency on the quotient; LOAD followed immediately by DIV consuming the loaded value; branch that consumes DIV's result — all must retire in-order with correct rvfi_order and no channel-1 retirement.

title: Split M-extension into multi-cycle unit off EX critical path
motivation:
The current alu.sv computes, every cycle, three parallel 64-bit multipliers
(mul_ss signed*signed, mul_uu unsigned*unsigned, mul_su signed*unsigned)
and a combinational 32-bit divider (`/` and `%`), then muxes one into `out`
via the ALU_* case. On Tang Nano 20K (GW2AR-18C) this path is almost
certainly the Fmax bottleneck and a substantial share of the 9880 LUT
budget (target 10000 — only 120 LUTs of headroom). The baseline Fmax
seeds span 121..136 MHz, indicating P&R is pressure-sensitive — exactly
the signature of an overly wide combinational datapath in EX.

CoreMark's hot loop is dominated by ADD / SHIFT / LOAD / STORE / BRANCH;
MUL/DIV/REM occur only sporadically (CRC updates, the matrix/list code
uses a handful per iteration). So trading a multi-cycle M-unit (with a
small IPC hit on those few instructions) for a much shorter EX critical
path and lower LUT count is strictly fitness-positive for this workload.

hypothesis.motivation:
The current alu.sv computes, every cycle, three parallel 64-bit multipliers
(mul_ss signed*signed, mul_uu unsigned*unsigned, mul_su signed*unsigned)
and a combinational 32-bit divider (`/` and `%`), then muxes one into `out`
via the ALU_* case. On Tang Nano 20K (GW2AR-18C) this path is almost
certainly the Fmax bottleneck and a substantial share of the 9880 LUT
budget (target 10000 — only 120 LUTs of headroom). The baseline Fmax
seeds span 121..136 MHz, indicating P&R is pressure-sensitive — exactly
the signature of an overly wide combinational datapath in EX.

CoreMark's hot loop is dominated by ADD / SHIFT / LOAD / STORE / BRANCH;
MUL/DIV/REM occur only sporadically (CRC updates, the matrix/list code
uses a handful per iteration). So trading a multi-cycle M-unit (with a

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Split the ALU into two execution units and route ALU_MUL*, ALU_DIV*,
ALU_REM* to a new multi-cycle M-unit:

  1. `alu.sv` keeps only single-cycle ops (ADD/SUB/AND/OR/XOR/SLT/SLTU/
     SLL/SRL/SRA/LUI). The three 64-bit multipliers and the divider are
     deleted from its combinational body. The M-extension op decodes
     remain legal in decoder.sv (invariants #2, #10 preserved).

  2. A new `m_unit.sv` implements MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU as a 2-cycle
     pipelined unsigned 32x32 multiplier with sign-fixup at the output
     stage (Booth-style via sign-extension registers). DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU
     run as a 32-cycle iterative restoring divider. The unit has a
     `start` / `busy` / `done` handshake and an internal result register.

  3. `ex_stage.sv` gains a stall-output `m_busy`. When an id_ex_t enters
     EX with an M-extension op, the stage starts the M-unit, holds the
     ID/EX register via `stall_ex_mem` (wired through hazard_unit), and
     only advances into EX/MEM when the M-unit reports `done`. The ALTOPS
     path still does the algebraic stand-in in a single cycle so the
     formal gate's 20-step budget is unaffected.

  4. `hazard_unit.sv` widens `stall_ex_mem` to OR in `m_busy`. Because
     id_ex_t is held during a multi-cycle M-op, rvfi_order's +1 invariant
     (#4) is naturally preserved — the retirement happens exactly once
     when the M-unit finishes and MEM/WB captures the result.

Expected effect: removing four parallel 32-bit arithmetic units from the
EX critical path should raise median Fmax from ~135 MHz to ~155-170 MHz
(+15-25%), and free ~1500-2500 LUTs (the three combinational multipliers
are the largest single block; the divider is similarly expensive because
the GW2AR lacks dedicated div hardware). The IPC hit is bounded: at
~10 MUL/iter * 1 extra cycle + ~2 DIV/iter * 31 extra cycles ≈ 70-80
added cycles per CoreMark iteration on a baseline of ~449k cycles/iter —
well under 0.1% slowdown. Net: +10-20% fitness.

Implementation notes: keep the ALTOPS branch of alu.sv exactly as-is
(single-cycle, formal-tractable) and only the `else` branch changes. The
M-unit always reports done in 1 cycle under ALTOPS so formal stays
bounded. test/test_alu.py vectors for M-ext move to test/test_m_unit.py
with the same 30+ vectors per op; test/test_pipeline.py gets a multi-
cycle-stall smoke test (a MUL immediately followed by a dependent ADD).

changes.description: Remove the three combinational 64-bit multipliers and the combinational 32-bit divider from the non-ALTOPS branch. Keep only RV32I single-cycle ops. ALTOPS branch stays byte-identical so formal-gate bounds are unchanged.

New module: multi-cycle M-extension execution unit. 2-cycle pipelined 32x32 multiplier with sign fix-up for MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU. 32-cycle iterative restoring divider for DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU. Handshake: start/busy/done/result. ALTOPS branch collapses to 1-cycle algebraic stand-in.

Instantiate m_unit alongside alu. When id_ex_t carries an M-ext opcode, start the m_unit, hold the EX/MEM register until m_unit.done, and route m_unit.result into reg_q.alu_result when done fires. Emit m_busy upward to hazard_unit.
Add m_busy input; OR it into stall_ex_mem and stall_id so the pipeline freezes while an M-ext op is in flight. Forwarding correctness preserved because ID/EX is held with its original operands.
Wire new m_busy signal between ex_stage and hazard_unit. No change to top-level IO, RVFI port set, or memory contract.
Remove M-extension vectors (they move to test_m_unit.py). Keep base RV32I coverage intact.

New cocotb suite: 30+ vectors per MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU/DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU covering all RV32M corner cases (div-by-zero, INT_MIN/-1, negative operands). Verifies the start/busy/done handshake completes in 2 cycles for MUL and 32 cycles for DIV.
Add a multi-cycle-stall regression: MUL rs,a,b followed immediately by ADD rd,rs,... asserts rd sees the MUL result on the correct cycle and rvfi_order increments only once per retirement.

Add a multi-cycle-stall regression: MUL rs,a,b followed immediately by ADD rd,rs,... asserts rd sees the MUL result on the correct cycle and rvfi_order increments only once per retirement.

title: BTB with 1-bit hysteresis for taken branches and unconditional jumps
motivation:
The current 5-stage in-order pipeline resolves branches and jumps in EX,
producing a 2-cycle redirect penalty on every taken branch and every
JAL/JALR (IF/ID is flushed to NOP and ID/EX is flushed to a bubble
before the redirected instruction reaches IF). CoreMark is dominated
by tight loops (core_list_find, crcu8/crcu16, core_bench_matrix inner
loops) — 10-20% of dynamic instructions are control-flow, and the
vast majority of those are backward loop-closing branches that are
almost always taken. Assuming ~15% branches+jumps at ~60% taken rate
and 2 cycles of penalty each, the lost IPC is on the order of 18%
of total CoreMark cycles. Removing even 70% of those bubbles is the
single biggest IPC lever this core has short of going superscalar,
and it does not touch the RVFI contract, the memory interface, or
the ALU path. No prior iteration has attempted this — the baseline
core has zero prediction, PC always fetches pc+4 until EX redirects.

hypothesis.motivation:
The current 5-stage in-order pipeline resolves branches and jumps in EX,
producing a 2-cycle redirect penalty on every taken branch and every
JAL/JALR (IF/ID is flushed to NOP and ID/EX is flushed to a bubble
before the redirected instruction reaches IF). CoreMark is dominated
by tight loops (core_list_find, crcu8/crcu16, core_bench_matrix inner
loops) — 10-20% of dynamic instructions are control-flow, and the
vast majority of those are backward loop-closing branches that are
almost always taken. Assuming ~15% branches+jumps at ~60% taken rate
and 2 cycles of penalty each, the lost IPC is on the order of 18%
of total CoreMark cycles. Removing even 70% of those bubbles is the
single biggest IPC lever this core has short of going superscalar,
and it does not touch the RVFI contract, the memory interface, or
the ALU path. No prior iteration has attempted this — the baseline
core has zero prediction, PC always fetches pc+4 until EX redirects.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a small direct-mapped Branch Target Buffer (BTB) in the IF stage
with 32 entries indexed by PC[6:2] (5 bits). Each entry holds
{valid, tag[24:0], target[31:1], taken_bit}. The taken_bit is a
1-bit hysteresis state (updated on every resolved branch/jump in
EX) — simpler than 2-bit counters but still captures loop
branches, which are what dominate CoreMark.

Fetch-side: in IF, combinationally look up BTB[PC[6:2]]. If
valid & tag-match & taken_bit, predict taken — the next PC is the
stored target instead of pc+4, and the IF/ID bundle carries a
"predicted" flag (packed into an unused bit of if_id_t or a new
field) so EX knows this instruction was speculatively redirected.

EX-side: when a branch or jump is resolved, compare the actual
outcome (taken & target) against the prediction carried with the
instruction:
  - correctly-predicted taken branch / jump with matching target:
    no redirect, no flush, 0-cycle penalty.
  - mispredict (wrongly predicted taken, wrongly predicted
    not-taken, or correct direction but wrong target for JALR):
    redirect + flush as today, 2-cycle penalty.
Update the BTB entry in the same cycle with the resolved outcome
(write port driven from EX). JAL writes its immediate target.
JALR updates the stored target each time it resolves (single-target
approximation — good enough for monomorphic call/return sites
common in CoreMark's non-recursive call graph; polymorphic sites
fall back to the existing mispredict path at baseline cost).

Misalignment and illegal-instruction discipline are unaffected:
predictions only redirect PC; the instruction itself still decodes
and traps/retires exactly as before, because EX is the authoritative
resolver. RVFI fields remain driven by the MEM/WB retirement
register, unchanged.

Sizing rationale: 32 entries × (1+25+31+1) = ~58 bits per entry
= ~1860 bits. This fits comfortably in distributed LUT-RAM on the
Tang Nano 20K (no BRAM pressure), and the lookup is a single
LUT-indexed read with a small equality comparator — the critical
path stays under the existing IF-to-ID slack window. If the BTB
lookup+mux pushes Fmax down, the fallback is to pipeline the BTB
read one cycle (predict the taken target on the cycle AFTER the
branch is fetched, losing 1 cycle of the 2-cycle gain — still a
net win). The cocotb pipeline test and the existing riscv-formal
wrapper will catch any RVFI or liveness regression; the VexRiscv
cosim with ~22% random istall/dstall will catch any interaction
with the existing hazard/redirect priority logic.

changes.description: New module. 32-entry direct-mapped BTB with {valid, tag, target, taken} per entry. Combinational read port for IF; synchronous write port for EX updates.
Add `predicted` (and optionally `pred_target`) field to if_id_t and propagate through id_ex_t / ex_mem_t so EX can compare prediction vs. resolved outcome.
Instantiate the BTB. On BTB hit with taken_bit set, override next_pc with the BTB target (still subordinate to EX redirect priority). Emit `predicted` and `pred_target` on the IF/ID bundle.
Plumb `predicted` / `pred_target` from IF/ID through into the ID/EX register (no decode changes — purely pass-through metadata).

Compare resolved (taken, target) against the incoming prediction. Suppress the redirect+flush when the prediction matches; issue redirect+flush on mispredict. Drive BTB update port with resolved outcome on every branch/jump.
On correctly-predicted taken branch/jump, do not assert flush_if/flush_id — the IF stream already followed the predicted path. Mispredicts retain the current flush behavior.
New cocotb suite: unit tests for BTB lookup, update, tag mismatch, aliasing collisions, and a mini-loop trace asserting zero mispredicts after warmup on a 16-iteration backward-branch loop.

Augment with a fetch-redirect-during-prediction scenario: interleave a branch that aliases another in BTB, confirm rvfi_order stays monotonic and pc_wdata is correct on both mispredict and correct-predict cycles.

Instantiate the BTB. On BTB hit with taken_bit set, override next_pc with the BTB target (still subordinate to EX redirect priority). Emit `predicted` and `pred_target` on the IF/ID bundle.
Plumb `predicted` / `pred_target` from IF/ID through into the ID/EX register (no decode changes — purely pass-through metadata).

Compare resolved (taken, target) against the incoming prediction. Suppress the redirect+flush when the prediction matches; issue redirect+flush on mispredict. Drive BTB update port with resolved outcome on every branch/jump.
On correctly-predicted taken branch/jump, do not assert flush_if/flush_id — the IF stream already followed the predicted path. Mispredicts retain the current flush behavior.
New cocotb suite: unit tests for BTB lookup, update, tag mismatch, aliasing collisions, and a mini-loop trace asserting zero mispredicts after warmup on a 16-iteration backward-branch loop.

Augment with a fetch-redirect-during-prediction scenario: interleave a branch that aliases another in BTB, confirm rvfi_order stays monotonic and pc_wdata is correct on both mispredict and correct-predict cycles.

title: Pre-register rd_wdata at MEM/WB boundary to shorten forward + regfile read paths
motivation:
Prior round's two M-extension latency splits (hyp-001 divider, hyp-002
M-unit) both returned fitness delta = 0.00%, which means the EX-stage
divider was NOT actually the Fmax-limiting combinational path — Yosys
was already factoring it well or another path dominates. The BTB
attempt (hyp-003) broke formal.

Re-reading the RTL with fresh eyes: the WB-stage mux
`w_data = in.ctrl.mem_to_reg ? in.read_data : in.alu_result`
is purely combinational, and its output fans out to TWO critical-path
consumers:

  1. `fwd_mem_wb` into ex_stage's rs1/rs2 forwarding muxes, meaning
     the EX-stage ALU's operand path is
     (MEM/WB flop) → wb_stage mux → fwd_rs?_sel mux → is_auipc mux →
     ALU → EX/MEM flop — four mux levels before ALU input.
  2. `w_data` into reg_file's write-first read bypass, meaning the
     ID-stage's regfile read path is
     (MEM/WB flop) → wb_stage mux → regfile bypass mux → ID/EX flop
     — two mux levels on every register read the WB instruction's
     rd matches.

That extra mux level lives on both sides of the pipeline and is a
plausible candidate for the actual Fmax cap. Fmax gains translate
1:1 to fitness since CoreMark iter/cycle is unaffected by this
transformation (the functional mux just moves one pipe stage earlier).

hypothesis.motivation:
Prior round's two M-extension latency splits (hyp-001 divider, hyp-002
M-unit) both returned fitness delta = 0.00%, which means the EX-stage
divider was NOT actually the Fmax-limiting combinational path — Yosys
was already factoring it well or another path dominates. The BTB
attempt (hyp-003) broke formal.

Re-reading the RTL with fresh eyes: the WB-stage mux
`w_data = in.ctrl.mem_to_reg ? in.read_data : in.alu_result`
is purely combinational, and its output fans out to TWO critical-path
consumers:

  1. `fwd_mem_wb` into ex_stage's rs1/rs2 forwarding muxes, meaning
     the EX-stage ALU's operand path is
     (MEM/WB flop) → wb_stage mux → fwd_rs?_sel mux → is_auipc mux →
     ALU → EX/MEM flop — four mux levels before ALU input.
  2. `w_data` into reg_file's write-first read bypass, meaning the
     ID-stage's regfile read path is
     (MEM/WB flop) → wb_stage mux → regfile bypass mux → ID/EX flop
     — two mux levels on every register read the WB instruction's
     rd matches.

That extra mux level lives on both sides of the pipeline and is a
plausible candidate for the actual Fmax cap. Fmax gains translate
1:1 to fitness since CoreMark iter/cycle is unaffected by this
transformation (the functional mux just moves one pipe stage earlier).
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Consolidate the WB-stage write-data mux into the MEM stage so the
selected rd_wdata is clocked into the MEM/WB register directly,
turning wb_stage into a trivial `w_data = in.rd_wdata` pass-through.

Concretely:
 - Extend `mem_wb_t` in core_pkg.sv with a new field
   `logic [31:0] rd_wdata;`  (can replace `read_data` since
   read_data is only consumed by wb_stage's mux anyway; keep
   read_data for the RVFI-accurate rmask path if needed).
 - In mem_stage.sv, compute
   `rd_wdata = in.ctrl.mem_to_reg ? load_data : in.alu_result;`
   and assign it to `reg_q.rd_wdata`. This is a 32-bit 2:1 mux on
   already-combinational outputs of the same stage — zero added
   delay in MEM (it fits behind the existing `load_data` combo
   without deepening that cone).
 - In wb_stage.sv, set `w_data = in.rd_wdata;`
   — one LUT level of mux gone from the forwarding + regfile
   read-bypass critical paths.

This is an IPC-neutral transformation (same retirement semantics,
same forwarded values, same regfile writes) that purely shortens two
combinational cones by one mux level each. It must stay IPC-neutral
because `load_data` was already computed combinationally in mem_stage
every cycle; we just route its mux result through the existing
MEM/WB flop instead of re-doing the mux combinationally in WB.

Expected Fmax lift: 3–8% (one LUT-mux level out of a typical 7–10
level path on GW2A). Zero IPC change. Zero formal-state expansion
(no new pipeline payload, same retirement sequence). RVFI contract
preserved: wb_stage still drives w_en/w_addr/w_data identically at
the interface; only the internal provenance of w_data changes.

Simplify w_data to pass mem_wb.rd_wdata straight through; remove the combinational mem_to_reg mux.
Ensure fwd_mem_wb input to ex_stage remains wired to wb_stage's w_data output (now a pass-through of the registered rd_wdata) so the forwarding critical path shortens by one mux level.

title: Zero-bubble JAL via IF-stage early redirect
motivation:
The 5-stage pipeline resolves all control flow in EX. Because JAL/JALR
set reg_write, they are always routed through EX to compute rd=PC+4 and
signal redirect. For an unconditional JAL this is wasteful: the target
is purely a function of the in-flight instruction bits (PC + sign-extended
J-imm), fully available the same cycle the word leaves imem. The current
penalty is 2 bubbles per JAL (IF/ID-fetch-of-PC+4 becomes NOP via flush_if
when EX redirects, and the next cycle's IF also delivers no useful work
until pc<=target takes effect). CoreMark's hot loops are riddled with
function-call JALs (core_bench_list/matrix/state all call helpers in
their per-iteration critical path, plus all crcu* calls, plus epilogue
JALR returns). Eliminating just the JAL bubbles (not JALR, which needs
rs1) is worth a conservative 3-6% IPC on CoreMark.

Prior structural attempts failed on different axes: r1s0/r1s1 targeted
the M-extension datapath and regressed (likely Fmax didn't move because
the divider is DSP-muxed, not LUT-dominated, once Yosys maps it; or LUT
count grew). r1s2 (BTB) broke formal by threading speculative state
(`predicted`, `pred_target`) through the RVFI-observed payload, which
blew the per-instruction BMC wall-clock budget. This hypothesis avoids
both failure modes: JAL redirect is deterministic (never mispredicted),
introduces no speculative wrong-path state, and doesn't touch the ALU.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Decode JAL (opcode 0x6F) combinationally in if_stage.sv. When the
fetched word is JAL and the IF output is valid (not flushed by an EX
redirect, imem_stall, or reset), compute the J-type immediate and
override next_pc with pc + imm_J instead of pc + 4. The JAL instruction
itself still flows normally through ID/EX/MEM/WB so its PC+4 writeback
retires and RVFI sees the canonical pc_wdata = target. EX must NOT
re-redirect for a JAL that IF already jumped, so a single-bit
`early_jumped` flag is added to `if_id_t` (propagated into `id_ex_t`
as a field separate from ctrl_t). `ex_stage` ANDs its redirect
condition with `!in.early_jumped` for the JAL case (is_jump &&
!is_jalr). JALR is untouched (it needs rs1 + forwarding and has no
purely-IF-visible target).

Cost in logic:
  - A 32-bit J-imm extractor (existing pattern from imm_gen.sv) and
    a 2:1 mux on next_pc gated by `imem_data[6:0] == 7'b1101111`.
  - 1-bit `early_jumped` through 2 pipeline registers.
Expected Fmax impact: the added IF-stage adder is 32-bit pc + 32-bit
sign-extended imm, parallel with the existing pc+4 incrementer — it
is unlikely to become the new critical path given EX currently owns
Fmax via the ALU/multiplier mux. Worst case Fmax gives up ~2-3%;
IPC gain from eliminating ~2 bubbles on every dynamically-retired
JAL (CoreMark has thousands per iteration) should dominate.

Correctness hooks:
  - RVFI: pc_wdata of the JAL is always `pc + imm_J` whether IF
    early-jumped or EX did. rd_wdata stays PC+4. No change to any
    RVFI field → riscv-formal `pc_fwd` / `unique` / `causal` checks
    remain closed with zero added state-space work.
  - Formal: the `early_jumped` flag is a pure function of decoded
    opcode + IF stall/flush signals — not speculative, not predicted
    — so SBY's BMC depth is unchanged.
  - Traps: a misaligned JAL target still traps in EX (its
    misalign_fault path fires there as before; we gate the EX redirect
    but not the misalign_fault computation, which also suppresses
    reg_write and leaves pc_next = pc + 4).

changes.description: Add a single-bit `early_jumped` field to if_id_t and id_ex_t (outside ctrl_t so decoder default-illegal semantics are unaffected).

Detect JAL (opcode 7'b1101111) on imem_data, extract the J-imm, and override next_pc = pc + imm_J when the IF output would otherwise be a valid instruction. Set out.early_jumped accordingly. Preserve reset/flush/stall priority so imem_stall and EX redirects still win over the early-jump.
Propagate in.early_jumped into the ID/EX register as a standalone field (zero when reset/flush).

Gate the redirect output for unconditional JAL (is_jump && !is_jalr) with !in.early_jumped so IF's in-flight target fetch is not flushed. Misalign_fault still asserts and still overrides via the existing trap path.

Add a JAL-latency cocotb test: two JALs back-to-back to verify zero bubble cycles in rvfi_order deltas, plus a branch-then-JAL interleaving test to confirm EX redirects still win when IF's JAL-prediction would race an older redirect, plus a misaligned-JAL test (imm with bit[1] set) to confirm trap semantics are preserved.

Propagate in.early_jumped into the ID/EX register as a standalone field (zero when reset/flush).

Gate the redirect output for unconditional JAL (is_jump && !is_jalr) with !in.early_jumped so IF's in-flight target fetch is not flushed. Misalign_fault still asserts and still overrides via the existing trap path.

Add a JAL-latency cocotb test: two JALs back-to-back to verify zero bubble cycles in rvfi_order deltas, plus a branch-then-JAL interleaving test to confirm EX redirects still win when IF's JAL-prediction would race an older redirect, plus a misaligned-JAL test (imm with bit[1] set) to confirm trap semantics are preserved.

title: Stateless IF-stage static JAL-taken prediction (zero bubble on unconditional jumps)
motivation:
The 5-stage baseline pays a 2-cycle redirect penalty on every taken
branch AND every unconditional jump. For JAL (opcode 0x6F) this
penalty is pure waste: JAL is unconditionally taken, and its target
is PC + a sign-extended immediate extracted directly from the
instruction word — IF can compute it as soon as imem_data arrives,
with no prior history. CoreMark is heavy on function calls (the
inner crcu8/crcu16/list_find loops all make indirect calls; the
harness is littered with JAL prologue/epilogue pairs), so even a
modest share of control-flow instructions being JAL (empirically
~30–40% of taken branches in RV32 compiled code) translates to
~1–3% of total CoreMark cycles wasted as JAL redirect bubbles.

Prior iter (hyp-20260502-003-r1s2) tried a full BTB and blew the
1800s formal budget because it threaded a `predicted` flag and
`pred_target` through if_id_t / id_ex_t, expanding the per-insn
state space seen by SBY. The lesson is: keep prediction OUT of the
RVFI-observed pipeline payload. A static JAL predictor needs no
state at all — the "prediction" is always correct by construction,
so no comparison or recovery logic is required downstream.

Add combinational JAL-target computation inside if_stage.sv and use
it to redirect the PC register in the SAME cycle the JAL is fetched,
eliminating the normal 2-cycle EX-redirect bubble for every JAL.

Implementation details:

  1. if_stage.sv decodes the currently-fetched imem_data for JAL
     (opcode == 7'b1101111) combinationally. If JAL AND the fetch
     is valid (no flush, no imem stall, no EX redirect this cycle),
     compute jal_imm the same way imm_gen does:
       jal_imm = {{11{instr[31]}}, instr[31], instr[19:12],
                  instr[20], instr[30:21], 1'b0}
     and set next_pc = pc + jal_imm instead of pc + 4. EX redirect
     still has top priority over the JAL prediction (so a redirect
     in flight this cycle correctly wins). The JAL instruction
     itself is still emitted to ID normally so it decodes, writes
     PC+4 to rd in EX, and retires via the usual RVFI path.

  2. ex_stage.sv: narrow the redirect condition to exclude JAL.
     Currently redirect fires on (branch_taken || in.ctrl.is_jump)
     — change to (branch_taken || in.ctrl.is_jalr). JALR and taken
     branches still redirect as today. JAL never does, because IF
     has already fetched from the correct target. The misalignment
     trap path stays unchanged (JAL immediates have bit0 forced to
     0, and PC is 4-aligned, so JAL-misalign is impossible without
     C-ext; the existing misalign check remains but is dead code
     for JAL specifically).

  3. reg_q.pc_next in EX is UNCHANGED — it still records the
     jump_target for RVFI's pc_wdata. RVFI correctness is identical
     to the baseline; only the externally-visible redirect signal
     is suppressed.

  4. No changes to if_id_t / id_ex_t / ex_mem_t / mem_wb_t. No new
     pipeline payload bits. No BTB, no counters, no tables. The
     formal state space is unchanged — the IF combinational logic
     just has one extra mux on next_pc, and EX has a narrower OR
     condition on redirect. SBY should proof this as fast as the
     baseline.

Why this is always safe without a prediction-correctness check:
a JAL can only reach EX when (a) it was fetched with flush=0 and
redirect=0, and (b) imem delivered the real instruction (not a
NOP). In both cases the IF-stage JAL predictor saw the same
opcode and computed the same target, so the EX-stage jump_target
provably matches the target IF already redirected to. When
flush=1 or redirect=1 at IF, the emitted instr is NOP (0x00000013),
so ctrl.is_jump is 0 when it reaches EX — nothing to redirect.
When imem_ready=0, flush_if is asserted upstream (via
hazard_unit), same story. So no "was this predicted?" bit is
ever needed.

Expected delta: saves exactly 2 cycles per dynamically-executed
JAL. Estimated JAL density in CoreMark ~2–4% of all retired
instructions; over 449k cycles this is ~2–4% IPC lift. Fmax
impact should be near-zero — the added IF path is a 32-bit adder
with a constant-pattern opcode compare in parallel with the
existing pc+4 adder, slotted into the same IF combinational
cone. Net fitness: +2 to +4%.

changes.description: Add combinational JAL-target predictor: decode imem_data[6:0]==0x6F, extract JAL immediate, mux next_pc to pc+jal_imm when JAL is fetched (gated by !redirect and !flush, with EX redirect keeping top priority). No state, no new register.

Narrow redirect to (branch_taken || in.ctrl.is_jalr) && !misalign_fault — JAL no longer fires redirect because IF already handled it correctly. reg_q.pc_next still records jump_target for RVFI pc_wdata. jump_target/branch_target calculation unchanged.

Add regressions: (1) a JAL at arbitrary PC with arbitrary sign-extended immediate retires with correct pc_wdata on channel 0 and the instruction fetched on the cycle after JAL is the one at the JAL target (not pc+4); (2) a JAL immediately after a taken branch still picks up the redirect target first, then the predicted JAL target on the next cycle; (3) JAL with imem_ready=0 in its fetch cycle correctly stalls PC and does NOT predict (NOP is emitted); (4) back-to-back JAL chain retires every cycle (no bubbles between JALs).

Add combinational JAL-target predictor: decode imem_data[6:0]==0x6F, extract JAL immediate, mux next_pc to pc+jal_imm when JAL is fetched (gated by !redirect and !flush, with EX redirect keeping top priority). No state, no new register.

Narrow redirect to (branch_taken || in.ctrl.is_jalr) && !misalign_fault — JAL no longer fires redirect because IF already handled it correctly. reg_q.pc_next still records jump_target for RVFI pc_wdata. jump_target/branch_target calculation unchanged.

Add regressions: (1) a JAL at arbitrary PC with arbitrary sign-extended immediate retires with correct pc_wdata on channel 0 and the instruction fetched on the cycle after JAL is the one at the JAL target (not pc+4); (2) a JAL immediately after a taken branch still picks up the redirect target first, then the predicted JAL target on the next cycle; (3) JAL with imem_ready=0 in its fetch cycle correctly stalls PC and does NOT predict (NOP is emitted); (4) back-to-back JAL chain retires every cycle (no bubbles between JALs).

lesson: - 2026-05-02 hyp-20260502-003-r2s2 (improvement, +5.87%): A stateless IF-stage JAL predictor — combinationally decode opcode 0x6F, compute `pc + J-imm`, guard on `target[1:0]==0` to preserve misalign-trap semantics, and narrow EX redirect to `(branch_taken || is_jalr)` — eliminates the 2-cycle JAL bubble without adding any pipeline payload bits or new formal state, confirming the r1s2 lesson: prediction that is *provably correct by construction* needs no `predicted` flag, no recovery logic, and leaves SBY proof time unchanged while still netting ~5-6% CoreMark fitness from JAL-heavy call/return sequences.

title: Fold MUL variants into single 33x33 signed multiplier with sign-ext mux
motivation:
The current alu.sv computes three separate 64-bit products unconditionally
on every EX cycle: mul_ss (signed*signed), mul_uu (unsigned*unsigned), and
mul_su (signed*unsigned). Yosys synthesises each as a distinct 33x33 signed
multiplier, and on Tang Nano 20K (GW2A) each multiplier instantiates a
dedicated DSP18 (or LUT-cascade) block. Running three DSPs in parallel for
every EX cycle costs LUT4/FF for operand replication and congests the DSP
routing channel, contributing to Fmax ceiling at 141.58 MHz. The prior
"split M-extension into multi-cycle unit" attempt (r1s1) regressed because
moving MUL off the EX critical path hurt IPC more than it helped Fmax —
evidence that the multiplier is timing-tolerable when kept single-cycle
but has free area/routing headroom to give up.

hypothesis.motivation:
The current alu.sv computes three separate 64-bit products unconditionally
on every EX cycle: mul_ss (signed*signed), mul_uu (unsigned*unsigned), and
mul_su (signed*unsigned). Yosys synthesises each as a distinct 33x33 signed
multiplier, and on Tang Nano 20K (GW2A) each multiplier instantiates a
dedicated DSP18 (or LUT-cascade) block. Running three DSPs in parallel for
every EX cycle costs LUT4/FF for operand replication and congests the DSP
routing channel, contributing to Fmax ceiling at 141.58 MHz. The prior
"split M-extension into multi-cycle unit" attempt (r1s1) regressed because
moving MUL off the EX critical path hurt IPC more than it helped Fmax —
evidence that the multiplier is timing-tolerable when kept single-cycle
but has free area/routing headroom to give up.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Replace mul_ss / mul_uu / mul_su with a single 33-bit signed multiplier
whose operand sign bits are muxed from op: MUL / MULH use signed*signed
(sign-extend both), MULHU uses unsigned*unsigned (zero-extend both),
MULHSU uses signed*unsigned (sign-extend a, zero-extend b). MUL only
reads the low 32 bits of the 66-bit product, so the "signed vs unsigned
a" sign bit is a don't-care for MUL and we alias it to MULH (signed).
The substitution is confined to alu.sv and only affects the
!RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS branch; the ALTOPS XOR stand-ins and the
divider logic stay byte-for-byte identical. Expected wins:
  * DSP block count drops 3 -> 1 (frees two DSPs, reduces routing
    pressure around the EX critical path).
  * LUT4 / FF footprint of the ALU shrinks because operand
    sign-extension wires collapse into a single 33-bit pair.
  * Fmax ceiling may lift 2-5% if the three-DSP routing was the
    binding path; IPC is identical (single-cycle semantics preserved).
Correctness is covered by test/test_alu.py's existing MUL/MULH/MULHU/
MULHSU vectors and cosim's field-by-field diff against reference.py.
Formal runs under ALTOPS so formal time is unchanged. No pipeline
payload bits change; no RVFI tap repoints; no formal state expansion.

changes.description: Replace the three parallel mul_ss / mul_uu / mul_su 64-bit products with one 33x33 signed multiplier. Mux the operand sign-extension bits from the ALU op code: signed-extend rs1 for MUL/MULH/MULHSU (zero for MULHU); signed-extend rs2 for MUL/MULH (zero for MULHU/MULHSU). Keep the 66-bit product; select low-32 for MUL, high-32 for MULH/MULHU/MULHSU. Leave the RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS branch untouched. Leave DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU untouched.

Extend the existing MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU coverage with a corner-case sweep (INT_MIN*INT_MIN, INT_MIN*-1, 0xFFFFFFFF*0xFFFFFFFF, 0x80000000*0x00000001) to prove the muxed-sign multiplier matches the three-multiplier reference bit-for-bit under both signed and unsigned interpretations; add a directed test that MUL's low-32 output is independent of the chosen sign-ext path (since low bits are invariant under sign-extension).

Replace the three parallel mul_ss / mul_uu / mul_su 64-bit products with one 33x33 signed multiplier. Mux the operand sign-extension bits from the ALU op code: signed-extend rs1 for MUL/MULH/MULHSU (zero for MULHU); signed-extend rs2 for MUL/MULH (zero for MULHU/MULHSU). Keep the 66-bit product; select low-32 for MUL, high-32 for MULH/MULHU/MULHSU. Leave the RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS branch untouched. Leave DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU untouched.

Extend the existing MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU coverage with a corner-case sweep (INT_MIN*INT_MIN, INT_MIN*-1, 0xFFFFFFFF*0xFFFFFFFF, 0x80000000*0x00000001) to prove the muxed-sign multiplier matches the three-multiplier reference bit-for-bit under both signed and unsigned interpretations; add a directed test that MUL's low-32 output is independent of the chosen sign-ext path (since low bits are invariant under sign-extension).

lesson: - 2026-05-02 hyp-20260502-001-r3s0 (improvement, +8.50%): Folding MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU onto a single 33x33 signed multiplier with muxed sign-extension bits (aliasing MUL to the signed-signed path since low-32 bits are invariant under operand sign-extension) cuts GW2A DSP18 instantiations from 3 to 1 and nets a large fitness gain without touching IPC or formal proof time — on Tang Nano 20K the three-parallel-DSP ALU was area/routing-bound, not timing-bound, so consolidating redundant arithmetic trees pays off even when the sub-expressions are already single-cycle; always check for structural DSP duplication before chasing Fmax on EX-stage critical paths.

title: Stateless IF-stage backward-taken branch prediction (BTFNT)
motivation:
The successful r2s2 JAL predictor (+5.87%) eliminated the 2-cycle redirect
bubble for unconditional jumps. The next structural lever on the same
axis is conditional branches — still paying a 2-cycle EX-redirect penalty
every time a branch is taken. In CoreMark's hot loops (core_list_find,
crcu8/crcu16 bit loops, core_bench_matrix nested loops, core_state state
machines) the dominant control flow is loop-closing backward branches
that are taken on every iteration except the last. A 5-iteration loop
pays 2 bubbles × 4 = 8 cycles of taken-branch redirect and 0 cycles on
the exit-fallthrough; a 50-iteration loop pays 98 cycles of bubbles.

Static backwards-taken / forwards-not-taken (BTFNT) is the classic
prediction heuristic that captures this without any history: a BRANCH
instruction whose sign-extended B-immediate is negative is almost
certainly a loop edge. The r1s2 BTB lesson (broken, formal timeout)
was that threading speculative state through the RVFI-observed payload
blows the per-insn BMC budget. The r2s2 lesson was the exact opposite
pattern succeeds: a *stateless* predictor whose decision is a pure
function of the already-observed (PC, instr) pair costs zero formal
state because EX can re-derive the IF-side decision from the same bits
it already has in ID/EX.

On CoreMark (~15% branch frequency, ~70% backward-taken), eliminating
the 2-cycle bubble on backward-taken branches while paying a 2-cycle
bubble on the ~30% of backward branches that fall through (loop
exits, rare) is net-positive IPC. Rough estimate: 0.15 × 0.70 × 2 =
0.21 cycle savings per dynamic instr, offset by 0.15 × 0.30 × 2 = 0.09
new mispredict cost ≈ 0.12 cycle/instr net, i.e. ~8–10% IPC win on
top of the JAL baseline. Fmax impact is expected to be minimal:
the IF-stage critical path grows by one 32-bit adder + alignment
check (same incremental work the JAL predictor already added), and
EX-stage redirect logic gains a single XOR comparing predicted vs.
actual branch-taken — far below the current Fmax-limiting ALU/divider
mux reported by r1s0/r1s1.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a stateless BTFNT predictor at IF that mirrors the JAL-predictor
pattern already in `if_stage.sv`:

1. IF stage (rtl/if_stage.sv):
   - Combinationally decode the fetched word. When it is a BRANCH
     opcode (0x63), funct3 is a valid branch (not 2/3), the
     sign-extended B-immediate is negative (imm[31]==1, i.e. backward),
     AND the computed target `pc + imm` is 4-byte aligned (target[1:0]
     == 0), redirect the PC register to the branch target this same
     cycle — exactly like the existing JAL predictor. No pipeline
     payload bits are added; no "predicted" flag threaded anywhere.
   - The fetched branch instruction itself still flows into ID/EX/MEM
     as a normal branch. The next fetched word comes from the
     predicted target (not pc+4).
   - Guards: same as JAL — skip prediction on flush/redirect in the
     same cycle; skip on misaligned target; skip on `funct3 ∈ {2,3}`
     (reserved / illegal branch).

2. EX stage (rtl/ex_stage.sv):
   - Re-derive the IF-side prediction from the same (pc, instr, imm)
     bits already in id_ex_t. Call it `predicted_taken`:
       predicted_taken = in.ctrl.is_branch
                      && (in.imm[31] == 1'b1)
                      && (branch_target[1:0] == 2'b00)
                      && (in.ctrl.branch_op != 3'd2)   // guarded
                      && (in.ctrl.branch_op != 3'd3);
     This is pure combinational of bits already present in EX's input,
     exactly mirroring IF's decision — no new pipeline payload.
   - Redirect becomes:
       redirect = (branch_actual != branch_predicted_nextpc)
               || in.ctrl.is_jalr;
       redirect_target = branch_taken ? branch_target : (pc + 4);
     where `branch_actual` = resolved next PC and
     `branch_predicted_nextpc` = predicted_taken ? branch_target : pc+4.
     In other words: redirect only when IF's prediction disagrees with
     EX's resolution. Matches today's JAL behavior (no redirect on
     correctly-predicted JAL).
   - The misalign-trap path is unchanged; misaligned branch targets
     already trap in EX, and IF's predictor guard ensures misaligned
     backward branches fall through IF's normal pc+4 path so EX's
     misalign trap fires exactly as today.

3. pc_next bookkeeping for RVFI (learned from r2s1 failure):
   - The retiring branch's rvfi_pc_wdata MUST be the architecturally
     resolved next PC (branch_target on taken, pc+4 on not-taken),
     regardless of whether IF predicted. This is already what the
     current EX-stage reg_q.pc_next mux computes — leave it alone.
     Only the redirect *gate* is narrowed, not the pc_next
     computation. This was the exact bug that killed r2s1: gating
     pc_next's target path on `!predicted` instead of on
     `branch_taken` desynced rvfi_pc_wdata from rvfi_pc_rdata of the
     next retirement.

No state lives in the RVFI-observed pipeline payload. No new
recovery FSM. SBY re-derives the predictor's decision from the same
bits it already proves over. The additional constraint on the
formal state space is zero.

changes.description: Add BTFNT predictor alongside the existing JAL predictor: decode BRANCH opcode 0x63, check backward immediate (imm[31]==1), check target alignment, check branch_op != {2,3}; when all hold and no flush/redirect, set next_pc to branch_target instead of pc+4. Priority: redirect > JAL > BTFNT > pc+4.

Re-derive IF's prediction combinationally (predicted_taken = is_branch && imm[31] && aligned && !reserved_funct3); narrow redirect to fire only on mismatch between predicted next-PC and resolved next-PC (branch_taken ? target : pc+4). Keep reg_q.pc_next unchanged — it still reports the architectural next PC for RVFI. Misalign-trap path unchanged.

New cocotb suite exercising BTFNT correctness: (a) backward-taken branch hits zero-bubble path; (b) backward-not-taken branch triggers EX redirect to pc+4; (c) forward-taken branch still costs 2 cycles (no prediction); (d) misaligned backward branch target traps exactly as today; (e) reserved branch_op (funct3=2/3) is never predicted; (f) rvfi_pc_wdata matches resolved next-PC on every retirement.

Re-derive IF's prediction combinationally (predicted_taken = is_branch && imm[31] && aligned && !reserved_funct3); narrow redirect to fire only on mismatch between predicted next-PC and resolved next-PC (branch_taken ? target : pc+4). Keep reg_q.pc_next unchanged — it still reports the architectural next PC for RVFI. Misalign-trap path unchanged.

New cocotb suite exercising BTFNT correctness: (a) backward-taken branch hits zero-bubble path; (b) backward-not-taken branch triggers EX redirect to pc+4; (c) forward-taken branch still costs 2 cycles (no prediction); (d) misaligned backward branch target traps exactly as today; (e) reserved branch_op (funct3=2/3) is never predicted; (f) rvfi_pc_wdata matches resolved next-PC on every retirement.

title: Stateless IF-stage BTFN conditional-branch predictor (reconstruct at EX)
motivation:
The previous round's JAL predictor (hyp-20260502-003-r2s2, +5.87%)
confirmed the "provably correct by construction" pattern: decode the
instruction word at IF, redirect PC the same cycle, reconstruct the
prediction decision at EX from the same instruction bits, and narrow
the EX-stage redirect signal to mispredicts. Zero new pipeline
payload bits, zero new formal state — SBY proof time unchanged.

JAL is now zero-bubble, but conditional branches (opcode 0x63) still
pay the full 2-cycle redirect penalty on every taken branch. In
CoreMark the inner hot loops (crcu8 loop, core_list_find reverse
walk, core_bench_matrix triple-nested i/j/k, core_state_transition's
big switch) are dominated by backward loop-closing conditional
branches that are taken ~90% of the time. Forward branches in
if/else-error paths are taken <20% of the time. Static BTFN
(Backward-Taken-Forward-Not-taken) captures both biases perfectly
and needs zero bits of history — the prediction is a pure function
of the B-immediate's sign bit (instr[31]).

Prior attempts and their lessons inform this design:
  - r1s2 (BTB): blew formal budget by threading `predicted`+`pred_target`
    through if_id_t/id_ex_t. AVOID payload fields.
  - r2s1 (JAL early-redirect with `early_jumped` flag): broke
    pc_fwd because gating EX `redirect` silenced the pc_wdata tap.
    AVOID: keep pc_next computation independent of the redirect
    assertion gate.
  - r2s2 (stateless JAL predictor): succeeded because IF's decision
    is a pure function of instruction bits, and EX reconstructs the
    exact same decision to decide whether to redirect. ADOPT this
    pattern verbatim, extended to branches.

With the JAL predictor already deployed, conditional branches are
the next-biggest source of control-flow bubbles. Estimated
density in CoreMark: ~12-15% of retired instructions are
conditional branches; ~55-65% are taken; ~75-85% of taken branches
are backward. BTFN nets ~80-90% accuracy overall. At 2 cycles saved
per correct prediction and ~10% of cycles going to branch bubbles
today, a +6 to +10% CoreMark IPC lift is realistic.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a stateless static BTFN conditional-branch predictor to
if_stage.sv, mirrored by EX-stage reconstruction that narrows the
redirect signal to actual mispredicts only. No new pipeline payload
bits, no new registers, no history tables.

IF-STAGE CHANGES (rtl/if_stage.sv):
  1. Decode opcode 0x63 (BRANCH) from imem_data combinationally.
  2. Extract the B-type immediate:
       b_imm = {{19{imem_data[31]}}, imem_data[31], imem_data[7],
                imem_data[30:25], imem_data[11:8], 1'b0}
  3. Compute br_target = pc + b_imm.
  4. Predict taken iff all of:
       - opcode == 7'b1100011 (BRANCH)
       - imem_data[31] == 1     (negative immediate = backward)
       - br_target[1:0] == 2'b00 (aligned; same guard as JAL
         predictor to preserve misalign-trap semantics)
       - funct3 is a valid branch (0,1,4,5,6,7 — exclude 2,3 which
         are reserved/illegal and must retire with rvfi_trap)
       - !flush && !redirect (same fetch-validity gate used for
         JAL)
  5. next_pc priority: redirect (EX) > jal_target > br_target >
     pc+4. Update the existing next_pc mux.
  6. The branch instruction itself is still emitted to ID normally
     (no predicted flag, no NOP substitution) — it decodes and
     flows through EX just like any other branch.

EX-STAGE CHANGES (rtl/ex_stage.sv):
  1. Reconstruct IF's prediction decision from the same instruction
     bits that are already carried through id_ex_t:
       is_branch_opcode   = (in.instr[6:0] == 7'b1100011)
       funct3_valid_br    = (in.instr[14:12] != 3'd2 &&
                            in.instr[14:12] != 3'd3)
       target_aligned     = (branch_target[1:0] == 2'b00)
       if_predicted_taken = in.ctrl.is_branch
                            && is_branch_opcode
                            && in.instr[31]
                            && funct3_valid_br
                            && target_aligned
  2. Detect mispredict:
       branch_mispredict = in.ctrl.is_branch
                           && (if_predicted_taken != branch_taken)
  3. Narrow the redirect signal:
       redirect = !misalign_fault
                  && (in.ctrl.is_jalr || branch_mispredict)
     (JAL is already excluded by r2s2; we keep that narrowing.)
  4. Broaden the redirect_target mux to cover the "predicted taken
     but actually not taken" case, which must redirect back to pc+4:
       redirect_target = in.ctrl.is_jalr ? jump_target
                       : branch_taken    ? branch_target
                                         : (in.pc + 32'd4)
  5. reg_q.pc_next is UNCHANGED. It already reads
     `branch_taken ? branch_target : pc+4`, independent of the
     redirect assertion — so RVFI's pc_wdata tap remains
     authoritative regardless of whether EX physically redirects.
     This is the fix the r2s1 lesson called out: keep pc_next
     decoupled from the redirect gate.

WHY NO PAYLOAD BITS / NO FORMAL BLOWUP:
  - IF's prediction is a pure combinational function of imem_data,
    pc, flush, redirect. It writes nothing new into the IF/ID
    bundle.
  - EX reconstructs the prediction from in.instr (already in
    id_ex_t for RVFI), in.ctrl.is_branch, and branch_target
    (computed from in.pc + in.imm, also already present).
  - SBY sees exactly the same id_ex_t / ex_mem_t / mem_wb_t as
    baseline. The new logic is pure combinational at IF and EX.
    BMC depth and state count are unchanged.

WHY THE PREDICTION IS SELF-CONSISTENT:
  - If IF predicted taken for this branch (by the 5 conditions
    above), the SAME branch reaches EX with the SAME instr bits
    and the SAME branch_target (imm is derived from the same
    instr), so EX's reconstruction produces if_predicted_taken=1.
  - If flush=1 or redirect=1 when this branch was at IF, IF
    emitted NOP (0x00000013) instead — ctrl.is_branch=0 at EX,
    reconstruction is a no-op.
  - If imem_stall at fetch, flush_if is asserted upstream — same
    NOP outcome.
  - The funct3_valid_br guard mirrors decoder.sv's branch-arm
    acceptance: only funct3 ∈ {0,1,4,5,6,7} are legal branches.
    Illegal-funct3 branches (funct3=2,3) don't have ctrl.is_branch
    set, so reconstruction gracefully sees predicted=0.

RVFI FIELD IMPACT:
  - rvfi_pc_wdata: still driven by reg_q.pc_next, unchanged.
  - rvfi_trap: misalign_fault path untouched; IF's alignment guard
    matches EX's misalign_branch guard, so a misaligned taken
    branch is NEVER predicted and traps normally in EX.
  - rvfi_pc_rdata of the next retirement: now physically equals
    the predicted-target for correctly-predicted backward
    branches, matching the prior pc_wdata. pc_fwd holds.
  - All other RVFI fields unchanged (no decode, ALU, mem, or
    regfile changes).

EXPECTED FITNESS:
  - IPC lift: ~6-10% from eliminating taken-branch bubbles across
    CoreMark's hot loops. Static BTFN on RV32 compiled code
    typically achieves 80-88% accuracy.
  - Fmax: one extra 32-bit adder in IF (parallel with pc+4 and
    jal_target adders) + one 4-way mux. Fmax ceiling is currently
    set by EX's ALU/multiplier cone, not IF. Expect ±1% Fmax
    drift.
  - LUT4 / FF: +30 to +80 LUTs for IF adder and EX reconstruction,
    no new FFs. Well within the 10000 LUT budget.
  - Net fitness: +5 to +8%, confidence medium.

FALLBACK IF EX RECONSTRUCTION CRITICAL PATH HURTS FMAX:
  The reconstruction logic is ~5 LUT levels at worst
  (instr[6:0]==0x63, instr[14:12] decode, branch_target[1:0]
  check, AND-tree, XOR-with-branch_taken, OR into redirect).
  It sits in parallel with the existing ALU-result cone and the
  branch-comparator, which are both ~8-12 levels — reconstruction
  should be well off the critical path. If Fmax regresses
  unexpectedly, moving the `is_branch_opcode` and
  `funct3_valid_br` evaluation into id_stage.sv and passing them
  as a single `if_predicted_taken_mask` bit (1 bit, still no
  payload widening of semantic fields) is the targeted escape
  hatch.

changes.description: Add stateless BTFN conditional-branch predictor. Decode opcode 0x63, extract B-imm, compute br_target = pc + b_imm, predict taken iff imm[31]=1 AND target[1:0]=0 AND funct3 is legal branch AND !flush AND !redirect. Extend next_pc priority mux: redirect > jal_target > br_target > pc+4. No new state, no new output fields on if_id_t.

Reconstruct IF's prediction decision from in.instr bits (opcode==0x63, instr[31], funct3 validity, branch_target alignment). Detect branch_mispredict = is_branch && (if_predicted_taken != branch_taken). Narrow redirect to !misalign_fault && (is_jalr || branch_mispredict). Broaden redirect_target mux to route to pc+4 when actually-not-taken (after predicted-taken). Keep reg_q.pc_next = branch_taken ? branch_target : pc+4 unchanged so RVFI pc_wdata tap stays authoritative regardless of redirect gate (per the r2s1 lesson).

Add regressions: (1) backward BEQ that is taken retires with zero bubble cycles between branch retirement and target retirement (rvfi_order deltas); (2) forward BEQ that is taken still costs 2 cycles (mispredict); (3) backward BEQ with taken=false must redirect back to pc+4 (mispredict-back) and the next retirement has correct pc_rdata=pc+4; (4) BEQ with misaligned backward target (imm with bit[1]=1) is NOT predicted (IF alignment guard) and traps normally via existing misalign_fault path; (5) branch with funct3=2 (reserved/illegal) is NOT predicted and retires with rvfi_trap=1; (6) back-to-back taken backward branches (tight loop body) retire every cycle after the first; (7) branch immediately after JAL: JAL predicts correctly, then the branch at the JAL target predicts according to BTFN — verify both rvfi_order monotonicity and correct pc_wdata on each retirement.

Add stateless BTFN conditional-branch predictor. Decode opcode 0x63, extract B-imm, compute br_target = pc + b_imm, predict taken iff imm[31]=1 AND target[1:0]=0 AND funct3 is legal branch AND !flush AND !redirect. Extend next_pc priority mux: redirect > jal_target > br_target > pc+4. No new state, no new output fields on if_id_t.

Reconstruct IF's prediction decision from in.instr bits (opcode==0x63, instr[31], funct3 validity, branch_target alignment). Detect branch_mispredict = is_branch && (if_predicted_taken != branch_taken). Narrow redirect to !misalign_fault && (is_jalr || branch_mispredict). Broaden redirect_target mux to route to pc+4 when actually-not-taken (after predicted-taken). Keep reg_q.pc_next = branch_taken ? branch_target : pc+4 unchanged so RVFI pc_wdata tap stays authoritative regardless of redirect gate (per the r2s1 lesson).

Add regressions: (1) backward BEQ that is taken retires with zero bubble cycles between branch retirement and target retirement (rvfi_order deltas); (2) forward BEQ that is taken still costs 2 cycles (mispredict); (3) backward BEQ with taken=false must redirect back to pc+4 (mispredict-back) and the next retirement has correct pc_rdata=pc+4; (4) BEQ with misaligned backward target (imm with bit[1]=1) is NOT predicted (IF alignment guard) and traps normally via existing misalign_fault path; (5) branch with funct3=2 (reserved/illegal) is NOT predicted and retires with rvfi_trap=1; (6) back-to-back taken backward branches (tight loop body) retire every cycle after the first; (7) branch immediately after JAL: JAL predicts correctly, then the branch at the JAL target predicts according to BTFN — verify both rvfi_order monotonicity and correct pc_wdata on each retirement.

lesson:
- 2026-05-02 hyp-20260502-003-r3s2 (broken, n/a): Second BTFN attempt — this time applying the r2s1 "keep pc_next decoupled from the redirect gate" fix plus the r2s2 stateless-reconstruction pattern verbatim — still regressed formal (reg_ch0 PREUNSAT after 75s + insn_*_ch1 rc=16 cascade), confirming that the failure mode is NOT the pc_wdata tap but the act of making EX `redirect` correlate with `branch_taken` via `branch_mispredict = is_branch && (if_predicted_taken != branch_taken)`; under symbolic fetch the IF predictor can "redirect" onto an address whose imem_data is also free, so SBY's induction for the `reg` check cannot close the loop that reaches arbitrary regfile states through backward-taken-branch sequences — JAL sidestepped this because its prediction/resolution equality is tautological (unconditional), whereas conditional-branch static prediction fundamentally couples two free variables (branch direction + fetched opcode), so future branch-prediction hypotheses should either target the EX-stage bubble reduction path (non-speculative, keeps IF linear) or accept that a formal wrapper-level assume relating imem_data at the predicted target to the branch's resolved direction is required before BTFN-class predictors can prove out.
- 2026-05-02 hyp-20260502-003-r3s2 (broken, n/a): A second BTFN attempt — this time applying the r2s1 "keep pc_next decoupled from redirect gate" fix and the r2s2 stateless-reconstruction pattern verbatim — still regressed formal the same way r3s1 did (reg_ch0 PREUNSAT + cascade of insn_*_ch1 ERROR), proving the failure mode is NOT the pc_wdata tap: the real hazard is that narrowing EX redirect to  makes  correlate with , and SBY'\''s induction over the  property needs to reach arbitrary regfile states via arbitrary branch sequences — when the IF predictor redirects backward taken branches the same cycle they'\''re fetched, the ch0 retirement pattern required to reach some regfile states becomes vacuously unreachable under BMC's symbolic fetch stream (imem_data is free, so IF may "predict" onto an address whose imem_data is also free, creating a branch-prediction feedback loop the solver can'\''t close); the JAL predictor escaped this because JAL is always taken (the prediction/resolution equality is tautological) — conditional-branch static prediction needs either (a) a non-speculative microarchitectural home (e.g. delayed-redirect at EX keeping the baseline bubble but cutting its duration via earlier resolution), or (b) explicit SBY help (an assume over the predicted-taken backward-branch target's imem_data matching the resolved branch'\''s imm) rather than relying on structural reconstruction alone.

title: Unified EX-stage subtractor: share ALU SUB/SLT/SLTU and branch comparator
motivation:
The EX stage currently contains two parallel comparator networks that
consume the same post-forward rs1/rs2 operands:

  1. ALU ops SUB / SLT / SLTU — each compiled separately via `a - b`,
     `$signed(a) < $signed(b)`, and `a < b` in alu.sv's case arms.
  2. Branch resolve in ex_stage.sv — six funct3-indexed compares
     (BEQ/BNE/BLT/BGE/BLTU/BGEU), structurally independent of the
     ALU.

That is two full-width adder/subtractor trees plus two equality trees
competing for LUT4s and fanout on the forward-mux outputs. Yosys CSE
does fold some of this, but the compares live in two different
`always_comb` blocks in two different modules with different output
polarities (signed/unsigned/equal/not-equal) so the sharing is far
from guaranteed on GW2A.

The r3s0 win (+8.50%) proved that on Tang Nano 20K the EX path is
area/routing-bound rather than timing-bound: explicitly collapsing
three redundant DSP multipliers into one paid off even though each
sub-expression was already single-cycle. The same structural
consolidation opportunity exists here for the 32-bit sub/compare
logic — a fifth of EX-stage LUTs in a typical textbook 5-stage go
to these parallel comparators.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Introduce a single shared 33-bit signed subtract at the front of the
EX stage:

    logic [32:0] sub_a_ext = {rs1[31], rs1};   // sign-extend for signed
    logic [32:0] sub_b_ext = {rs2[31], rs2};
    logic [32:0] sub_s33   = $signed(sub_a_ext) - $signed(sub_b_ext);
    logic [33:0] sub_u34   = {1'b0, rs1} - {1'b0, rs2};   // borrow bit

From this one subtractor derive the shared flags:

    lt_signed   = sub_s33[32];     // MSB of signed 33-bit difference
    lt_unsigned = sub_u34[32];     // borrow-out of unsigned subtract
    is_zero     = (sub_s33[31:0] == 32'b0);

Drive the branch comparator in ex_stage.sv from {is_zero, lt_signed,
lt_unsigned} via a 6-way funct3 mux — identical semantics to the
current `$signed(rs1) < $signed(rs2)` etc. arms, but sourced from
one subtractor instead of six.

In alu.sv, reshape SUB/SLT/SLTU to consume the same a/b inputs via
an equivalent single-subtract structure:

    ALU_SUB:  out = sub_s33[31:0];
    ALU_SLT:  out = {31'b0, sub_s33[32]};
    ALU_SLTU: out = {31'b0, sub_u34[32]};

Because the ALU's a/b differ from ex_stage's rs1/rs2 for I-type
SLTI/SLTIU (alu_src selects immediate), keep the ALU's copy operating
on alu_a/alu_b, and the ex_stage branch compare on rs1/rs2. The two
subtractors have IDENTICAL structure but distinct operand sources, so
Yosys will emit two adder chains not one — but each replaces
three/six separate compare paths, for a net reduction of about 4
comparator trees worth of LUT4s in the EX stage.

For BRANCH instructions specifically (ctrl.alu_src=0, ctrl.is_auipc=0),
alu_a==rs1 and alu_b==rs2, so the ALU-internal subtractor and the
ex_stage branch subtractor are bit-identical on that cycle. Yosys's
resource-sharing pass (share -aggressive) should fold these two into
one physical subtractor when it observes they share the same inputs
under mutually-exclusive enable signatures, which is common-case for
in-order pipelines.

Correctness: RV32I semantics unchanged — signed LT from the 33-bit
sign-extended subtract's MSB is textbook, unsigned LT from borrow-out
is textbook, equality from zero-check on the 32-bit difference is
textbook. `a - b` for SUB is the low 32 bits of sub_s33. No change to
RVFI taps, pipeline payload, or stage depth. Decoder and regfile
unchanged. The ALTOPS M-ext branch in alu.sv and all M-ext paths
remain untouched (MUL/DIV remain on their existing hardware).

Expected fitness path: ~4-5% LUT4 reduction in ex_stage + alu
combined, freeing nextpnr routing slack. Same-day P&R seeds typically
convert 3-4% LUT reduction into ~1-3% Fmax on Tang Nano 20K's
congested-floorplan regime, which directly multiplies into fitness
since IPC is unchanged.
