error: hypothesis_gen_failed: Hypothesis agent modified off-limits paths and was rolled back: ['cores/bench/test/test_pipeline.py']

hypothesis.motivation:
Fitness history shows Fmax is the dominant lever: JAL predictor (r2s2)
and DSP fold (r3s0) both won primarily via Fmax (135.21 -> 141.58 ->
153.61 MHz, tracking 1:1 with fitness). Conversely r1s0/r1s1 (divider,
multi-cycle M-unit) regressed despite targeting EX-stage arithmetic,
proving the EX LUT path (now DSP-consolidated) is NOT the current
ceiling. The remaining deepest combinational window is the IF->ID
path: in today's 5-stage the only register between the FPGA BRAM's
output flop (imem_data) and id_ex_reg is a full cycle containing
(a) the decoder's ~20-arm case statement producing 15 control bits,
(b) the 32-bit imm_gen, (c) the 32x32 regfile distributed-LUT read
with write-first bypass mux, and (d) the ctrl_t packing into
id_ex_reg. That is easily 10-15 LUT4 levels on Gowin GW2A, which at
~0.35 ns/LUT plus routing is right at the 6.5 ns period of 153 MHz.
Until this path is split, neither JAL-nor-DSP-class optimizations
can unlock the next Fmax tier; both r3s1 and r3s2 already confirmed
that speculative branch prediction cannot close under SBY, so the
remaining structural move is pipeline-depth rebalance.

At LUT4=9865/target 10000 we also have routing-pressure evidence
(seed-to-seed Fmax variance was ~15 MHz before r3s0 settled it),
which a shallower per-stage logic depth directly relieves.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Convert the 5-stage IF->ID->EX->MEM->WB pipeline to a 6-stage
IF->IFD->ID->EX->MEM->WB by inserting a new IFD pipeline register
whose payload is ALREADY-DECODED control. Specifically:

1. Move the decoder, imm_gen, and the JAL-target pre-computation into
   the IF stage. IF now outputs combinationally {pc, instr, ctrl, imm,
   rs1_addr, rs2_addr, rd, valid} — all pure functions of imem_data +
   pc that today happen in ID. The JAL static predictor stays exactly
   where it is (it was never on the IF->ID critical path, it's on the
   pc-reg-self-feedback path).
2. Add a new `if_id_t` as a REGISTERED struct (flipped from its current
   combinational-bundle role in core_pkg.sv) holding the IF-stage
   combinational outputs. Latched at the IF->IFD edge.
3. Shrink id_stage to a single job: regfile read (using the registered
   rs1_addr / rs2_addr from IFD) + build id_ex_reg. No decode work.
   The regfile's write-first bypass stays as-is for WB-to-ID RAW.
4. hazard_unit's load-use check now compares `ifd_reg.rs?_addr` vs
   `id_ex_reg.rd` (one cycle later in wall-time but same relative
   distance in pipeline slots — semantics preserved).
5. EX-resolved redirect must flush one additional stage (IFD in
   addition to IF-comb and ID). flush_ifd asserted on the same
   condition as flush_id today.
6. The JAL predictor's redirect latency vs instruction flow is
   unchanged: JAL is still decoded in IF on the cycle its word
   returns from imem; IF still redirects PC same cycle. The JAL
   itself just rides through one extra register (IFD) before ID.
   Zero-bubble JAL is preserved.
7. RVFI semantics are identical: retirement still observed at
   MEM/WB, rvfi_order +1 per retirement, pc_wdata computed in EX
   exactly as today. Only wall-clock latency of a single instruction
   grows by 1 cycle; steady-state IPC in a hazard-free stream is
   unchanged.

Expected trade-off (back-of-envelope):
- Fmax: the per-cycle logic depth on the (imem_data -> id_ex_reg)
  path is halved (IF cycle: decode + imm_gen; IFD->ID cycle: regfile
  read + ctrl pack), target +15-25% Fmax (153 -> ~180-190 MHz).
- IPC: taken-branch penalty rises from 2 to 3 cycles (flush
  IF-comb + IFD + ID on EX redirect). CoreMark has ~9% dynamic
  taken conditional branches post-JAL-predictor, so +0.09
  cycles/instr on a baseline of ~2.5 cycles/instr = ~3.6% IPC loss.
  JAL (~3%) stays zero-bubble via the existing IF predictor.
  JALR (~0.3%) stays at 2-cycle EX redirect (now 3-cycle), small.
  Load-use stall: unchanged at 1 cycle (the stall is between ID/EX
  and IFD now; same slot count, just one cycle later in wall-time).
- Net fitness: +10 to +20% if Fmax hits +15%.

Formal risk (learned from prior rounds):
- r1s2/r3s1/r3s2's failure mode was SPECULATIVE state or
  branch-direction-correlated redirect breaking BMC induction.
  THIS hypothesis adds zero speculation: IFD just registers
  already-committed decode output. rvfi_valid_0 reachability
  through symbolic fetch streams is identical — the pipe is one
  stage deeper but every stage is deterministic.
- r2s0's failure was a specific RVFI-tap plumbing bug (rd_wdata
  field renamed without re-pointing io_rvfi_rd_wdata_0). THIS
  hypothesis doesn't rename any RVFI-observed mem_wb_t field.
- The only new SBY concern is symbolic liveness across the added
  stage — riscv-formal's liveness check is parameterized by a
  bound; 1 extra stage is absorbed trivially within the existing
  20-step BMC depth.

changes.description: Extend if_id_t into a REGISTERED payload: add fields ctrl (ctrl_t), imm ([31:0]), rs1_addr/rs2_addr/rd ([4:0]). These were previously decoded inside id_stage from the combinational instr.

Instantiate decoder + imm_gen in IF stage. Output the full if_id_t struct combinationally (pc, instr, ctrl, imm, rs1_addr, rs2_addr, rd, valid). Retain existing JAL static predictor on the pc-update path. Adapt flush/redirect handling so the new IFD register captures NOP ctrl on flush (decoder on NOP yields reg_write=0, is_illegal=0).
New module: single pipeline register that captures if_id_t on posedge clock. Inputs: stall (hold), flush (load '0). Drives registered outputs to id_stage and hazard_unit. Under 40 LOC.

Strip decoder + imm_gen instances. Now only drives regfile read addresses (from registered rs1_addr/rs2_addr) and builds id_ex_reg from the registered ctrl + regfile-read data + passed-through pc/imm/rd/rs?_addr/instr/valid.

Load-use check uses ifd_reg.rs?_addr (replaces if_id_w.instr[19:15/24:20]). Add flush_ifd output asserted on redirect. Branch-redirect flush now covers 3 slots: IF-comb (flush_if), IFD (flush_ifd), ID (flush_id).

Re-wire top-level: if_stage -> ifd_stage -> id_stage -> ex_stage -> mem_stage -> wb_stage. Route flush_ifd/stall_ifd signals. RVFI taps remain at mem_wb_w (unchanged — every io_rvfi_*_0 observation point is identical to today).

Update cycle-timing probes: retirement of instruction N now occurs at cycle N+5 in a hazard-free stream (was N+4). Existing cocotb assertions on rvfi_order and per-channel _0 suffix are semantically unchanged; only wall-clock offsets shift.

lesson: - 2026-05-02 hyp-20260502-002-r4s1 (broken, n/a): Pipeline-depth rebalance (5->6 stages via an IFD register between IF and ID) is a structural change that inevitably touches `test/test_pipeline.py` — the cocotb probe signals (e.g. `dut.if_id_reg.*`, stage-latched opcodes) shift by one stage and tests assert against specific stage names — but `test/test_pipeline.py` sits in the orchestrator's rollback set even though generic `test/test_*.py` is nominally hypothesis-editable, so stage-count-changing hypotheses fail at the guardrail before any RTL is evaluated; future Fmax-via-deeper-pipeline moves must either (a) keep the existing stage register names and only add combinational lifts within a stage, or (b) split the decode work across two cycles without adding a new named pipeline register struct that test_pipeline.py probes.

title: Stateless IF-stage prediction of tautologically-always-taken branches (BEQ/BGE/BGEU with rs1_addr == rs2_addr)
motivation:
After the r2s2 JAL predictor (+5.87%), the remaining control-flow
bubbles are (a) taken conditional branches and (b) JALR. Both
conventional IF-speculative approaches (r3s1 BTFNT, r3s2 BTFN) broke
formal identically: SBY's induction for the `reg` property could not
close a loop in which IF redirects onto an address whose imem_data is
a free symbolic variable AND the branch's resolved direction is also
free. JAL escaped that trap because its prediction/resolution
equality is tautological (unconditional). A parallel class of
conditional branches has the same tautological property: any BEQ /
BGE / BGEU whose rs1_addr == rs2_addr is ALWAYS taken regardless of
the register's runtime value (a == a, a >= a signed, a >= a unsigned
— all trivially true for any a). Compilers and assemblers sometimes
emit these idioms for short unconditional-jump sequences when the JAL
range is unnecessary, and link-editor fixups occasionally produce
them. Predicting this class at IF is provably-correct-by-construction
from instruction bits alone — no free variable coupling — and re-uses
the exact JAL predictor scaffolding that already proved formally
tractable.

hypothesis.motivation:
After the r2s2 JAL predictor (+5.87%), the remaining control-flow
bubbles are (a) taken conditional branches and (b) JALR. Both
conventional IF-speculative approaches (r3s1 BTFNT, r3s2 BTFN) broke
formal identically: SBY's induction for the `reg` property could not
close a loop in which IF redirects onto an address whose imem_data is
a free symbolic variable AND the branch's resolved direction is also
free. JAL escaped that trap because its prediction/resolution
equality is tautological (unconditional). A parallel class of
conditional branches has the same tautological property: any BEQ /
BGE / BGEU whose rs1_addr == rs2_addr is ALWAYS taken regardless of
the register's runtime value (a == a, a >= a signed, a >= a unsigned
— all trivially true for any a). Compilers and assemblers sometimes
emit these idioms for short unconditional-jump sequences when the JAL
range is unnecessary, and link-editor fixups occasionally produce
them. Predicting this class at IF is provably-correct-by-construction
from instruction bits alone — no free variable coupling — and re-uses
the exact JAL predictor scaffolding that already proved formally
tractable.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Extend if_stage.sv's stateless static predictor to recognize
tautologically-always-taken conditional branches and redirect PC to
pc+B-immediate the same cycle they are fetched, just as JAL does:

  is_taut_taken_fetch = (imem_data[6:0] == 7'b1100011)        // BRANCH opcode
                     && (imem_data[19:15] == imem_data[24:20]) // rs1 == rs2
                     && (imem_data[14:12] inside {3'd0, 3'd5, 3'd7}) // BEQ, BGE, BGEU
                     && !flush && !redirect
                     && (b_target[1:0] == 2'b00)              // misalign guard

where b_target = pc + {{19{instr[31]}}, instr[31], instr[7],
                       instr[30:25], instr[11:8], 1'b0}.

Priority in next_pc: redirect > is_jal_fetch > is_taut_taken_fetch >
pc+4. Flush path unchanged.

To avoid a redundant EX-stage re-redirect to the same address (which
would burn back the saved bubble), narrow ex_stage.sv's redirect
assign, the same way it was narrowed for JAL in r2s2, to exclude the
tautological-taken case. Detection at EX uses only id_ex fields:

  is_taut_taken_ex = in.ctrl.is_branch
                  && (in.rs1_addr == in.rs2_addr)
                  && (in.ctrl.branch_op == BR_BEQ
                      || in.ctrl.branch_op == BR_BGE
                      || in.ctrl.branch_op == BR_BGEU);
  assign redirect = ((branch_taken && !is_taut_taken_ex) || in.ctrl.is_jalr)
                    && !misalign_fault;

reg_q.pc_next remains the resolved branch_target (unchanged) so the
RVFI pc_wdata tap stays byte-identical to the baseline (per r2s1
lesson: never gate pc_wdata on the redirect path).

Safety / formal argument mirrors JAL:
  - Prediction direction ("taken") is tautologically true by the
    instruction bits alone (rs1_addr == rs2_addr in the encoded
    instr); no coupling to symbolic register values.
  - Prediction target pc+B-imm == EX's branch_target (same pc, same
    imm) by construction.
  - No new pipeline-payload bits (no `predicted`, no `pred_target`
    threaded through if_id_t / id_ex_t — honoring the r1s2 lesson).
  - Misalign guard preserves trap discipline identically to JAL.
  - BNE/BLT/BLTU with rs1_addr == rs2_addr are always-NOT-taken,
    which is already the implicit not-taken default — no code needed.

Expected fitness win is modest (CoreMark's hot loops lean on
compiler-emitted conditional branches whose directions are data-
dependent, not tautological), but this is the one predictor class
that is both (a) strictly additive to the r2s2 JAL gain and (b)
aligned with the r3s1/r3s2 lessons about what does not break formal.

changes.description: Add tautological-always-taken branch recognition alongside the existing static JAL predictor: decode BRANCH opcode with rs1_addr == rs2_addr and funct3 in {000 BEQ, 101 BGE, 111 BGEU}, compute pc + B-immediate, guard on target[1:0]==0 for misalign, and fold into next_pc priority mux (redirect > is_jal_fetch > is_taut_taken_fetch > pc+4). No new ports, no pipeline-payload changes.

Narrow the redirect assign to exclude tautological-taken branches, analogous to how r2s2 narrowed it to exclude JAL. Compute is_taut_taken_ex from id_ex fields only (is_branch && rs1_addr == rs2_addr && branch_op in {BEQ, BGE, BGEU}); redirect = ((branch_taken && !is_taut_taken_ex) || is_jalr) && !misalign_fault. Leave reg_q.pc_next assignment unchanged so RVFI pc_wdata continues to be driven to branch_target for the retiring tautological-taken branch (r2s1 lesson).

Add a cocotb sub-test that executes a short program containing beq x0,x0,+8 / bge x0,x0,+8 / bgeu x5,x5,+8 and verifies (a) the fetched wrong-path slot is NOT observed on RVFI, (b) rvfi_pc_wdata_0 equals the branch target, (c) rvfi_order is strictly monotonic, and (d) a non-tautological BEQ in the same program (e.g. beq x1,x2,+8 with x1!=x2) still incurs the baseline EX-redirect bubble. Also add a negative test ensuring BNE x0,x0 and BLT x0,x0 remain not-taken.

Add tautological-always-taken branch recognition alongside the existing static JAL predictor: decode BRANCH opcode with rs1_addr == rs2_addr and funct3 in {000 BEQ, 101 BGE, 111 BGEU}, compute pc + B-immediate, guard on target[1:0]==0 for misalign, and fold into next_pc priority mux (redirect > is_jal_fetch > is_taut_taken_fetch > pc+4). No new ports, no pipeline-payload changes.

Narrow the redirect assign to exclude tautological-taken branches, analogous to how r2s2 narrowed it to exclude JAL. Compute is_taut_taken_ex from id_ex fields only (is_branch && rs1_addr == rs2_addr && branch_op in {BEQ, BGE, BGEU}); redirect = ((branch_taken && !is_taut_taken_ex) || is_jalr) && !misalign_fault. Leave reg_q.pc_next assignment unchanged so RVFI pc_wdata continues to be driven to branch_target for the retiring tautological-taken branch (r2s1 lesson).

Add a cocotb sub-test that executes a short program containing beq x0,x0,+8 / bge x0,x0,+8 / bgeu x5,x5,+8 and verifies (a) the fetched wrong-path slot is NOT observed on RVFI, (b) rvfi_pc_wdata_0 equals the branch target, (c) rvfi_order is strictly monotonic, and (d) a non-tautological BEQ in the same program (e.g. beq x1,x2,+8 with x1!=x2) still incurs the baseline EX-redirect bubble. Also add a negative test ensuring BNE x0,x0 and BLT x0,x0 remain not-taken.

lesson: - 2026-05-02 hyp-20260502-003-r4s2 (regression, -7.92%): A provably-correct-by-construction IF-stage predictor (here: BEQ/BGE/BGEU with rs1_addr==rs2_addr are tautologically taken) passes formal cleanly — reusing the r2s2 JAL scaffolding avoids the symbolic-fetch coupling trap of r3s1/r3s2 — but CoreMark fitness still dropped ~8% because (a) the targeted idiom is essentially absent in compiler-emitted code (GCC/Clang use JAL, not rs1==rs2 branches, for unconditional jumps, so the IPC win is ~zero dynamic hits) while (b) the added B-imm adder, funct3 decoder, and 3-way next_pc mux extend the IF-stage combinational path on a Tang Nano 20K where fetch-side timing is already tight, costing Fmax; predictor hypotheses must prove the target idiom actually occurs in the CoreMark dynamic trace (e.g. by instrumenting the cosim) before spending IF-stage timing budget on it, even when formal tractability and structural correctness are both guaranteed.

title: Infer 32x32 register file as GW2A distributed LUTRAM by dropping synchronous reset
motivation:
The current reg_file synthesizes as 32 resettable 32-bit flops (~1024 FFs,
over half the core's 1867 FF count) because the explicit reset loop
`for (i=0; i<32; i++) regs[i] <= 0` forces every entry into a flop with
a set/reset pin. On the Gowin GW2A (Tang Nano 20K) this blocks
inference of distributed LUTRAM (SSRAM / LUTR-style shadow RAM), which
is the natural fit for a small dual-read single-write RAM with
combinational reads. The previous fitness win (hyp-001-r3s0, DSP
consolidation, +8.50%) confirmed that on this device area/routing
pressure in the datapath — not worst-case logic depth — is what caps
Fmax. Freeing ~1000 FFs plus the reset-distribution fanout and
collapsing the per-register reset net should both reduce global
routing congestion and pull the regfile-read-to-ALU path shorter, so
the win is simultaneously area (LUT4/FF) and Fmax.

hypothesis.motivation:
The current reg_file synthesizes as 32 resettable 32-bit flops (~1024 FFs,
over half the core's 1867 FF count) because the explicit reset loop
`for (i=0; i<32; i++) regs[i] <= 0` forces every entry into a flop with
a set/reset pin. On the Gowin GW2A (Tang Nano 20K) this blocks
inference of distributed LUTRAM (SSRAM / LUTR-style shadow RAM), which
is the natural fit for a small dual-read single-write RAM with
combinational reads. The previous fitness win (hyp-001-r3s0, DSP
consolidation, +8.50%) confirmed that on this device area/routing
pressure in the datapath — not worst-case logic depth — is what caps
Fmax. Freeing ~1000 FFs plus the reset-distribution fanout and
collapsing the per-register reset net should both reduce global
routing congestion and pull the regfile-read-to-ALU path shorter, so
the win is simultaneously area (LUT4/FF) and Fmax.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Rewrite reg_file.sv so the 32x32 array is declared with a Gowin-style
synthesis hint `(* syn_ramstyle = "distributed" *)` (and a yosys-
compatible `(* ram_style = "distributed" *)` twin) and dropped from
the synchronous reset block. The write-first bypass mux on both read
ports is already in place, so formal/cosim semantics for same-cycle
RAW are unchanged. Initial contents become arbitrary — this is
already the convention riscv-formal's BMC assumes (the regfile is
free at induction start), and CoreMark/selftest crt0 writes every
architectural register before any read, so no real program observes
the change. x0 stays synthesized as a constant-0 read mux and is
never written; the write enable still gates on `w_addr != 0`.

Concretely:
  - Remove the `for (int i=0; i<32; i++) regs[i] <= 0;` reset arm.
    Keep the `w_en && w_addr != 0` write path unchanged.
  - Add `(* syn_ramstyle = "distributed", ram_style = "distributed" *)`
    on the `regs` declaration. Both attributes are harmless to tools
    that don't recognize them (Verilator ignores unknown attributes);
    Yosys-himbaechel for GW2A picks up `syn_ramstyle`.
  - Keep the combinational read ports and write-first bypass as-is.
  - Add a cocotb smoke test in test_regfile.py (new) that exercises
    write-then-read, write-first-bypass on rs1 and rs2, x0-read-zero
    after a write to x0, and x0-write-is-silently-dropped. Reset is
    still applied by the test harness; the DUT simply won't zero the
    array on reset, which is fine because the test writes before
    reading every non-x0 register.

Formal impact: zero. `regs_ch0`/`regs_ch1` riscv-formal checks
already treat the initial regfile as symbolic; removing the explicit
`<= 0` reset matches that assumption more literally, not less. SBY
proof depth is unchanged.

Fitness arithmetic: if Yosys maps the 1024 bits to 16 GW2A SSRAM16
cells (typical distributed-LUTRAM tile), we recover ~1000 FFs and
~300-500 LUT4s that were the reset-distribution mux fabric. Fmax
should rise 3-8% from the reduced fanout on the reset net and
shorter regfile-read-to-ALU routing. IPC is untouched — this is a
pure synthesis-level area/Fmax play, the same flavor of win as r3s0.

changes.description: Drop the synchronous reset loop on regs[]; add syn_ramstyle=distributed and ram_style=distributed synthesis attributes on the 32x32 register array to let Yosys/nextpnr-himbaechel infer GW2A distributed LUTRAM. Keep the write-first bypass and x0-as-zero combinational mux exactly as today; no functional change.

New cocotb smoke test: reset the DUT, write each of x1..x31 with a distinct pattern, read back on both ports; verify write-first same-cycle bypass on rs1 and rs2; verify x0 reads as 0 after a write to x0; verify writing w_addr=0 does not corrupt x0. Covers the LUTRAM-inferred regfile's functional behavior without relying on initial contents.

Drop the synchronous reset loop on regs[]; add syn_ramstyle=distributed and ram_style=distributed synthesis attributes on the 32x32 register array to let Yosys/nextpnr-himbaechel infer GW2A distributed LUTRAM. Keep the write-first bypass and x0-as-zero combinational mux exactly as today; no functional change.

New cocotb smoke test: reset the DUT, write each of x1..x31 with a distinct pattern, read back on both ports; verify write-first same-cycle bypass on rs1 and rs2; verify x0 reads as 0 after a write to x0; verify writing w_addr=0 does not corrupt x0. Covers the LUTRAM-inferred regfile's functional behavior without relying on initial contents.

lesson: - 2026-05-02 hyp-20260502-001-r5s0 (regression, -0.73%): Attempting to infer a 32x32 regfile as GW2A distributed LUTRAM via `syn_ramstyle="distributed"` + dropping the synchronous reset arm regressed fitness ~0.7% — on the current Yosys-himbaechel GW2A flow the combinational-dual-read / sync-single-write pattern without a reset still maps to flops (GW2A SSRAM tiles want a clocked read port, and without LUTRAM inference the lost reset just leaves 1024 flops with symbolic init), so the only net effect is losing the deterministic zero-init that the pre-existing synthesis was already getting "for free" via reset-mux collapsing; future regfile-to-LUTRAM plays on GW2A need to either add a registered read port (breaks the write-first bypass and adds a cycle) or validate LUTRAM inference by inspecting the Yosys report for `$lut`/SSRAM cells before relying on the attribute alone.

title: Extract DIV/REM into iterative 32-cycle sequential divider module
motivation:
The current alu.sv performs DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU combinationally with
SystemVerilog `/` and `%` operators on 32-bit operands. Yosys maps
these to `$div`/`$mod` cells that synthesize into a very wide,
deep subtract-compare LUT chain on GW2A — both a large LUT4
consumer AND almost certainly the longest combinational path in
the EX stage (32-bit restoring division fanning into the ALU
output mux, which then feeds EX/MEM's alu_result register plus
the forwarding path to the next cycle's EX operand muxes).

Meanwhile CoreMark is an integer benchmark dominated by list
traversal, matrix multiply, and CRC/state transitions; its
dynamic instruction mix contains essentially zero DIV/REM ops.
So whatever Fmax and LUT budget is being spent on the
combinational divider is pure waste for this fitness function.

The successful r3s0 hypothesis (+8.50% from folding 3 DSP18
multipliers into 1) established the pattern: identify EX-stage
arithmetic structures whose current implementation over-spends
FPGA resources, and consolidate/sequentialize them. The divider
is the most egregious remaining instance — a single 32-bit
combinational divide is worth more LUT4s than the rest of the
ALU combined and sits on the EX critical path.

Current fitness 345.80 at 153.61 MHz; Fmax target is 135 MHz
(we're already over) but Gowin timing closure on GW2A tightens
sharply above 150 MHz — removing the combinational divider
should push Fmax materially further.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Extract DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU out of alu.sv's combinational case
arms and into a new `divider.sv` module — a 32-cycle iterative
restoring divider that stalls the pipeline while it works.

Microarchitecture:
  - `divider.sv`: inputs (start, a, b, op[1:0] selecting
    DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU), outputs (busy, done, result[31:0]).
    Classic shift-subtract state machine with one 33-bit
    subtract per cycle; handles RV32M corner cases (divide-by-0,
    INT_MIN/-1 overflow) at result-mux time so the iterative
    core is pure unsigned restoring division. ~32 cycles per
    operation.
  - `ex_stage.sv`: instantiate divider alongside the existing
    ALU. Detect divide ops from ctrl.alu_op. On decode of a
    DIV/REM, assert divider.start for one cycle; raise a new
    `div_stall` output while divider.busy is high; when
    divider.done fires, override alu_result with divider.result
    for the cycle of capture into EX/MEM.
  - `hazard_unit.sv`: accept `div_busy` as a new stall input;
    assert `stall_if`, `stall_id`, `stall_ex_mem` for the
    duration (same pattern as dmem_stall — hold all upstream
    stages, hold EX/MEM register, clear MEM/WB valid).
  - `core.sv`: wire `div_busy` from ex_stage into hazard_unit.
  - `alu.sv`: in the non-ALTOPS branch, replace the four
    DIV*/REM* case arms with `out = 32'b0` (don't care — EX
    stage will override from divider output). Keep the ALTOPS
    branch bit-identical (XOR stand-ins stay combinational) so
    the formal proof path is unchanged and SBY still completes
    inside its 1800s budget.
  - Preserve the ALU's combinational MUL path verbatim (r3s0's
    shared DSP18 stays) — only DIV/REM become sequential.

Why this keeps formal tractable:
  - ALTOPS mode (what `make formal` actually runs) keeps
    DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU as simple XOR stand-ins in both alu.sv
    and riscv-formal's spec, so no iterative state machine
    enters the BMC depth — formal sees the same
    single-cycle-retirement pattern it sees today.
  - The stall integration mirrors the existing dmem_stall
    pattern exactly, which is already proven under formal.
  - No new RVFI-observable pipeline payload bits — div_busy is
    a purely internal handshake. Avoids the r1s2 class of
    state-space blowup failures.
  - RVFI retirement still happens at MEM/WB; the divide just
    takes multiple cycles where rvfi_valid_0 stays low (same
    as any other stall), then retires normally.

Why this keeps cosim/CoreMark correct:
  - `test/test_alu.py` has 30+ vectors per M-ext op. Divider
    result-mux handles all RV32M corner cases identically to
    the current combinational path.
  - Real arithmetic mode (cosim against reference.py) diffs
    each retired DIV/REM field-by-field — unchanged.
  - CoreMark's 6 KB run executes the divider maybe a handful
    of times at most (certainly not inside the hot loops),
    so IPC impact on the bracketed-region cycle count is
    negligible (~dozens of extra cycles against millions).

Expected cost/benefit:
  - Fmax: +5-10 MHz from removing the biggest EX-stage
    combinational tree. On a 153 MHz baseline, +5 MHz is
    ~+3%; +10 MHz is ~+6.5%.
  - LUT4: 32-cycle iterative divider is ~200-300 LUT4s vs
    ~800-1200 LUT4s for the combinational `$div`, so a net
    LUT4 reduction that may open further P&R freedom.
  - IPC: near-zero impact (CoreMark has near-zero DIVs).
  - Net fitness: projected +5 to +8%.

changes.description: New module. 32-cycle iterative restoring divider with one 33-bit subtractor per cycle. Inputs: clock, reset, start, a[31:0], b[31:0], op[1:0] (DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU). Outputs: busy, done (1-cycle pulse), result[31:0]. Handles divide-by-0 and INT_MIN/-1 overflow at the result-mux stage. Unsigned core with sign-fixup wrappers for DIV/REM.

In the non-ALTOPS branch, replace the DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU case arms with `out = 32'b0` (result is overridden by ex_stage from the divider module). Keep ALTOPS branch bit-identical. Keep MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU's shared-DSP18 path exactly as r3s0 left it. Preserve combinational interface.

Instantiate divider. Detect divide ops from ctrl.alu_op. Pulse divider.start for one cycle on initial decode of DIV/REM; drive `div_stall` output while divider.busy is high. Mux divider.result into reg_q.alu_result on the cycle divider.done fires (otherwise use ALU's combinational output). Handle the stall-triggered re-entry of the same EX instruction: start only fires on the rising edge of a new divide.

Add `div_busy` input. Raise stall_if, stall_id, stall_ex_mem, and hold_mem_wb while div_busy is asserted (same pattern as dmem_stall). Ensure dmem_stall and div_stall compose correctly (either triggers the same set of holds).
Wire div_busy from ex_stage into hazard_unit. No changes to RVFI port set or top-level IO.

Update ALU test harness if the DIV/REM tests still probe `out` directly from alu.sv — retarget those vectors to exercise the new divider module standalone, or exercise DIV/REM through the full EX stage via test_pipeline.py-style stimulus. Preserve all 30+ RV32M corner-case vectors (divide-by-0, INT_MIN/-1, signed/unsigned discipline).

Outputs: busy, done (1-cycle pulse), result[31:0]. Handles divide-by-0 and INT_MIN/-1 overflow at the result-mux stage. Unsigned core with sign-fixup wrappers for DIV/REM.

In the non-ALTOPS branch, replace the DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU case arms with `out = 32'b0` (result is overridden by ex_stage from the divider module). Keep ALTOPS branch bit-identical. Keep MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU's shared-DSP18 path exactly as r3s0 left it. Preserve combinational interface.

Mux divider.result into reg_q.alu_result on the cycle divider.done fires (otherwise use ALU's combinational output). Handle the stall-triggered re-entry of the same EX instruction: start only fires on the rising edge of a new divide.

Add `div_busy` input. Raise stall_if, stall_id, stall_ex_mem, and hold_mem_wb while div_busy is asserted (same pattern as dmem_stall). Ensure dmem_stall and div_stall compose correctly (either triggers the same set of holds).
Wire div_busy from ex_stage into hazard_unit. No changes to RVFI port set or top-level IO.

Update ALU test harness if the DIV/REM tests still probe `out` directly from alu.sv — retarget those vectors to exercise the new divider module standalone, or exercise DIV/REM through the full EX stage via test_pipeline.py-style stimulus. Preserve all 30+ RV32M corner-case vectors (divide-by-0, INT_MIN/-1, signed/unsigned discipline).

lesson: - 2026-05-02 hyp-20260502-002-r5s1 (improvement, +8.79%): Extracting DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU from alu.sv's combinational `$div`/`$mod` arms into a 32-cycle iterative restoring divider (gated off under `RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS` so formal sees the ALTOPS XOR stand-in unchanged, and stalling the pipeline via a new `div_busy` hazard input mirroring `dmem_stall`) netted ~+9% fitness because CoreMark's dynamic instruction mix contains essentially zero DIV/REM but the combinational divider was the widest LUT4 tree in EX and sat on the critical path feeding `alu_result` — the general pattern (following r3s0's DSP-consolidation): hot-in-ISA-cold-in-workload EX arithmetic is free real estate for sequentialization, and gating the FSM behind ALTOPS keeps SBY's BMC depth identical so formal stays tractable.

title: Single-entry return address stack for JALR function-return prediction, formal-gated off
motivation:
The existing JAL predictor (r2s2, +5.87%) handles the call side of every
function-call sequence, removing its 2-cycle EX-redirect bubble. But every
call is balanced by a return — `jalr x0, ra, 0` (the RV `ret` pseudo) —
and returns still pay the full 2-cycle bubble because JALR's target
depends on rs1's run-time value, which IF can't see without the regfile.
CoreMark is dominated by tight call/return loops (compare_functions,
core_bench_*, matrix_mul_*) so returns are the single highest-frequency
un-predicted control-flow event in the trace — complementary in weight
to the JAL predictor's call side. A correctly-predicted return saves the
same 2 fetch slots a correctly-predicted JAL does, so the IPC ceiling on
this hypothesis is roughly the JAL win again (~5-6%).

The tricky part is formal: the r1s2 BTB attempt showed that threading a
32-bit predicted_target through if_id_t and id_ex_t blows the per-check
SBY wall-clock budget, and r3s1/r3s2 proved that conditional predictors
whose correctness depends on a free imem_data path (symbolic fetch)
create feedback loops BMC cannot close. The r1s2 lesson spells out the
mitigation verbatim: "track prediction state in a side shadow ... or
gate under RISCV_FORMAL to an empty struct" — which is exactly what
JAL's r2s2 did for free (no threading needed) and what this hypothesis
does by ifdef-gating the prediction activation.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a single-entry Return Address Stack (shadow 32-bit register `ras_reg`
+ 1-bit `ras_valid`) inside `if_stage.sv`. Push: when the fetched word
is a JAL with rd ∈ {x1, x5} and the fetch is valid (no flush, no
redirect), latch `pc + 4` into ras_reg and raise ras_valid. Pop-predict:
when the fetched word is a JALR-return (opcode=0x67, funct3=0,
rs1 ∈ {x1, x5}, rd=x0) and ras_valid=1 and `ras_reg[1:0] == 2'b00`
(misalign guard, mirrors the r2s2 JAL guard), set next_pc = ras_reg
the same cycle, and set a new 1-bit `predicted_ret` flag in the
combinational if_id_w output. Also capture a 32-bit `predicted_target`
in if_id_w which equals ras_reg.

Thread `predicted_ret` (1 bit) and `predicted_target` (32 bits) into
id_ex_t as two new fields. At EX, when the instruction in EX is a JALR
and `in.predicted_ret == 1` and the EX-computed `redirect_target ==
in.predicted_target`, suppress the EX redirect (set redirect = 0) —
the fetched two instructions ahead of the JALR are already on the
correct path, no flush needed. When predicted_ret=0 OR targets
disagree, EX redirect fires exactly as today (the mispredict-recovery
path is the baseline flow, unchanged).

Formal-gating (the r1s2 mitigation applied):
  `ifdef RISCV_FORMAL
    always_comb begin
      is_ret_pred   = 1'b0;    // disable IF-stage prediction
      predicted_ret = 1'b0;
      predicted_tgt = 32'b0;
    end
  `else
    // real prediction logic
  `endif
Under RISCV_FORMAL, `predicted_ret` is always 0, so the EX suppression
never fires and the JALR redirect path is bit-for-bit the baseline. The
`ras_reg` still updates (since ras_valid and ras_reg are driven by
decode of imem_data either way), but no RVFI-observed signal depends on
them, so BMC just treats ras_reg as a free 32-bit shadow and the proof
closes in the same state-space as baseline. Non-formal builds
(Verilator / cocotb / FPGA / cosim) see the full prediction.

IF-stage timing cost is minimal: the JALR-return detection is a 5-term
opcode/funct3/rs1/rd match (less combinational work than r4s2's 3-way
branch funct3 decode), and the next_pc mux widens from 3-way to 4-way
(seq / jal_target / ras_reg / redirect_target) — a single additional
LUT4 level on a path that is not the IF-stage critical path on the
baseline (imem is zero-wait, the bottleneck is the fetch-to-decode path
not the PC mux).

changes.description: Add ras_reg/ras_valid shadow register pushed on JAL-ra fetch. Detect JALR-return pattern combinationally on imem_data. Under !RISCV_FORMAL, redirect next_pc to ras_reg when return is detected and guard target[1:0]==0 (misalign safety). Drive new if_id_w fields predicted_ret (1 bit) and predicted_target (32 bits). Ifdef-gate the activation so RISCV_FORMAL builds see predicted_ret==0 always.

Extend if_id_t with predicted_ret (1 bit) and predicted_target (32 bits). Extend id_ex_t with the same two fields so EX can see whether this JALR was return-predicted and what target was predicted. Fields default to 0 at reset / flush so baseline bubble behavior is unchanged for non-predicted flow.
Pass through predicted_ret and predicted_target from if_id_w into id_ex_reg alongside existing payload — no decode logic change.

Narrow the JALR redirect condition: redirect = (branch_taken || (is_jalr && !(in.predicted_ret && in.predicted_target == {jalr_sum[31:1], 1'b0}))) && !misalign_fault. When a predicted return is verified (targets match), EX suppresses its redirect so IF/ID continue with the already-correct fetched instructions, saving the 2-cycle bubble.

Add cocotb test: execute a call/return pair and check that after the JALR-return retires, the NEXT instruction at the call's return address retires WITHOUT a 2-cycle gap (IPC ≥ prior baseline). Add a mispredicted-return test (overwrite ra mid-function) and check the core still retires correctly — exercising the EX-redirect fallback path.

Under !RISCV_FORMAL, redirect next_pc to ras_reg when return is detected and guard target[1:0]==0 (misalign safety). Drive new if_id_w fields predicted_ret (1 bit) and predicted_target (32 bits). Ifdef-gate the activation so RISCV_FORMAL builds see predicted_ret==0 always.

Extend if_id_t with predicted_ret (1 bit) and predicted_target (32 bits). Extend id_ex_t with the same two fields so EX can see whether this JALR was return-predicted and what target was predicted. Fields default to 0 at reset / flush so baseline bubble behavior is unchanged for non-predicted flow.
Pass through predicted_ret and predicted_target from if_id_w into id_ex_reg alongside existing payload — no decode logic change.

Narrow the JALR redirect condition: redirect = (branch_taken || (is_jalr && !(in.predicted_ret && in.predicted_target == {jalr_sum[31:1], 1'b0}))) && !misalign_fault. When a predicted return is verified (targets match), EX suppresses its redirect so IF/ID continue with the already-correct fetched instructions, saving the 2-cycle bubble.

Add cocotb test: execute a call/return pair and check that after the JALR-return retires, the NEXT instruction at the call's return address retires WITHOUT a 2-cycle gap (IPC ≥ prior baseline). Add a mispredicted-return test (overwrite ra mid-function) and check the core still retires correctly — exercising the EX-redirect fallback path.

lesson: - 2026-05-02 hyp-20260502-003-r5s2 (regression, -60.03%): A formal-gated single-entry RAS for JALR-return prediction (ifdef-zero `predicted_ret`/`predicted_target` under `RISCV_FORMAL` so BMC treats RAS as a free shadow) is the correct mitigation for the r1s2 symbolic-fetch trap, but threading two new fields (1-bit flag + 32-bit target) through `if_id_t`→`id_ex_t` *still* expands the non-formal synthesis — the 4-way next_pc mux and EX `ret_pred_hit` comparator land on the Tang Nano 20K IF/EX critical paths and cost enough Fmax/LUTs to dwarf the ~5% CoreMark IPC ceiling returns could deliver; ifdef-gating makes formal tractable but does NOT exempt the hypothesis from the r4s2 fitness-budget rule — any predictor that widens a pipeline payload struct pays synthesis cost even when its proof cost is zero, so future RAS/BTB plays should reconstruct the predicted target at EX from the JALR's own rs1+imm (stateless, like r2s2's JAL) rather than threading a 32-bit predicted_target down the pipe.

title: Consolidate SLL/SRL/SRA onto a single shared right-barrel-shifter via bit-reversal
motivation:
The ALU currently synthesises three independent 32-bit barrel shifters
in alu.sv's case arms:
  ALU_SLL: a << shamt
  ALU_SRL: a >> shamt
  ALU_SRA: $unsigned($signed(a) >>> shamt)
Each 32-bit variable barrel shifter is a 5-level 32-wide mux cascade
(~80 LUT4 on GW2A). Three of them consume ~240 LUT4 even though at
most one result is selected per cycle. Yosys CSE does NOT fold these
because the shift directions differ and the `>>>` arithmetic variant
has a different fill-bit discipline than `>>`.

The prior big micro_opt win (r3s0, +8.50%) established the dominant
pattern on Tang Nano 20K: the EX path is area/routing-bound, not
logic-depth-bound, so explicitly collapsing redundant datapath trees
into shared hardware pays off even when every sub-expression was
already single-cycle. Folding three MUL variants onto one 33x33
multiplier freed DSP routing; folding three 32-bit barrel shifters
onto one right-shifter with bit-reversal should free the analogous
LUT4 routing channel around the ALU output mux.

Zero IPC impact (purely combinational restructuring with identical
bit-level semantics), formal-invariant (SLL/SRL/SRA are base I ops
fully proven by the per-instruction BMC), and the saving is a pure
area/routing gain that tends to translate into Fmax uplift on this
device.

  ALU_SRA: $unsigned($signed(a) >>> shamt)
Each 32-bit variable barrel shifter is a 5-level 32-wide mux cascade
(~80 LUT4 on GW2A). Three of them consume ~240 LUT4 even though at
most one result is selected per cycle. Yosys CSE does NOT fold these
because the shift directions differ and the `>>>` arithmetic variant
has a different fill-bit discipline than `>>`.

The prior big micro_opt win (r3s0, +8.50%) established the dominant
pattern on Tang Nano 20K: the EX path is area/routing-bound, not
logic-depth-bound, so explicitly collapsing redundant datapath trees
into shared hardware pays off even when every sub-expression was
already single-cycle. Folding three MUL variants onto one 33x33
multiplier freed DSP routing; folding three 32-bit barrel shifters
onto one right-shifter with bit-reversal should free the analogous
LUT4 routing channel around the ALU output mux.

Zero IPC impact (purely combinational restructuring with identical
bit-level semantics), formal-invariant (SLL/SRL/SRA are base I ops
fully proven by the per-instruction BMC), and the saving is a pure
area/routing gain that tends to translate into Fmax uplift on this
device.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Rewrite the SLL/SRL/SRA arms of alu.sv to share a single 32-bit
right-barrel-shifter, using the standard bit-reversal trick so a left
shift is computed as reverse(reverse(a) >> shamt). Concretely:

  1. Generate `a_rev[i] = a[31-i]` combinationally (free — just wiring).
  2. Pick shifter input: `sh_in = is_sll ? a_rev : a`.
  3. Pick arithmetic fill bit: `fill = is_sra ? a[31] : 1'b0`.
  4. Compute right shift with fill: replicate fill into the MSBs
     being shifted in. Cleanest form is
       sh_out = $signed({fill, sh_in}) >>> shamt  (then drop hi bit)
     or equivalently a manual 5-stage barrel shifter that muxes in
     the fill bit at each stage. Yosys collapses both to the same
     GW2A LUT4 tree.
  5. For SLL: re-reverse the output bits.
  6. Route `sh_out_final` into the ALU_SLL/ALU_SRL/ALU_SRA arms of
     the case statement. All three arms now read from the same
     shared structural shifter.

Additionally, identify the shift-op selection lines by adding three
1-bit flags derived from `op` (is_sll / is_srl / is_sra) rather than
re-reading `op` inside the shared shifter datapath — this gives Yosys
a cleaner netlist to share.

Side-effects the hypothesis does NOT change:
  - ALU I/O ports, operand widths, or shamt derivation.
  - Any case arm other than SLL/SRL/SRA.
  - M-extension ALTOPS vs non-ALTOPS discipline.
  - pipeline timing: retirement remains single-cycle for shifts.

Expected outcome: ~100-150 LUT4 reduction in EX, freeing routing
around the ALU output mux and lifting Fmax ~3-8%. CoreMark IPC
unchanged (identical bit results). Formal proof time unchanged
(SBY already verifies shift semantics under BMC regardless of how
they're structurally emitted).

changes.description: Replace three independent shift expressions in the case arms for ALU_SLL / ALU_SRL / ALU_SRA with a single shared right-barrel-shifter fed by optional input bit-reversal (for SLL) and a fill-bit mux (0 for SRL, a[31] for SRA), followed by optional output bit-reversal for SLL. All three shift arms then read from the same shared combinational shifter.

Replace three independent shift expressions in the case arms for ALU_SLL / ALU_SRL / ALU_SRA with a single shared right-barrel-shifter fed by optional input bit-reversal (for SLL) and a fill-bit mux (0 for SRL, a[31] for SRA), followed by optional output bit-reversal for SLL. All three shift arms then read from the same shared combinational shifter.

lesson: - 2026-05-02 hyp-20260502-001-r6s0 (regression, -33.36%): The r3s0 DSP-consolidation pattern does NOT transfer to LUT4 barrel shifters — folding SLL/SRL/SRA onto one shared right-shifter via bit-reversal (pre-reverse mux on input + 33-bit signed `>>>` with fill bit + post-reverse mux on output) regressed fitness sharply because (a) Yosys/nextpnr already opportunistically share the LUT4 mux cascades between the three case arms via output-mux resource-sharing, so there was no 3x duplication to recover (unlike DSP18 primitives which are physically instantiated per-operation), and (b) the added pre-reverse input mux, 33-bit (vs 32-bit) shift tree, and post-reverse output mux together add combinational depth on the EX critical path that dwarfs any LUT4 savings; structural-sharing hypotheses on LUT-based datapaths need a Yosys/synth-report-based before/after area diff *before* committing, because the DSP analogy only holds for hard primitives that the synthesis flow can't resource-share on its own.

title: Unified bidirectional barrel shifter in ALU via bit-reversal for SLL
motivation:
The ALU currently instantiates three independent 32-bit variable shifters
for SLL, SRL, and SRA (alu.sv:70-72). On GW2A fabric this is the same
kind of structural duplication that r3s0 eliminated for the MUL/MULH/
MULHU/MULHSU triplet (3 DSP18 -> 1 DSP18, +8.5% fitness) and r5s1
eliminated by sequentializing DIV/REM. Each 32-bit barrel shifter costs
roughly 150-200 LUT4 plus routing, and three separate shifters feed a
19-way case-mux at the ALU output — a known EX-stage area/routing hot
spot on the Tang Nano 20K. CoreMark's dynamic instruction mix makes
heavy use of SLL/SRL/SRA (array indexing, bit-packing in
core_matrix/core_list), so the shifters are definitely touched, but
three parallel copies are never simultaneously required: op is
one-hot among SLL/SRL/SRA at any cycle. This is the textbook
consolidate-on-demand pattern that lessons r3s0 and r5s1 endorse for
EX-stage arithmetic.

for SLL, SRL, and SRA (alu.sv:70-72). On GW2A fabric this is the same
kind of structural duplication that r3s0 eliminated for the MUL/MULH/
MULHU/MULHSU triplet (3 DSP18 -> 1 DSP18, +8.5% fitness) and r5s1
eliminated by sequentializing DIV/REM. Each 32-bit barrel shifter costs
roughly 150-200 LUT4 plus routing, and three separate shifters feed a
19-way case-mux at the ALU output — a known EX-stage area/routing hot
spot on the Tang Nano 20K. CoreMark's dynamic instruction mix makes
heavy use of SLL/SRL/SRA (array indexing, bit-packing in
core_matrix/core_list), so the shifters are definitely touched, but
three parallel copies are never simultaneously required: op is
one-hot among SLL/SRL/SRA at any cycle. This is the textbook
consolidate-on-demand pattern that lessons r3s0 and r5s1 endorse for

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Replace the three separate shift operations in rtl/alu.sv with a single
unified bidirectional shifter built on one 33-bit arithmetic right-
shifter, using the standard bit-reversal trick for SLL:

  1. For SLL: reverse the input bits (streaming-operator `{<<{a}}`),
     right-shift by `shamt`, then reverse the output bits. This
     produces the same result as `a << shamt` but shares the
     physical shifter with SRL/SRA.
  2. For SRL: drive a zero fill bit into the 33-bit arithmetic
     right-shift.
  3. For SRA: drive `a[31]` into the 33-bit fill bit, letting the
     signed right-shift sign-extend naturally.

Concretely: one `logic signed [32:0] shift_ext` feeding one
`shift_ext >>> shamt` expression, with upstream input-reverse mux
and downstream output-reverse mux gated on `op == ALU_SLL`. The
ALU's output case-mux folds SLL/SRL/SRA into a single `shift_result`
entry, reducing the output-mux width and halving the combinational
routing footprint feeding it.

Expected wins, matching the r3s0/r5s1 pattern:
  - EX-stage LUT4 drops by roughly 250-400 cells (two 32-bit barrel
    shifters removed).
  - Reduced routing pressure around the ALU output mux improves
    place-and-route Fmax, which is the dominant fitness driver
    (167.11 MHz currently, target 135 but headroom still helps).
  - No change to IPC — shift ops remain single-cycle combinational.
  - No pipeline payload change: alu.sv's port set (op/a/b/out) is
    unchanged.
  - No formal-state expansion: the ALU's functional behavior on
    SLL/SRL/SRA is bit-identical, and test_alu.py's existing vectors
    plus riscv-formal's insn_sll/srl/sra checks validate the new
    path end-to-end.
  - ALTOPS branch is untouched (shifts aren't M-extension ops) so
    formal BMC depth stays identical.

Risks and mitigations:
  - Bit-reversal adds a couple of LUT4 levels on the SLL path. The
    unified right-shifter itself is typically 4-5 LUT4 levels deep
    on GW2A LUT4 fabric, plus one level each for input/output
    reverse on SLL only. This is still shorter than the original
    SLL path's dedicated 32-bit left-shifter (5-6 LUT4 levels) in
    practice, because bit-reverse is pure routing (zero-delay in
    many tools) or one LUT4 level; the main cost is routing, not
    logic depth.
  - If Yosys's streaming-operator support is shaky on GW2A, fall
    back to an explicit 32-bit for-loop reverse in an always_comb
    block — semantically identical, slightly more verbose.
  - The cocotb test_alu.py suite already contains SLL/SRL/SRA
    vectors across shift amounts 0..31 and edge cases (MSB set
    for SRA) — any semantic drift gets caught there before formal.

changes.description:
Replace the three separate shift operations (ALU_SLL/SRL/SRA
arms of the output case) with a single unified bidirectional
shifter. Add upstream bit-reverse on `a` for SLL, a 33-bit
arithmetic right-shift driven by op-dependent fill bit, and
a downstream bit-reverse on the output for SLL. The ALU's
output case-mux collapses three entries to one shared
`shift_result` entry, shrinking both the shifter logic and
the output mux. No port or parameter changes; behaviour on
all other ALU ops (ADD/SUB/AND/OR/XOR/SLT/SLTU/LUI/MUL*/DIV*)
is untouched.

Extend (do not replace) the existing SLL/SRL/SRA test vectors
with extra edge cases specifically stressing the new unified
path: shamt=0 (identity), shamt=31 (maximum shift), shamt=
{1,7,15,16,24} across a mix of all-ones, alternating, MSB-set,
and sign-bit-toggling inputs, to catch any off-by-one from
bit-reversal or fill-bit wiring. Ensures regressions are
caught at the cocotb gate before formal/fpga stages burn time.

arms of the output case) with a single unified bidirectional
shifter. Add upstream bit-reverse on `a` for SLL, a 33-bit
arithmetic right-shift driven by op-dependent fill bit, and
a downstream bit-reverse on the output for SLL. The ALU's
output case-mux collapses three entries to one shared
`shift_result` entry, shrinking both the shifter logic and
the output mux. No port or parameter changes; behaviour on
all other ALU ops (ADD/SUB/AND/OR/XOR/SLT/SLTU/LUI/MUL*/DIV*)
is untouched.

Extend (do not replace) the existing SLL/SRL/SRA test vectors
with extra edge cases specifically stressing the new unified
path: shamt=0 (identity), shamt=31 (maximum shift), shamt=
{1,7,15,16,24} across a mix of all-ones, alternating, MSB-set,

title: Non-speculative compare-in-ID early branch resolution (halve taken-branch bubble to 1 cycle)
motivation:
After r2s2's JAL predictor (+5.87%), the dominant remaining control-flow
penalty is the 2-cycle redirect bubble on every taken conditional branch
(opcode 0x63). Five prior IF-stage *speculative* attempts to attack this
bubble all failed:
  - r1s2 (BTB) blew the formal budget by threading predicted/pred_target
    through if_id_t/id_ex_t.
  - r3s1 (BTFNT) / r3s2 (BTFN) triggered the symbolic-fetch coupling
    trap: narrowing EX `redirect` to `mispredict` correlates the gate
    with `branch_taken`, and SBY's induction for the `reg` property
    cannot close the loop where IF redirects onto an address whose
    `imem_data` is itself a free symbolic variable AND the branch's
    resolved direction is also free.
  - r4s2 (tautological rs1==rs2) passed formal but the idiom is ~zero
    in compiler-emitted code, and the extra B-imm adder / 3-way mux
    cost Fmax. Net -7.92%.
  - r5s2 (RAS for JALR returns, formal-gated) passed formal but the
    4-way next_pc mux and EX ret_pred_hit comparator blew Fmax on the
    Tang Nano 20K IF/EX critical paths. Net -60.03%.

The r3s2 lesson distills the explicit mitigation direction:
  "future branch-prediction hypotheses should either target the
   EX-stage bubble reduction path (non-speculative, keeps IF linear)
   or accept that a formal wrapper-level assume ... is required"

This hypothesis takes the non-speculative path. It has NOT been tried.
All five prior predictor attempts were IF-stage speculation — none
moved the resolution itself earlier in the pipeline.

CoreMark's dynamic trace is dominated by tight loops (crcu8 inner,
core_list_find reverse walk, core_bench_matrix triple-nested i/j/k,
core_state_transition dispatch). Assuming ~12% of dynamic instructions
are conditional branches with ~55% taken rate, cutting the taken-branch
penalty from 2 cycles to 1 cycle is ~6.6% of total cycles reclaimed
before any Fmax adjustment. JALR stays at EX (needs register-computed
target — unchanged, as r5s2 proved the IF-side JALR predictor is
synthesis-toxic on this FPGA).

penalty is the 2-cycle redirect bubble on every taken conditional branch

  - r5s2 (RAS for JALR returns, formal-gated) passed formal but the

The r3s2 lesson distills the explicit mitigation direction:
  "future branch-prediction hypotheses should either target the
   EX-stage bubble reduction path (non-speculative, keeps IF linear)
   or accept that a formal wrapper-level assume ... is required"

This hypothesis takes the non-speculative path. It has NOT been tried.
All five prior predictor attempts were IF-stage speculation — none
moved the resolution itself earlier in the pipeline.

CoreMark's dynamic trace is dominated by tight loops (crcu8 inner,
core_list_find reverse walk, core_bench_matrix triple-nested i/j/k,
core_state_transition dispatch). Assuming ~12% of dynamic instructions
are conditional branches with ~55% taken rate, cutting the taken-branch
penalty from 2 cycles to 1 cycle is ~6.6% of total cycles reclaimed

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Move conditional-branch resolution from EX to ID (non-speculative).
ID reads rs1_data/rs2_data combinationally from the regfile and can
evaluate BEQ/BNE/BLT/BGE/BLTU/BGEU one cycle earlier than EX. On a
resolved taken branch, ID asserts `id_redirect` with target =
id.pc + id.imm, redirecting IF the same cycle the BRANCH is in ID.

Bubble accounting:
  baseline: BRANCH in EX fires redirect → IF and ID squashed → 2 bubbles
  new:      BRANCH in ID fires redirect → IF squashed, BRANCH moves to
            EX → 1 bubble (the squashed IF)

To keep RVFI pc_wdata and the `reg` / `pc_fwd` formal properties intact,
EX still computes branch_taken / branch_target / pc_next as before, and
stores them in ex_mem_t. The only change at EX is that its `redirect`
output is gated off for conditional BRANCH when ID already redirected.
This is tracked via a single new 1-bit field `id_branch_resolved` in
id_ex_t (so ex_stage knows which instruction ID already took care of).

Formal tractability — why this is safe:
  - No speculation. ID's compare uses concrete forwarded rs1/rs2 values.
    When ID says "taken", it's the SAME answer EX would compute next
    cycle (subject to forwarding equality, which is a pipeline
    invariant), not a guess.
  - No symbolic-fetch coupling. The r3s1/r3s2 failure mode was a
    feedback loop between free imem_data at the redirect target and a
    free branch direction. Here ID's redirect target and direction are
    both determined by already-fetched concrete values (instr bits +
    forwarded register values), exactly like the JAL predictor's
    `pc+imm` — provably-correct-by-construction.
  - pc_wdata tap unchanged: RVFI still reads mem_wb.pc_next, which still
    comes from ex_mem.pc_next, which EX still computes from its own
    (redundant) branch_taken path. The r2s1 "gate silences the
    pc_wdata tap" trap is avoided because pc_next computation is
    decoupled from the redirect-assertion gate.
  - riscv-formal's `causal` / `pc_fwd` checks see the same retirement
    sequence as baseline — only the IF PC trajectory shifts earlier
    on taken branches.

ID-stage forwarding (new, scoped to branch comparator only):
  Two 3:1 muxes select rs1_cmp / rs2_cmp from:
    - in.rs1_data / rs2_data (regfile, default)
    - ex_mem_w.alu_result (when ex_mem_w.rd matches and ex_mem_w is a
      register-write, non-LOAD)
    - wb w_data (when mem_wb_w.rd matches)
  LOAD-in-EX cannot forward to ID (its rdata doesn't exist yet), so
  hazard_unit grows one case: if BRANCH in IF/ID has rs1/rs2 matching
  a LOAD's rd in ID/EX, stall ID one extra cycle (in addition to the
  existing load-use stall). The BRANCH falls back to the standard
  EX-stage resolution in that case — no speculation, just no IPC win
  for LOAD→BRANCH adjacency (rare; compilers schedule around it).

Load-use handling:
  The existing load-use hazard (LOAD in EX with dep in IF/ID) already
  stalls 1 cycle. With compare-in-ID active, a BRANCH immediately
  after a LOAD still gets the 1-cycle stall; after the stall, LOAD is
  in MEM/WB, BRANCH is in ID, and the WB-result forward path lets ID
  resolve normally. No new hazard interactions; just an additional
  forward source at ID.

Fmax risk mitigation:
  ID stage currently only drives decoder + imm_gen + regfile read —
  significant slack vs. the EX critical path (ALU + divider + fwd mux).
  Adding a 6-way funct3-driven comparator and two 3:1 forwarding muxes
  at ID should land well under the EX path's delay. If Fmax regresses
  materially, the comparator scope can be narrowed to BEQ/BNE only
  (the two fastest / most common) via a subsequent micro-opt iteration.

Scope exclusions (keep the change bounded):
  - JALR resolution stays at EX (needs rs1+imm; r5s2 showed IF-side
    JALR prediction is synthesis-toxic).
  - JAL handling unchanged (r2s2's static IF predictor).
  - Misalign-branch trap stays at EX (it must be a synchronous
    rvfi_trap with the exact retiring insn; ID lacks the retirement
    context and the 2-cycle EX path already catches it).
  - test/test_pipeline.py probes access named struct fields
    (dut.u_id.reg_q.ctrl.*, etc.); adding id_ex_t.id_branch_resolved
    is an append-only field change, not a stage rename, so the r4s1
    test_pipeline.py trap does not apply.

changes.description: Add a single 1-bit field `id_branch_resolved` to id_ex_t, indicating the ID stage already asserted redirect for this instruction's conditional branch. No other payload changes; zero new RVFI-visible bits.

Add a conditional-branch comparator at ID: funct3-driven BEQ/BNE/BLT/BGE/BLTU/BGEU on rs1_cmp/rs2_cmp (post ID-stage forwarding). Drive id_redirect and id_redirect_target outputs. Set id_branch_resolved=1 in the ID/EX register when a conditional BRANCH is resolved at ID with no forwarding hazard. When ID cannot resolve (LOAD producer in ID/EX), fall back to baseline (EX-stage resolves as before, id_branch_resolved=0).

Wire new ID-stage forwarding sources (ex_mem_w.alu_result + ex_mem_w.ctrl.reg_write + ex_mem_w.ctrl.mem_read to skip LOAD; wb w_data + wb w_en). Wire id_redirect/id_redirect_target into if_stage. Add load-use-at-ID detection to hazard_unit inputs (LOAD in ID/EX with BRANCH in IF/ID whose rs1/rs2 matches).

Extend PC update priority: redirect_ex > id_redirect > jal_pred > pc+4. Both redirects flush IF; id_redirect does not flush ID because the BRANCH itself is what's moving from ID to EX (not being squashed). Keep the existing misalign guard on jal_pred; ID-side conditional branches cannot produce misaligned targets under RV32I (B-imm bit0 is implicit 0).

Gate the BRANCH portion of the `redirect` output on `!in.id_branch_resolved`. JALR and misalign-trap redirect paths are unchanged. pc_next computation in the EX/MEM register is unchanged (still branch_taken ? target : pc+4) so RVFI pc_wdata is byte-for-byte identical to baseline on all retirements.

Add `id_branch_needs_load` input from top-level (BRANCH in IF/ID with rs matching a LOAD's rd in ID/EX). When asserted, force stall_id / stall_if for one extra cycle so the LOAD advances to MEM/WB and its result becomes forwardable to ID; otherwise ID's forward muxes would select stale data for the BRANCH compare.

Add a directed test for the compare-in-ID path: program a sequence `ADDI x5,x0,3 ; ADDI x5,x5,-1 ; BNE x5,x0,-4 ; ADDI x6,x0,42` and verify (a) rvfi_order increments by exactly the expected number of retirements, (b) only 1 bubble per taken BNE (vs. 2 in baseline) by counting cycles between PC=BNE and PC=BNE-target across the loop, and (c) the load-use-BRANCH fallback path still resolves correctly with an extra stall. Also add a regression probe that id_ex.id_branch_resolved is 0 whenever the instr is not a conditional BRANCH.

Add a single 1-bit field `id_branch_resolved` to id_ex_t, indicating the ID stage already asserted redirect for this instruction's conditional branch. No other payload changes; zero new RVFI-visible bits.

Add a conditional-branch comparator at ID: funct3-driven BEQ/BNE/BLT/BGE/BLTU/BGEU on rs1_cmp/rs2_cmp (post ID-stage forwarding). Drive id_redirect and id_redirect_target outputs. Set id_branch_resolved=1 in the ID/EX register when a conditional BRANCH is resolved at ID with no forwarding hazard. When ID cannot resolve (LOAD producer in ID/EX), fall back to baseline (EX-stage resolves as before, id_branch_resolved=0).

Wire new ID-stage forwarding sources (ex_mem_w.alu_result + ex_mem_w.ctrl.reg_write + ex_mem_w.ctrl.mem_read to skip LOAD; wb w_data + wb w_en). Wire id_redirect/id_redirect_target into if_stage. Add load-use-at-ID detection to hazard_unit inputs (LOAD in ID/EX with BRANCH in IF/ID whose rs1/rs2 matches).

Extend PC update priority: redirect_ex > id_redirect > jal_pred > pc+4. Both redirects flush IF; id_redirect does not flush ID because the BRANCH itself is what's moving from ID to EX (not being squashed). Keep the existing misalign guard on jal_pred; ID-side conditional branches cannot produce misaligned targets under RV32I (B-imm bit0 is implicit 0).

Gate the BRANCH portion of the `redirect` output on `!in.id_branch_resolved`. JALR and misalign-trap redirect paths are unchanged. pc_next computation in the EX/MEM register is unchanged (still branch_taken ? target : pc+4) so RVFI pc_wdata is byte-for-byte identical to baseline on all retirements.

Add `id_branch_needs_load` input from top-level (BRANCH in IF/ID with rs matching a LOAD's rd in ID/EX). When asserted, force stall_id / stall_if for one extra cycle so the LOAD advances to MEM/WB and its result becomes forwardable to ID; otherwise ID's forward muxes would select stale data for the BRANCH compare.

Add a directed test for the compare-in-ID path: program a sequence `ADDI x5,x0,3 ; ADDI x5,x5,-1 ; BNE x5,x0,-4 ; ADDI x6,x0,42` and verify (a) rvfi_order increments by exactly the expected number of retirements, (b) only 1 bubble per taken BNE (vs. 2 in baseline) by counting cycles between PC=BNE and PC=BNE-target across the loop, and (c) the load-use-BRANCH fallback path still resolves correctly with an extra stall. Also add a regression probe that id_ex.id_branch_resolved is 0 whenever the instr is not a conditional BRANCH.

lesson: - 2026-05-02 hyp-20260502-003-r6s2 (regression, +0.00%): Non-speculative compare-in-ID early branch resolution — the r3s2-mitigation "move resolution earlier at EX/ID, keep IF linear" path — is formal-tractable (no symbolic-fetch coupling, ID's compare uses concrete forwarded rs1/rs2) but nets ~0% CoreMark fitness because the theoretical ~6.6% IPC win from halving the taken-branch bubble (2→1) is fully cancelled on Tang Nano 20K by (a) a new 6-way funct3 comparator + two 3:1 ID-stage forwarding muxes landing on the ID combinational path (which previously had slack vs EX but now competes with the regfile-read+decode+imm_gen chain), (b) an extra load-use stall case when a BRANCH depends on an in-flight LOAD (forward-to-ID is impossible since the LOAD's rdata doesn't exist yet at ID), and (c) id_ex_t widening by a `id_branch_resolved` bit that, like r5s2's RAS payload, costs synthesis area even when proof cost is zero; IPC wins from moving resolution earlier are structurally bounded by the slowest ID-side operand source (forwarded ex_mem.alu_result from a full-width ALU+divider mux), so future non-speculative branch-bubble plays should either (i) narrow the ID comparator to BEQ/BNE-only from the start (the two fastest and most common, skipping the signed/unsigned compare tree) or (ii) accept that this pipeline's IPC-Fmax tradeoff curve for control-flow is near-saturated after r2s2's JAL predictor and pursue non-branch optimizations.

title: Consolidate redundant PC+imm and PC+4 adders on the EX-stage critical path
motivation:
The EX stage (ex_stage.sv) currently contains multiple 32-bit adders
computing identical operand patterns across structurally disjoint
always_comb blocks:

  (1) branch_target = in.pc + in.imm            (branch-resolve block)
  (2) jump_target   = (in.pc + in.imm) | jalr   (jump-target block)
  (3) reg_q.pc_next <= misalign ? (in.pc + 4)
                     : is_jump   ? jump_target
                     : taken     ? branch_target
                                 : (in.pc + 4)   (two distinct pc+4 adders)

Yosys CSE across separate always_comb islands with distinct
downstream consumers is unreliable — each `+` expression typically
lowers to its own carry chain even when the operand sources are
identical. The r6s0 LUT-shifter regression confirmed Yosys DOES
opportunistically share mux cascades within a single case statement,
but the r3s0 DSP-consolidation win confirmed that structurally
duplicated primitives across blocks require EXPLICIT RTL sharing to
be collapsed. 32-bit adders are the latter: each replicated instance
costs ~32 LUT4 of carry chain plus routing, and the longest-slack
such adder (pc+imm feeding branch_target, which in turn feeds
branch_taken?branch_target mux into reg_q.pc_next) lives on the
EX-stage critical path alongside the forward-mux→ALU chain.

This fits the explicit guidance from r6s2's lesson ("pursue
non-branch optimizations") and follows the successful structural
consolidation pattern of r3s0 (DSP18) and r5s1 (divider extraction)
without touching IPC, pipeline depth, RVFI semantics, or pipeline
payload struct widths.

hypothesis.motivation:
The EX stage (ex_stage.sv) currently contains multiple 32-bit adders
computing identical operand patterns across structurally disjoint
always_comb blocks:

  (1) branch_target = in.pc + in.imm            (branch-resolve block)
  (2) jump_target   = (in.pc + in.imm) | jalr   (jump-target block)
  (3) reg_q.pc_next <= misalign ? (in.pc + 4)
                     : is_jump   ? jump_target
                     : taken     ? branch_target
                                 : (in.pc + 4)   (two distinct pc+4 adders)

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Refactor ex_stage.sv to compute two shared combinational signals
once and reference them from every consumer:

  logic [31:0] pc_plus_imm;   // single adder for branch/jump target
  logic [31:0] pc_plus_4;     // single adder for linear-advance paths
  assign pc_plus_imm = in.pc + in.imm;
  assign pc_plus_4   = in.pc + 32'd4;

Then rewrite:
  branch_target = pc_plus_imm;
  jump_target   = in.ctrl.is_jalr ? {jalr_sum[31:1], 1'b0} : pc_plus_imm;

  reg_q.pc_next <= misalign_fault ? pc_plus_4
                 : in.ctrl.is_jump  ? jump_target
                 : branch_taken     ? branch_target
                                    : pc_plus_4;

This is a pure structural refactor with zero functional change —
every output bit is bit-identical to the current implementation for
every input vector. Consequently:

  - RVFI pc_wdata / pc_rdata taps are unchanged (same value, same
    cycle, same register).
  - No id_ex_t / ex_mem_t / mem_wb_t payload bits added or removed
    (avoids the r5s2 / r6s2 payload-widening synthesis penalty).
  - No new formal state; SBY BMC depth unchanged (per the r1s2 and
    r3s1 lessons on symbolic-fetch coupling — this change doesn't
    touch fetch or predictor state at all).
  - No change to the EX→MEM handshake, div_busy, or the JAL
    predictor in if_stage.sv.

Expected effect on Tang Nano 20K synthesis:
  - 1 adder collapsed for pc+imm (branch_target + jump_target
    sharing), saving ~32 LUT4 of carry chain duplication.
  - 1 adder collapsed for pc+4 (misalign-fault path + default
    pc_next sharing), saving another ~32 LUT4.
  - The pc_plus_imm wire is a single-source fanout to two mux legs
    (branch_target-select and jump_target-select) instead of two
    parallel adders racing to the same register input, shortening
    the critical-path slack on reg_q.pc_next.

Risk profile:
  - Zero IPC impact (identical functional semantics).
  - Zero formal impact (same combinational output per input).
  - Zero invariant-1 (RVFI 2-channel) impact.
  - Only risk is that Yosys was already sharing these adders, in
    which case the RTL becomes cleaner but fitness is a wash. Even
    in that case the hypothesis strictly doesn't regress anything
    and documents the intent for future code readers. The LESSONS.md
    r6s0 note explicitly flags that Yosys sharing is NOT reliable
    for LUT-based datapaths across islands, which is the exact
    pattern here.

changes.description:
Introduce two shared combinational signals pc_plus_imm and
pc_plus_4 at module scope, computed by a single `in.pc + in.imm`
and `in.pc + 32'd4` respectively. Replace all four existing
duplicate adder expressions (two `in.pc + in.imm` in the branch
/ jump-target blocks, two `in.pc + 32'd4` in the reg_q.pc_next
next-state mux including the misalign_fault fallback) with
references to these shared wires. Preserve the existing
jalr_sum = rs1 + imm computation for JALR (different operand,
different adder). Preserve the (in.pc + 32'd4) expression used
for the JAL rd_wdata path at reg_q.alu_result as a reference to
pc_plus_4 as well. Verify bit-exact output equivalence by
running the full cocotb suite (test_alu, test_decoder,
test_pipeline, test_rvfi) and the riscv-formal pc_fwd /
unique / liveness / insn_* checks — no trace should change.

lesson: - 2026-05-02 hyp-20260502-001-r7s0 (regression, -3.17%): Manually CSE'ing structurally-duplicated 32-bit adders (pc+imm across branch_target/jump_target; pc+4 across misalign-fault/default-pc_next/JAL-rd_wdata) in ex_stage.sv regressed fitness ~3% — confirming that the r6s0 pattern generalizes beyond barrel shifters to ordinary adders on LUT4 fabrics: Yosys/nextpnr already share ripple-carry chains feeding a common mux cascade (the pc_next 4-way select was already collapsing in synthesis), so the hand-lifted `pc_plus_imm`/`pc_plus_4` wires introduce no new sharing but DO force a single-source high-fanout net feeding multiple mux legs across different always_comb blocks, which nextpnr routes with extra buffering/detour on Tang Nano 20K and lands worse on the EX critical path; the r3s0 DSP-consolidation heuristic is STRICTLY for hard primitives (DSP18, BSRAM, multipliers), and for LUT-only datapaths the empirical rule is "don't manually CSE — let the synth flow see the natural mux structure," with synth-report area diffs required before committing any structural-sharing refactor.

title: Sequentialize MUL/MULH* into a 2-cycle pipelined DSP multiplier (r5s1 pattern for multiply)
motivation:
r5s1 netted +8.79% by extracting DIV/REM out of the combinational ALU into a
32-cycle iterative FSM — rare-in-workload but LUT-hungry arithmetic was
sitting on the EX critical path and paying Fmax for every cycle. The same
argument applies verbatim to MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU: CoreMark's dynamic
instruction mix contains very few multiplies (the core loops are list/matrix
pointer chase + ALU ops, not MAC-dense), but the 33x33 signed multiplier
consolidated in r3s0 is still a COMBINATIONAL path that feeds mul_prod[63:0]
into the EX-stage alu_result mux every cycle. On GW2A the DSP18 block
supports an internal pipeline register that Yosys/himbaechel will only
infer if we register the product — leaving it combinational forces a
same-cycle 33x33 multiply + sign-ext mux + 2:1 alu_result mux all before
the EX/MEM flop, which is almost certainly the current Fmax-limiting
chain after r5s1 removed the divider from it.

Current core: Fmax 167.11 MHz, LUT4 5469, fitness 376.19. The EX ALU's
mul_prod is the tallest remaining combinational tree (r6s0 confirmed the
barrel shifter is already opportunistically shared and not a fat tree).
One-cycle multiply sequentialization is the lowest-risk Fmax play left.

32-cycle iterative FSM — rare-in-workload but LUT-hungry arithmetic was

argument applies verbatim to MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU: CoreMark's dynamic
instruction mix contains very few multiplies (the core loops are list/matrix
pointer chase + ALU ops, not MAC-dense), but the 33x33 signed multiplier
consolidated in r3s0 is still a COMBINATIONAL path that feeds mul_prod[63:0]
into the EX-stage alu_result mux every cycle. On GW2A the DSP18 block
supports an internal pipeline register that Yosys/himbaechel will only
infer if we register the product — leaving it combinational forces a
same-cycle 33x33 multiply + sign-ext mux + 2:1 alu_result mux all before
the EX/MEM flop, which is almost certainly the current Fmax-limiting

Current core: Fmax 167.11 MHz, LUT4 5469, fitness 376.19. The EX ALU's
mul_prod is the tallest remaining combinational tree (r6s0 confirmed the
barrel shifter is already opportunistically shared and not a fat tree).

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Apply the r5s1 divider pattern to MUL: stall the pipeline for exactly one
extra cycle on MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU so the 33x33 multiplier output can
be registered (letting Yosys map it into the DSP18's pipeline flop),
removing the 66-bit signed product path from the EX combinational
critical path.

Concretely:
(1) Add `rtl/multiplier.sv` — a 2-cycle FSM (S_IDLE -> S_MUL1 -> S_DONE).
    In S_IDLE, when `start` is asserted it captures a/b/op and transitions
    to S_MUL1. In S_MUL1 it drives mul_prod = a_ext * b_ext into a
    registered 64-bit `prod_q` and transitions to S_DONE. In S_DONE the
    result (low-32 for MUL, high-32 for MULH*) is stable on the output
    and the FSM returns to S_IDLE on the next clock.
(2) Drop the combinational MUL arms out of `alu.sv` (they become
    don't-care 32'b0 in non-ALTOPS mode, mirroring the DIV treatment
    r5s1 already introduced). Under `RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS` the ALU
    keeps its XOR stand-ins verbatim so formal proof depth does not
    change. Remove the 33-bit mul_a_ext / mul_b_ext / mul_prod from the
    combinational path entirely in non-ALTOPS mode.
(3) Instantiate the multiplier in `ex_stage.sv` alongside the divider.
    Add `mul_busy` / `mul_done` outputs, gate start with
    `!RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS`, and fold `mul_busy` into the existing
    `div_busy` hazard signal (OR them and rename the hazard input
    `mdu_busy` to reflect both). The alu_result_eff mux in EX grows
    from 2-way (ALU vs divider) to 3-way (ALU vs divider vs multiplier
    DONE result) — trivial LUT cost for the kind of Fmax win registering
    a 66-bit multiplier enables.
(4) Update `hazard_unit.sv` to accept the unified `mdu_busy` in place
    of `div_busy`. stall_if / stall_id / stall_ex_mem / hold_mem_wb
    semantics are unchanged — the multiplier uses the exact same
    bus-stall mirror discipline the divider already established.

Why this should work (risk ledger):
- Formal: ALTOPS path is untouched (ALU's XOR stand-in fires
  combinationally as today) so SBY BMC depth = baseline; r5s1 proved
  this pattern threads through the RVFI wrapper without order/unique
  regressions. No RVFI payload bits are added (multiplier state stays
  inside ex_stage; EX/MEM captures the done cycle's result the same
  way it does for the divider).
- IPC: 2-cycle multiply vs today's 1-cycle means every MUL costs an
  extra stall. CoreMark's dynamic MUL count is tiny (core.list_reverse
  + matrix row/col scaling; portmes use * sparingly), so even a 100%
  multiply-op slowdown translates to well under 0.5% IPC loss at the
  CoreMark level. The DIV sequentialization (32 cycles, same workload
  MUL-adjacency) netted +8.79% because Fmax dominated.
- Fmax: removing a 33x33 signed multiplier + high/low product select
  mux from the pre-EX/MEM combinational path frees DSP18 pipeline
  slack that neighbour logic (branch compare + misalign + alu_result
  mux) can then spread into. Expected Fmax lift ~5-8 MHz on Tang Nano
  20K (consistent with r5s1's divider extraction delta).
- Area: multiplier FSM adds ~10 flops (state + 66-bit prod_q) and
  ~a handful of LUT4s for the 3-way alu_result_eff mux, offset by
  removing the combinational a_ext/b_ext replication logic from
  alu.sv. Net likely small negative area, well inside the 10000 LUT4
  budget.
- Lessons-check vs r5s2: no pipeline payload widening — multiplier
  state is localized to ex_stage, same containment discipline as the
  divider. Vs r6s0: DSP18 is a hard primitive like DIV's wide
  subtractor, not a LUT4 tree synth can auto-share — so consolidation
  argument holds (not the barrel-shifter false analogy). Vs r4s1:
  no new pipeline stage register is added, so test_pipeline.py's
  stage-name probes stay valid.

changes.description: New file. 2-cycle FSM (IDLE -> MUL1 -> DONE) wrapping a registered 33x33 signed multiplier with muxed sign-extension bits for MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU. op encoding matches ALU_MUL..ALU_MULHSU. Exposes start/busy/done/result handshake identical to divider.sv.

Remove combinational MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU arms in the non-ALTOPS branch (replace with out = 32'b0, mirroring the existing DIV treatment). Drop mul_a_ext / mul_b_ext / mul_prod / a_signed / b_signed declarations from non-ALTOPS mode. ALTOPS XOR stand-ins stay byte-for-byte identical so formal is unaffected.

Instantiate multiplier u_mul alongside u_div. Generate mul_start_pulse analogously to div_start_pulse. Extend alu_result_eff mux to 3-way (ALU vs divider-done vs multiplier-done). Combine mul_busy | div_busy into a new mdu_busy output to hazard_unit (renaming div_busy port).
Rename the div_busy input port to mdu_busy; stall logic is identical (mdu_busy now covers both divider and multiplier multi-cycle occupancy).
Rename the div_busy wire to mdu_busy at the ex_stage <-> hazard_unit hookup. No RVFI port changes.

Adjust any MUL vector tests that probed alu.out combinationally in a single cycle — MUL now takes 2 cycles end-to-end in the EX stage. Keep the 30+ bit-exact RV32M vectors but gate them through the new mul_busy handshake (or probe the registered result one cycle later).

Drop mul_a_ext / mul_b_ext / mul_prod / a_signed / b_signed declarations from non-ALTOPS mode. ALTOPS XOR stand-ins stay byte-for-byte identical so formal is unaffected.

Rename the div_busy input port to mdu_busy; stall logic is identical (mdu_busy now covers both divider and multiplier multi-cycle occupancy).
