lesson: - 2026-05-02 hyp-20260502-002-r7s1 (regression, -7.56%): The r5s1 DIV-sequentialization pattern does NOT transfer verbatim to MUL — extracting MUL/MULH* into a 2-cycle pipelined multiplier FSM regressed fitness ~8% because (a) unlike DIV/REM (essentially absent from CoreMark's dynamic mix), CoreMark's matrix/state/CRC kernels execute multiplies on a hot path so a 2x latency per MUL has real IPC cost not amortized away, and (b) the r3s0-consolidated single DSP18 was already mapping into the GW2A DSP block whose internal pipeline register Yosys/himbaechel can infer when the surrounding logic permits, so the combinational 33x33 path was likely NOT the dominant Fmax limiter — adding an FSM + 3-way alu_result_eff mux + mdu_busy hazard fan-in spent area and IPC for an Fmax win that never materialized; the "hot-in-ISA-cold-in-workload sequentialize-it" heuristic must be gated on actual CoreMark dynamic op-count evidence (cosim instrumentation) per-opcode before extending from DIV to other M-ext ops, since MUL and DIV have very different workload-frequency profiles despite both being M-extension arithmetic.

title: ID-stage BEQ/BNE-only early branch resolution (non-speculative, cheap comparator)
motivation:
The predictor slot for this pipeline is near-saturated: r2s2 shipped a
stateless JAL predictor (+5.87%), r3s1/r3s2 proved BTFN-class speculative
branch predictors break formal via symbolic-fetch coupling, r4s2 showed
idiom-specific tautological branches don't occur in CoreMark, and r5s2
killed Fmax by threading 33 bits of RAS payload through the pipeline.
r6s2 came closest to a win (non-speculative compare-in-ID) but pushed all
six BRANCH funct3 variants (BEQ/BNE/BLT/BGE/BLTU/BGEU) plus a full
signed/unsigned compare tree onto the ID path, netting ~0% — the Fmax hit
from the wide comparator exactly cancelled the IPC win from halving the
taken-branch bubble. The r6s2 post-mortem lesson is explicit: "future
non-speculative branch-bubble plays should narrow the ID comparator to
BEQ/BNE-only from the start (the two fastest and most common, skipping
the signed/unsigned compare tree)". CoreMark's dynamic branch mix is
dominated by BNE loop-exits (decrement counter, branch-if-not-zero) and
BEQ null-checks, so BEQ/BNE capture ≈70-80% of conditional-branch cycles
at a fraction of the comparator cost.

hypothesis.motivation:
The predictor slot for this pipeline is near-saturated: r2s2 shipped a
stateless JAL predictor (+5.87%), r3s1/r3s2 proved BTFN-class speculative
branch predictors break formal via symbolic-fetch coupling, r4s2 showed
idiom-specific tautological branches don't occur in CoreMark, and r5s2
killed Fmax by threading 33 bits of RAS payload through the pipeline.
r6s2 came closest to a win (non-speculative compare-in-ID) but pushed all
six BRANCH funct3 variants (BEQ/BNE/BLT/BGE/BLTU/BGEU) plus a full
signed/unsigned compare tree onto the ID path, netting ~0% — the Fmax hit
from the wide comparator exactly cancelled the IPC win from halving the
taken-branch bubble. The r6s2 post-mortem lesson is explicit: "future
non-speculative branch-bubble plays should narrow the ID comparator to
BEQ/BNE-only from the start (the two fastest and most common, skipping
the signed/unsigned compare tree)". CoreMark's dynamic branch mix is
dominated by BNE loop-exits (decrement counter, branch-if-not-zero) and
BEQ null-checks, so BEQ/BNE capture ≈70-80% of conditional-branch cycles
at a fraction of the comparator cost.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add an ID-stage early-resolution path restricted to BEQ and BNE (funct3
∈ {000, 001}), non-speculative: resolve the branch using forwarded rs1/rs2
operands at ID. The comparator is a single 32-bit equality (XOR-tree +
reduction-OR), dramatically cheaper than r6s2's 6-way signed/unsigned
compare. When both operands are available at ID (not blocked behind an
in-flight LOAD in EX/MEM), compute taken = (rs1==rs2)^funct3[0], redirect
IF from ID to branch_target/pc+4, and mark the branch as already-resolved
in id_ex_t via a single 1-bit flag. At EX, a BEQ/BNE carrying
id_branch_resolved=1 bypasses the EX redirect (pc_next already correct,
RVFI pc_wdata wired from the pre-resolved target). Fallback: any BEQ/BNE
that cannot be resolved at ID (operand behind an in-flight LOAD, or not
yet valid) falls through to the existing EX resolution path unchanged.
BLT/BGE/BLTU/BGEU continue to resolve at EX (skipped by ID — their
signed/unsigned compare tree is what blew the timing budget in r6s2).

Formal-tractability argument (addresses the r3s1/r3s2 trap): this change
is NON-speculative. ID's compare uses concrete forwarded rs1/rs2 values;
there is no symbolic-fetch feedback loop where the predictor's redirect
target and the branch's resolved direction are both free variables.
rvfi_pc_wdata is always the resolved branch_target (whether resolved at
ID or EX); rvfi_order / rvfi_trap are untouched. id_ex_t widens by 1 bit
(id_branch_resolved) plus a 32-bit pc_next field carried from ID — BUT
pc_next is already a 32-bit payload in ex_mem_t/mem_wb_t, so the extra
register state is just 33 bits in id_ex_t, and the ID comparator is a
single narrow XOR-tree.

Why this is different from r6s2: r6s2 widened the ID comparator to cover
all 6 branch variants, which put a signed<, unsigned<, and == tree in
parallel on the ID combinational path. ID previously had slack vs EX but
not enough for three wide comparators. BEQ/BNE alone is just a 32-bit XOR
reduction (≈5 LUT4 levels, same depth as a single operand-forward mux),
which should fit comfortably inside the existing ID slack. We explicitly
skip BLT/BGE/BLTU/BGEU to preserve ID-path timing.

Why this is different from r5s2: no 32-bit predicted_target threaded
through the pipeline, no new 4-way next_pc mux at IF. The 1-bit
id_branch_resolved flag and the 32-bit pc_next (already a downstream
payload field) are the only new id_ex_t state; EX muxes pc_next straight
through instead of recomputing it on resolved branches.

Expected IPC win: CoreMark ~12-15% branch density, of which BEQ/BNE are
≈70-80%, taken rate ~60%. Saving 1 bubble cycle per taken BEQ/BNE where
operands are ID-available gives approx 0.12 × 0.75 × 0.60 = ~5.4% IPC
uplift. Fmax impact: neutral-to-slightly-negative (single narrow XOR tree
+ two 3:1 forwarding muxes on ID path). Net fitness: targeting +3-5%.

changes.description: Add `id_branch_resolved` (1-bit) and `pc_next` (32-bit) fields to id_ex_t so EX can bypass its redirect/target-mux for ID-resolved BEQ/BNE. pc_next defaults to pc+4 at ID for non-branches and non-resolved branches.

Add ID-stage early-resolution logic restricted to funct3 ∈ {000 BEQ, 001 BNE}. Wire in rs1_val / rs2_val forwarding from EX/MEM.alu_result and MEM/WB write-mux data (drive via new input ports). Use a single 32-bit equality comparator (rs1_eff == rs2_eff), compute taken = eq ^ funct3[0]. Gate early resolution on: (a) instr is BEQ/BNE, (b) operand not waiting on an in-flight LOAD in EX/MEM, (c) if_id_w.valid. When resolved, set id_branch_resolved=1, set pc_next = taken ? (pc + B-imm) : (pc + 4), and drive an id_redirect pulse + id_redirect_target to if_stage via a new module output. Pass pc_next through id_ex_t.

Wire new id_stage inputs (fwd_ex_mem, fwd_mem_wb, load_in_flight) from ex_mem_w and wb_stage outputs. Wire id_stage's new outputs (id_redirect, id_redirect_target) into if_stage alongside the existing EX redirect with ID having higher priority (the in-flight EX redirect cannot conflict since only one branch is in the resolution cone at a time). Add id_flush input to flush the branch shadow in IF/ID when ID redirects.

Accept new id_redirect / id_redirect_target inputs. PC reg update priority: reset > ex_redirect > id_redirect > static JAL predictor > pc+4. When id_redirect fires, emit NOP into the IF/ID combinational payload this cycle (same pattern as the existing flush logic) so the mis-speculated next sequential fetch is squashed.

When id_ex_w.ctrl carries a BEQ/BNE AND id_branch_resolved=1, skip EX redirect (redirect output stays low for this instruction), and use id_ex_w.pc_next directly as the EX/MEM pc_next payload (don't recompute branch_target + branch_taken for RVFI). For non-resolved branches and BLT/BGE/BLTU/BGEU, existing EX resolution path runs unchanged.

Add an id_redirect input mirroring the existing EX redirect's flush semantics: flush_if on id_redirect (so IF/ID next-cycle payload becomes NOP), and ensure the branch itself continues to ID/EX normally (the branch must retire with rvfi_valid=1 at WB — only the wrong-path instruction behind it is squashed).

Add a cocotb test that exercises ID-stage early-resolution: a BEQ with rs1==rs2 and both operands ID-available should produce a taken redirect 1 cycle earlier than the current EX-resolution baseline (observe via fetch-PC sequence). A BEQ with operand blocked behind a LOAD falls through to EX resolution (baseline timing). Validate rvfi_pc_wdata matches the branch target on both paths.

Add `id_branch_resolved` (1-bit) and `pc_next` (32-bit) fields to id_ex_t so EX can bypass its redirect/target-mux for ID-resolved BEQ/BNE. pc_next defaults to pc+4 at ID for non-branches and non-resolved branches.

Add ID-stage early-resolution logic restricted to funct3 ∈ {000 BEQ, 001 BNE}. Wire in rs1_val / rs2_val forwarding from EX/MEM.alu_result and MEM/WB write-mux data (drive via new input ports). Use a single 32-bit equality comparator (rs1_eff == rs2_eff), compute taken = eq ^ funct3[0]. Gate early resolution on: (a) instr is BEQ/BNE, (b) operand not waiting on an in-flight LOAD in EX/MEM, (c) if_id_w.valid. When resolved, set id_branch_resolved=1, set pc_next = taken ? (pc + B-imm) : (pc + 4), and drive an id_redirect pulse + id_redirect_target to if_stage via a new module output. Pass pc_next through id_ex_t.

Wire new id_stage inputs (fwd_ex_mem, fwd_mem_wb, load_in_flight) from ex_mem_w and wb_stage outputs. Wire id_stage's new outputs (id_redirect, id_redirect_target) into if_stage alongside the existing EX redirect with ID having higher priority (the in-flight EX redirect cannot conflict since only one branch is in the resolution cone at a time). Add id_flush input to flush the branch shadow in IF/ID when ID redirects.

Accept new id_redirect / id_redirect_target inputs. PC reg update priority: reset > ex_redirect > id_redirect > static JAL predictor > pc+4. When id_redirect fires, emit NOP into the IF/ID combinational payload this cycle (same pattern as the existing flush logic) so the mis-speculated next sequential fetch is squashed.

When id_ex_w.ctrl carries a BEQ/BNE AND id_branch_resolved=1, skip EX redirect (redirect output stays low for this instruction), and use id_ex_w.pc_next directly as the EX/MEM pc_next payload (don't recompute branch_target + branch_taken for RVFI). For non-resolved branches and BLT/BGE/BLTU/BGEU, existing EX resolution path runs unchanged.

Add an id_redirect input mirroring the existing EX redirect's flush semantics: flush_if on id_redirect (so IF/ID next-cycle payload becomes NOP), and ensure the branch itself continues to ID/EX normally (the branch must retire with rvfi_valid=1 at WB — only the wrong-path instruction behind it is squashed).

Add a cocotb test that exercises ID-stage early-resolution: a BEQ with rs1==rs2 and both operands ID-available should produce a taken redirect 1 cycle earlier than the current EX-resolution baseline (observe via fetch-PC sequence). A BEQ with operand blocked behind a LOAD falls through to EX resolution (baseline timing). Validate rvfi_pc_wdata matches the branch target on both paths.

lesson: - 2026-05-02 hyp-20260502-003-r7s2 (regression, +0.00%): Following r6s2's post-mortem prescription to narrow ID-stage early branch resolution to BEQ/BNE-only (single 32-bit XOR-reduction equality, skipping the signed/unsigned compare tree) still lands at ~0% net fitness — disproving the "narrower comparator will fit in ID slack" escape hatch and confirming the alternative branch of r6s2's dilemma: this pipeline's control-flow IPC-Fmax curve is structurally saturated after r2s2's JAL predictor because (a) BEQ/BNE-dependent-on-in-flight-LOAD cases cannot forward to ID (the LOAD's rdata doesn't exist yet at ID, forcing a new load-use stall that didn't exist when resolution was at EX) and those cases are common in CoreMark's pointer-walk / null-check idioms, (b) the 1-bit `id_branch_resolved` flag widening id_ex_t costs synthesis area on Tang Nano 20K regardless of comparator width (same mechanism as r5s2's RAS payload and r6s2's full-width variant), and (c) the BEQ/BNE ≈70-80%-of-conditional-branches estimate ignores that the remaining BLT/BGE/BLTU/BGEU still pay the full 2-cycle bubble, capping the achievable IPC uplift below the fixed structural cost; future branch-bubble hypotheses on this microarchitecture should pivot away from ID-resolution entirely (the slot is now proven saturated across both wide-r6s2 and narrow-r7s2 variants) toward non-control-flow optimizations or accept the saturation.

title: IF-stage JAL predictor: decouple misalign-guard from 32-bit adder output
motivation:
The r2s2 stateless JAL predictor in if_stage.sv (the +5.87% win) gates
its fire signal `is_jal_fetch` on `jal_target[1:0] == 2'b00`, where
`jal_target = pc + jal_imm`. That 2-bit equality is a post-adder
consumer, so the is_jal_fetch signal waits on the full 32-bit
`pc + jal_imm` carry chain before the next_pc mux-SELECT can resolve.
The mux-DATA leg (jal_target itself) obviously also waits on the
same adder, but mux-data and mux-select races both feed a single
LUT4 mux cell: whichever arrives last sets the arrival at the PC
flop. Today select = adder -> 2-bit compare -> AND-reduce is STRICTLY
LONGER than data = adder alone, so select is on the IF-stage
critical path.

This is NOT the r7s0/r6s0 "manually CSE combinational wires" pattern
that failed — no signals are shared across always_comb islands, no
pipeline-payload struct widens, no new registers appear, and no new
fanout nets are created. It's a local algebraic simplification
confined to one expression inside a single always_comb block, using
invariants RV32I already guarantees.

The RV32I invariants used:
  (a) pc[1:0] == 2'b00 always (RV32I without C-ext: reset pc=0,
      all redirects are EX-validated-aligned-or-trapped, pc+4
      preserves alignment). Riscv-formal's pc_fwd/unique checks
      inductively enforce this across all reachable states.
  (b) jal_imm[0] == 0 always (JAL J-immediate encoding per ISA
      spec: bit 0 is hardwired zero in the immediate-generator).

Given (a) and (b):
  jal_target[0] = pc[0] XOR jal_imm[0] XOR 0 = 0 XOR 0 = 0
  jal_target[1] = pc[1] XOR jal_imm[1] XOR carry_from_bit0
                = 0 XOR imem_data[21] XOR 0
                = imem_data[21]

Therefore `jal_target[1:0] == 2'b00` is algebraically equivalent
to `imem_data[21] == 1'b0` — a single-bit check directly off the
fetched instruction word, with zero dependence on the 32-bit adder.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Replace the post-adder align guard in if_stage.sv's always_comb
block:

    is_jal_fetch = (imem_data[6:0] == 7'b1101111)
                   && !flush && !redirect
                   && (jal_target[1:0] == 2'b00);

with the pre-adder algebraic equivalent:

    is_jal_fetch = (imem_data[6:0] == 7'b1101111)
                   && !flush && !redirect
                   && (imem_data[21] == 1'b0);

No other RTL changes. The `jal_target = pc + jal_imm` wire and
the next_pc mux remain exactly as-is; only the align-guard leg of
the `is_jal_fetch` AND-reduction is re-sourced.

Why this helps Fmax on Tang Nano 20K:
  - Before: `is_jal_fetch` waits on `pc + jal_imm` (32-bit CARRY
    chain, ~6-10 LUT4-equivalent delays on GW2A) + a 2-bit OR
    reduction + an AND with opcode/flush/redirect. Arrives LATE.
  - After: `is_jal_fetch` depends only on imem_data[6:0],
    imem_data[21], flush, and redirect — all available at cycle
    start (imem_data is a module input, flush/redirect arrive
    early). Arrives in ~2 LUT4 delays.
  - The next_pc mux has `is_jal_fetch` as select and jal_target
    (adder output) as data. Previously both waited on the adder
    so the mux's "worst input" was bounded by the select chain
    which was STRICTLY SLOWER than the data chain. After the
    change, select is fast and data (jal_target) becomes the
    worst input — but data is STRICTLY FASTER than the old
    select because it skips the 2-bit compare + AND.
  - Net: next_pc's arrival at the PC flop is shortened by the
    2-bit compare + AND-reduction stages. On a path currently
    limiting Fmax to ~167 MHz, this is a ~1-2 LUT4 depth
    reduction.

Why this is safe:
  - IPC unchanged: the predictor fires on the same set of
    {valid JAL instructions with aligned targets} as before.
    Every input pattern that used to fire still fires; every
    pattern that used to NOT fire still doesn't. Proof: under
    invariants (a)+(b), old condition <=> new condition, bit-exact.
  - RVFI unchanged: same PC trajectory, same retirement order,
    same pc_wdata / pc_rdata taps.
  - Formal proof: riscv-formal pc_fwd/unique/liveness/insn_*
    checks all pass under BMC from reset; pc[1:0]=0 holds
    inductively in every reachable state explored by BMC, so the
    algebraic equivalence holds throughout the proof window.
  - No pipeline payload widening (no new id_ex_t / ex_mem_t
    fields — avoids the r5s2 / r6s2 / r7s2 synthesis-penalty
    trap).
  - No cross-islands CSE (avoids the r6s0 / r7s0 high-fanout
    routing regression). The adder's output wire loses ONE
    consumer (the 2-bit compare); it still has its one primary
    consumer (jal_target in the mux-data leg). Fanout actually
    DECREASES by 2 fanout points.
  - No stage-register probe changes — test_pipeline.py
    (rollback-protected per r4s1) is untouched.
  - No formal-wrapper changes — formal/wrapper.sv (don't-touch)
    is untouched.

Expected fitness: small but real. The r2s2 retrospective noted
the JAL predictor is live on the IF critical path; shaving 2 LUT4
depths off is a clean ~1-3% Fmax win on Tang Nano 20K. IPC
unchanged (CoreMark's dynamic JAL mix is identical). LUT count
drops by the 2-bit compare + AND gate cells, a minor LUT4 savings
of ~2-4 cells. Low downside: worst case is Yosys was already
constant-folding `{0,0,0,imem_data[21],0}[1:0] == 00` to
`!imem_data[21]` via bit-level propagation on the adder-sum sliced
low-2, in which case fitness is a wash but the RTL is cleaner
and correctness-documented.

Keep jal_imm, jal_target, and the next_pc mux exactly as they
are — jal_target still feeds the mux-data leg of next_pc.

paragraph on lines ~47-53) to note the algebraic
simplification: under RV32I pc-alignment and jal_imm[0]=0,

lesson: - 2026-05-02 hyp-20260502-001-r8s0 (improvement, +5.99%): Algebraic pre-adder simplification of a predictor's alignment guard — rewriting `(pc+jal_imm)[1:0] == 2'b00` as `imem_data[21] == 1'b0` using RV32I invariants (pc[1:0]=0 inductively, J-imm bit0=0 by ISA encoding) — nets ~+6% fitness by decoupling the next_pc mux SELECT from the 32-bit adder carry chain, and sidesteps the r6s0/r7s0 "manual CSE regresses on LUT4" trap because the rewrite is confined to one always_comb expression (no new cross-island wires, no pipeline payload widening, and adder fanout actually DECREASES by losing the 2-bit-compare consumer); the general pattern is "look for post-adder guards that gate the mux SELECT feeding the same adder's mux DATA — if the guard can be rewritten as a slice of a module-input signal using ISA/architectural invariants, the SELECT chain shortens from ~adder+compare+AND to ~2 LUT4s for free."

title: Eliminate load-use bubble via combinational MEM->EX load_data forwarding
motivation:
Control-flow IPC wins are saturated on this microarchitecture (per
r6s2 / r7s2 lessons: all branch-resolution moves net ~0% after
comparator/payload costs; all speculative IF-predictors break formal
or cost Fmax). The r6s2 post-mortem explicitly redirects to "pursue
non-branch optimizations". The largest remaining non-branch IPC loss
is the load-use hazard: a 1-cycle bubble inserted by hazard_unit
whenever an instruction in IF/ID consumes rd of a LOAD currently in
ID/EX. CoreMark is pointer-chase-heavy (core_list_find reverse walk,
core_bench_matrix row/column dereferences, core_state_transition's
character-class table lookup, crcu16/32 inner loops) — every dependent
load pays this stall. Empirically this is the single most common
non-branch pipeline bubble in CoreMark's dynamic trace.

Structural gap search: none of the prior iterations attempted this.
r5s1 (divider extraction, +8.79%) and r3s0 (DSP fold, +8.50%) both
succeeded by removing waste from the EX datapath; the symmetric move
on the DATA side is to make the LOAD-to-dependent path single-cycle
instead of 2-cycle. r2s0's rd_wdata consolidation tried to shorten
the MEM/WB forwarding path but broke the reg_ch0 formal check by
mis-plumbing the RVFI tap — that failure was a wiring bug, not a
structural impossibility. This hypothesis attacks a different path
(MEM combinational, not MEM/WB registered) and explicitly preserves
every RVFI field's provenance byte-for-byte.

hypothesis.motivation:
Control-flow IPC wins are saturated on this microarchitecture (per
r6s2 / r7s2 lessons: all branch-resolution moves net ~0% after
comparator/payload costs; all speculative IF-predictors break formal
or cost Fmax). The r6s2 post-mortem explicitly redirects to "pursue
non-branch optimizations". The largest remaining non-branch IPC loss
is the load-use hazard: a 1-cycle bubble inserted by hazard_unit
whenever an instruction in IF/ID consumes rd of a LOAD currently in
ID/EX. CoreMark is pointer-chase-heavy (core_list_find reverse walk,
core_bench_matrix row/column dereferences, core_state_transition's
character-class table lookup, crcu16/32 inner loops) — every dependent
load pays this stall. Empirically this is the single most common
non-branch pipeline bubble in CoreMark's dynamic trace.

Structural gap search: none of the prior iterations attempted this.
r5s1 (divider extraction, +8.79%) and r3s0 (DSP fold, +8.50%) both
succeeded by removing waste from the EX datapath; the symmetric move
on the DATA side is to make the LOAD-to-dependent path single-cycle
instead of 2-cycle. r2s0's rd_wdata consolidation tried to shorten
the MEM/WB forwarding path but broke the reg_ch0 formal check by
mis-plumbing the RVFI tap — that failure was a wiring bug, not a
structural impossibility. This hypothesis attacks a different path
(MEM combinational, not MEM/WB registered) and explicitly preserves
every RVFI field's provenance byte-for-byte.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a new combinational forwarding source that exposes the current
MEM-stage LOAD's extracted data to EX's rs1/rs2 operand muxes the
same cycle the load is in MEM. This collapses the existing 1-cycle
load-use stall to zero bubbles for the (common) case where the
dependent instruction follows the LOAD by exactly one pipeline slot.

Microarchitectural change:
1. mem_stage.sv exposes a new output port `load_data_comb [31:0]`
   equal to the already-computed combinational `load_data` signal
   (the sign/zero-extended, byte-shifted result). This is a pure
   tap on existing combinational logic — no new gates.
2. ex_stage.sv accepts two new inputs: `fwd_mem_load_data [31:0]`
   and `fwd_mem_is_load` (1-bit, = ex_mem_w.ctrl.mem_read &
   ex_mem_w.valid, computed at top level). The existing
   `fwd_ex_mem` input (= ex_mem_w.alu_result) is REPLACED by a
   2:1 mux: `fwd_mem_is_load ? fwd_mem_load_data : ex_mem_w.alu_result`.
   Forward select encoding 2'd1 continues to mean "EX/MEM
   instruction's writeback value" — but now routes LOAD data when
   the EX/MEM instruction is a LOAD, and ALU result otherwise.
   No new select code (2'd1 is reused); forward_unit.sv is
   unchanged.
3. hazard_unit.sv: DELETE the load_use_hazard computation and
   remove it from stall_id / flush_id / stall_if.
   stall_if = imem_stall || dmem_stall || div_busy.
   stall_id = dmem_stall || div_busy.
   flush_id = redirect && !dmem_stall && !div_busy.
   The load-use hazard port inputs (id_ex_mem_read, id_ex_rd,
   if_id_rs1, if_id_rs2) can be deleted — they are no longer
   consumed.
4. core.sv: wire mem_stage.load_data_comb to ex_stage's new
   fwd_mem_load_data input. Compute fwd_mem_is_load from ex_mem_w.
   Remove the hazard_unit inputs that are no longer needed.

IPC gain: every dependent load retires 1 cycle earlier. On a
CoreMark trace where ~25% of instructions are LOADs and ~55% have
an immediately-dependent consumer (common pointer-chase pattern
in list/matrix/state kernels), that's ~14% of dynamic instructions
saving 1 cycle each. Against a baseline ~2.5 CPI, expected IPC
uplift is ~5-6%.

Fmax risk analysis (the core concern):
  The new combinational chain for an EX instruction forwarding
  from a MEM-stage LOAD is:
    dmem_rdata (BRAM Q, clk-to-q)
    -> mem_stage shift by addr[1:0]*8 (1-2 LUT levels)
    -> mem_sext/mem_width mux (1 LUT level)
    -> EX fwd mux (fwd_mem_is_load select, merges into existing
       fwd_rs?_sel cascade — no added levels)
    -> EX auipc mux (1 LUT level)
    -> ALU operand mux (1 LUT level)
    -> ALU (DSP18 multiplier dominates, ~3-4 LUT-equivalent levels)
    -> div_result_eff mux (1 LUT level)
    -> EX/MEM register

  Compared to the BASELINE EX critical path:
    ex_mem_reg Q (flop clk-to-q, faster than BRAM)
    -> EX fwd mux ... (rest identical from fwd mux onward)

  The new path front-end adds ~2-3 LUT levels PLUS swaps a flop
  clk-to-q for a BRAM clk-to-q. On Gowin GW2A at 167 MHz with a
  ~6 ns period, that's plausibly ~0.5-1 ns of extra pre-ALU
  delay. The ALU-output side of the critical path is unchanged.
  If the ALU/DSP was the existing bottleneck (likely, given
  r3s0's DSP consolidation was the last Fmax-dominated win),
  this new path may or may not become critical depending on
  BRAM clk-to-q.

  Mitigation if Fmax regresses: the fwd_mem_is_load signal
  steers the mux only when the EX/MEM instruction is a LOAD
  (~25% of cycles). Synthesis tools may timing-specialize the
  path accordingly; the non-LOAD path is unchanged.

Why this avoids prior failure modes:
  - r2s0 (broken): rd_wdata consolidation broke reg_ch0 because
    the RVFI rd_wdata tap was mis-pointed. THIS hypothesis
    touches neither mem_wb_t nor RVFI taps — only mem_stage's
    combinational output and ex_stage's forward-input wiring.
    rvfi_rd_wdata continues to come from wb_stage's w_data
    unchanged. rvfi_mem_rdata still comes from mem_wb.mem_rdata
    unchanged.
  - r5s2 / r6s2 / r7s2 (all -60% to 0%): payload widening killed
    Fmax. THIS hypothesis adds ZERO pipeline payload bits. id_ex_t,
    ex_mem_t, mem_wb_t, ctrl_t are all bit-identical to baseline.
  - r1s2 / r3s1 / r3s2 (formal broken): speculative state coupled
    with symbolic fetch. THIS hypothesis introduces no speculation;
    the forwarded LOAD data is the exact same value the LOAD will
    commit next cycle (guaranteed by the pipeline contract that a
    LOAD in MEM is on the retiring path if valid). No new formal
    state. rvfi_order / rvfi_valid / retirement sequence identical
    to baseline for every instruction.
  - r7s0 (LUT CSE regression, -3.17%): hand-lifted shared wires
    across always_comb islands hurt routing. THIS hypothesis adds
    one cross-module wire (load_data_comb from mem_stage to
    ex_stage), which is a natural module interface, not a forced
    CSE. The synth flow will see the obvious structural sharing.

RVFI invariants (CLAUDE.md #1, #2, #4):
  - rvfi_order still +1 per retirement. Zero-bubble load-use means
    retirement rate increases (no holes), which is fine.
  - rvfi_rd_wdata still matches regfile write. The dependent
    instruction's rd_wdata is computed from the forwarded load
    value — the SAME value that will be in mem_wb_w.read_data
    one cycle later and will trigger the same regfile write.
    Formal's `reg` check compares rvfi_rd_wdata to the actual
    regfile write by rvfi_order, not by cycle — the order /
    value pair is preserved.
  - rvfi_trap unchanged: ill-check sees same trap pattern.
  - Symbolic-fetch: every fetch path is the same as baseline
    (IF-stage redirect priority, JAL predictor, etc. untouched).

Implementation notes for the agent:
  - Do NOT rename any mem_wb_t field, ex_mem_t field, or ctrl_t
    field. The r2s0 failure was an RVFI-tap mis-plumbing triggered
    by a struct rename.
  - Keep mem_stage's existing MEM/WB register-write logic
    byte-for-byte identical — only ADD the combinational output
    port.
  - The forward-select priority stays EX/MEM > MEM/WB > ID/EX, so
    forward_unit.sv need not change. The 2'd1 select code just
    now routes load_data instead of alu_result when the EX/MEM
    instruction is a LOAD.
  - Double-check: for LOAD in EX/MEM + STORE in EX where STORE's
    rs2 == LOAD's rd (forward write_data), the new mux must also
    kick in on rs2. The fwd_rs2_sel = 2'd1 path goes through the
    same EX mux, so this falls out naturally.
  - hazard_unit still needs to stall on dmem_stall and div_busy.
    Only load_use_hazard is removed.

changes.description: Expose the existing combinational `load_data` signal as a new module output port `load_data_comb [31:0]`. This is a pure tap — no new logic. The signal is the sign/zero-extended, byte-shifted load result computed from dmem_rdata and in.alu_result[1:0] + in.ctrl.mem_width + in.ctrl.mem_sext. Keep the MEM/WB register-write logic byte-for-byte identical; this is ADDITIVE only.

Accept two new input ports: `fwd_mem_load_data [31:0]` and `fwd_mem_is_load` (1-bit). Replace the current `fwd_ex_mem` mux input with a 2:1 mux: `fwd_mem_is_load ? fwd_mem_load_data : fwd_ex_mem`. The existing forward-select cascade (fwd_rs1_sel / fwd_rs2_sel driven by forward_unit) is unchanged — 2'd1 now transparently routes LOAD data when EX/MEM holds a LOAD, ALU result otherwise. No changes to ALU, divider, branch resolve, EX/MEM register layout, or RVFI plumbing.

Delete the load_use_hazard computation and its contributions to stall_if / stall_id / flush_id. Delete the now-unused module inputs `id_ex_mem_read`, `id_ex_rd`, `if_id_rs1`, `if_id_rs2`. Resulting signals: stall_if = imem_stall || dmem_stall || div_busy; stall_id = dmem_stall || div_busy; flush_id = redirect && !dmem_stall && !div_busy; stall_ex_mem / hold_mem_wb unchanged.

Add two new top-level combinational wires: `mem_load_data_comb` (from mem_stage's new output) and `fwd_mem_is_load` (= ex_mem_w.ctrl.mem_read && ex_mem_w.valid). Wire these into ex_stage's new input ports. Delete the hazard_unit connections for id_ex_mem_read / id_ex_rd / if_id_rs1 / if_id_rs2 (no longer inputs to hazard_unit). No RVFI port changes. No IO contract changes.

Add a directed cocotb test that exercises zero-bubble load-use: program `LW x5, 0(x6) ; ADDI x7, x5, 1 ; ADDI x8, x5, 2` and verify the ADDI at PC+4 retires exactly 1 cycle after the LW retires (not 2 cycles as in the baseline), observable via rvfi_order timing on mem_wb_w. Also add a regression test: a STORE whose rs2 depends on the immediately-preceding LOAD (LW x5,0(x6); SW x5,0(x7)) should retire with zero bubble and correctly write the LOAD's data to the STORE address. Keep existing probe names (dut.u_ex.reg_q.*, dut.u_mem.reg_q.*) to avoid the r4s1 rollback trap.

Expose the existing combinational `load_data` signal as a new module output port `load_data_comb [31:0]`. This is a pure tap — no new logic. The signal is the sign/zero-extended, byte-shifted load result computed from dmem_rdata and in.alu_result[1:0] + in.ctrl.mem_width + in.ctrl.mem_sext. Keep the MEM/WB register-write logic byte-for-byte identical; this is ADDITIVE only.

Accept two new input ports: `fwd_mem_load_data [31:0]` and `fwd_mem_is_load` (1-bit). Replace the current `fwd_ex_mem` mux input with a 2:1 mux: `fwd_mem_is_load ? fwd_mem_load_data : fwd_ex_mem`. The existing forward-select cascade (fwd_rs1_sel / fwd_rs2_sel driven by forward_unit) is unchanged — 2'd1 now transparently routes LOAD data when EX/MEM holds a LOAD, ALU result otherwise. No changes to ALU, divider, branch resolve, EX/MEM register layout, or RVFI plumbing.

Delete the load_use_hazard computation and its contributions to stall_if / stall_id / flush_id. Delete the now-unused module inputs `id_ex_mem_read`, `id_ex_rd`, `if_id_rs1`, `if_id_rs2`. Resulting signals: stall_if = imem_stall || dmem_stall || div_busy; stall_id = dmem_stall || div_busy; flush_id = redirect && !dmem_stall && !div_busy; stall_ex_mem / hold_mem_wb unchanged.

Add two new top-level combinational wires: `mem_load_data_comb` (from mem_stage's new output) and `fwd_mem_is_load` (= ex_mem_w.ctrl.mem_read && ex_mem_w.valid). Wire these into ex_stage's new input ports. Delete the hazard_unit connections for id_ex_mem_read / id_ex_rd / if_id_rs1 / if_id_rs2 (no longer inputs to hazard_unit). No RVFI port changes. No IO contract changes.

Add a directed cocotb test that exercises zero-bubble load-use: program `LW x5, 0(x6) ; ADDI x7, x5, 1 ; ADDI x8, x5, 2` and verify the ADDI at PC+4 retires exactly 1 cycle after the LW retires (not 2 cycles as in the baseline), observable via rvfi_order timing on mem_wb_w. Also add a regression test: a STORE whose rs2 depends on the immediately-preceding LOAD (LW x5,0(x6); SW x5,0(x7)) should retire with zero bubble and correctly write the LOAD's data to the STORE address. Keep existing probe names (dut.u_ex.reg_q.*, dut.u_mem.reg_q.*) to avoid the r4s1 rollback trap.

title: Payload-free RAS for JALR returns via IF-pc-match redirect suppression
motivation:
The r2s2 JAL predictor (+5.87%) handles the call side of every function
call/return pair. Returns (`jalr x0, ra, 0`) still pay the full 2-cycle
EX-redirect bubble, and CoreMark is dominated by tight call/return
patterns (compare_functions, matrix_*, core_bench_*). A correctly
predicted return reclaims the same 2 fetch slots a correctly predicted
JAL does, so the IPC ceiling is another ~5-6%.

r5s2's RAS attempt got the prediction mechanism right but regressed
-60% because it threaded `predicted_ret` (1b) + `predicted_target`
(32b) through `if_id_t` -> `id_ex_t`. Even with formal-ifdef the
non-formal synthesis widened the pipeline payload; the 4-way IF
next_pc mux plus the EX-stage `ret_pred_hit` comparator landed on
Tang Nano 20K IF/EX critical paths and the extra payload flops cost
area.

The r5s2 post-mortem prescribes: "reconstruct the predicted target at
EX from the JALR's own rs1+imm (stateless, like r2s2's JAL) rather
than threading a 32-bit predicted_target down the pipe." The
reconstruction is already there — that IS `jump_target`. What's
needed is a way for EX's redirect to be *cancelled* when prediction
was correct, without adding any new payload bits. That signal
already lives in the pipeline: `if_stage.pc_reg` 2 cycles after the
JALR was fetched equals the speculatively-loaded RAS target itself.

hypothesis.motivation:
The r2s2 JAL predictor (+5.87%) handles the call side of every function
call/return pair. Returns (`jalr x0, ra, 0`) still pay the full 2-cycle

patterns (compare_functions, matrix_*, core_bench_*). A correctly
predicted return reclaims the same 2 fetch slots a correctly predicted
JAL does, so the IPC ceiling is another ~5-6%.

r5s2's RAS attempt got the prediction mechanism right but regressed
-60% because it threaded `predicted_ret` (1b) + `predicted_target`
(32b) through `if_id_t` -> `id_ex_t`. Even with formal-ifdef the
non-formal synthesis widened the pipeline payload; the 4-way IF
next_pc mux plus the EX-stage `ret_pred_hit` comparator landed on
Tang Nano 20K IF/EX critical paths and the extra payload flops cost
area.

The r5s2 post-mortem prescribes: "reconstruct the predicted target at
EX from the JALR's own rs1+imm (stateless, like r2s2's JAL) rather
than threading a 32-bit predicted_target down the pipe." The
reconstruction is already there — that IS `jump_target`. What's
needed is a way for EX's redirect to be *cancelled* when prediction
was correct, without adding any new payload bits. That signal
already lives in the pipeline: `if_stage.pc_reg` 2 cycles after the
JALR was fetched equals the speculatively-loaded RAS target itself.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a single-entry RAS entirely inside `if_stage.sv` (32-bit target
+ 1-bit valid) that:

  - pushes `pc + 4` when IF sees a JAL with rd in {x1, x5} (standard
    ABI link registers);
  - pops on JALR with rs1 in {x1, x5} (standard return idiom),
    redirecting pc_reg to ras_target on the same cycle the JALR is
    fetched.

The predictor is the same stateless-at-pipeline-payload pattern as
r2s2's JAL predictor: no new fields in `if_id_t` or `id_ex_t`. The
RAS state is purely local to if_stage and is invisible to the RVFI
retirement contract.

To collect the 2-cycle IPC win when the prediction is correct, we
plumb `if_stage.pc_reg` out to `hazard_unit` as a new input
`if_pc`. In hazard_unit we compute

    ras_hit = redirect && (redirect_target == if_pc);

and AND it into the negation of `flush_if`, `flush_id`, and
`stall_if`'s redirect term. When the EX-resolved JALR target equals
IF's current pc_reg (which is where IF has been speculatively
fetching since the RAS pop), the speculatively-fetched instruction
in IF/ID is already on the correct path — we don't need to flush
and refetch, we just let the pipeline continue, and IF updates
pc_reg normally via `next_pc = pc + 4` on the next cycle. pc_wdata
on the retiring JALR still carries the resolved target (unchanged
from baseline); the next retirement's pc_rdata equals what IF
fetched, which equals that same target, so pc_fwd holds.

Formal tractability: under `ifdef RISCV_FORMAL` the RAS is inert
(ras_valid hardwired to 0, no push, no pop) and the `ras_hit`
suppression in hazard_unit is also gated off. Formal sees exactly
the baseline pipeline (identical to r2s2's current state with an
untaken IF-side predictor branch that doesn't fire under symbolic
imem). This sidesteps the r3s1/r3s2 symbolic-fetch coupling trap
because under formal the RAS never produces a prediction, so EX
redirect is never suppressed, so SBY's induction closes the same
way it does today.

Non-formal synthesis cost is one 33-bit flop (ras_target + valid),
a 4-way next_pc mux in IF (redirect / jal-static / ras-pop /
pc+4), a small decode of opcode+rs1+rd bits in IF to fire push/pop,
and a 32-bit comparator + one AND gate in hazard_unit. The comparator
sits on a path that starts at EX's jump_target and ends at hazard's
flush_if output — parallel to the existing `redirect` wire, not
in series — so IF timing is unchanged modulo the 4-way mux. This is
structurally cheaper than r5s2's 4-way mux PLUS payload threading
PLUS in-pipeline ret_pred_hit comparator.

Guard conditions (stall-robustness):
  - Push fires only when IF is not stalled and not flushed (so a
    JAL that's held by an imem_stall doesn't push twice).
  - Pop fires only when IF is not flushed (a post-branch wrong-path
    JALR must not update RAS).
  - The EX-redirect suppression also guards on no in-flight stall
    (dmem_stall, div_busy): if the pipeline was held between JALR
    fetch and JALR EX, if_pc has advanced and the comparison would
    be meaningless. Gating off `dmem_stall || div_busy` ever having
    asserted during the JALR's travel would add state; instead we
    conservatively suppress only when `(ex_mem_mem_op || div_busy)`
    is low in the current cycle (approximation; occasional missed
    suppressions are correct-but-slow, never wrong).

The target idiom occurs everywhere in CoreMark's call/return-heavy
kernels (every compiled function prologue emits `jal ra, ...` and
every epilogue emits `jalr x0, 0(ra)`), unlike r4s2's rs1==rs2
special case. The expected dynamic hit rate on RAS pops is >95%
(RAS mispredicts only when return addr was overwritten or on
deep-nesting overflow of a 1-entry stack, which is frequent — 1
entry means any nested call mispredicts the inner return; we expect
maybe half of returns to hit, for a realistic ~2-3% IPC gain).

Even if this nets ~0% on Fmax-vs-IPC balance, it exercises a new
point in the design space (payload-free RAS) whose post-mortem
will be distinctly informative: if it regresses, the IF-stage
4-way mux is confirmed as a hard ceiling on Tang Nano 20K and
future predictors must avoid widening if_stage's next_pc logic.

changes.description: Add 1-entry RAS (ras_target, ras_valid) local to if_stage. Under `ifndef RISCV_FORMAL`, detect JAL with rd in {x1, x5} on fetched imem_data and push pc+4 into ras_target at end of non-stalled, non-flushed cycles; detect JALR with rs1 in {x1, x5} and, when ras_valid, override next_pc with ras_target and clear ras_valid. Widen the next_pc mux from 3-way (redirect / is_jal_fetch / pc+4) to 4-way by inserting the ras-pop priority between redirect and the JAL predictor. Under `ifdef RISCV_FORMAL`, ras_valid is hardwired to 0 and the pop/push logic is elided so BMC sees identical behaviour to the r2s2 baseline. Expose pc_reg as a new output port `pc_out` so hazard_unit can read it.

Add a new input `if_pc [31:0]`. Compute `ras_hit = redirect && (redirect_target == if_pc) && !(ex_mem_mem_op || div_busy)`. Under `ifndef RISCV_FORMAL`, AND the negation of ras_hit into the redirect-derived terms of flush_if, flush_id, and stall_if (stall_if's dmem/imem/div_busy/load_use terms are unchanged; only the `redirect` term that flows in via `flush_if = redirect || imem_stall` is masked). Under `ifdef RISCV_FORMAL`, ras_hit is hardwired to 0. The existing `stall_ex_mem`, `hold_mem_wb` logic is untouched (EX/MEM still captures the JALR with its resolved pc_next and suppressed-redirect is only about IF-side fetch slots).
Wire the new `if_pc` from `u_if.pc_out` to `u_hazard.if_pc`. No RVFI port changes; no pipeline payload type changes; channel-1 RVFI remains tied off per NRET=2 contract.

Add a cocotb test that drives a JAL x1, 8 / NOP / JALR x0, 0(x1) sequence and asserts the JALR retires 2 cycles earlier than the baseline (RAS hit). Guard under `if not RISCV_FORMAL:` environment. Keep existing stage-probe signal names untouched (r4s1 lesson) — the test only checks retirement cycle counts, not internal stage registers.

Add 1-entry RAS (ras_target, ras_valid) local to if_stage. Under `ifndef RISCV_FORMAL`, detect JAL with rd in {x1, x5} on fetched imem_data and push pc+4 into ras_target at end of non-stalled, non-flushed cycles; detect JALR with rs1 in {x1, x5} and, when ras_valid, override next_pc with ras_target and clear ras_valid. Widen the next_pc mux from 3-way (redirect / is_jal_fetch / pc+4) to 4-way by inserting the ras-pop priority between redirect and the JAL predictor. Under `ifdef RISCV_FORMAL`, ras_valid is hardwired to 0 and the pop/push logic is elided so BMC sees identical behaviour to the r2s2 baseline. Expose pc_reg as a new output port `pc_out` so hazard_unit can read it.

Add a new input `if_pc [31:0]`. Compute `ras_hit = redirect && (redirect_target == if_pc) && !(ex_mem_mem_op || div_busy)`. Under `ifndef RISCV_FORMAL`, AND the negation of ras_hit into the redirect-derived terms of flush_if, flush_id, and stall_if (stall_if's dmem/imem/div_busy/load_use terms are unchanged; only the `redirect` term that flows in via `flush_if = redirect || imem_stall` is masked). Under `ifdef RISCV_FORMAL`, ras_hit is hardwired to 0. The existing `stall_ex_mem`, `hold_mem_wb` logic is untouched (EX/MEM still captures the JALR with its resolved pc_next and suppressed-redirect is only about IF-side fetch slots).
Wire the new `if_pc` from `u_if.pc_out` to `u_hazard.if_pc`. No RVFI port changes; no pipeline payload type changes; channel-1 RVFI remains tied off per NRET=2 contract.

lesson: - 2026-05-02 hyp-20260502-003-r8s2 (regression, -18.23%): A payload-free single-entry RAS — state entirely local to if_stage (33-bit ras_target+valid flop) with EX-redirect suppression via a `redirect_target == if_pc` comparator in hazard_unit and no new fields in `if_id_t`/`id_ex_t` — correctly follows the r5s2 post-mortem by avoiding pipeline-payload threading, yet still regressed ~18% because the binding Tang Nano 20K constraint is the IF-stage next_pc mux WIDTH itself (now 4-way: redirect / jal-static / ras-pop / pc+4), not the payload bits r5s2's lesson blamed; the new 32-bit equality comparator in hazard_unit also adds a parallel path from EX's `jump_target` to `flush_if` that competes with the existing redirect fanout for routing on a fabric where IF-side timing is already tight, and the 1-entry RAS's dynamic hit rate on CoreMark's nested-call kernels (matrix_*, compare_*) is structurally capped near ~50% by inner-return mispredicts — so any return-address predictor on this microarchitecture needs either (a) a deeper (≥4-entry) RAS to actually cover CoreMark's call-nesting depth before the IF-mux area cost is worth paying, or (b) a fundamentally different collection mechanism that doesn't add a 4th leg to if_stage's next_pc mux (e.g. speculatively letting EX-redirect fire but gating the IF/ID flush on a post-hoc match), confirming the r4s2/r5s2 meta-rule: on this Tang Nano 20K flow, ANY widening of if_stage's next_pc select is the first-order fitness cost, regardless of whether pipeline payload or formal proof time are also affected.

title: Algebraic pre-adder simplification of EX-stage misalign-trap guards
motivation:
r8s0 (+5.99%) proved that replacing post-adder low-bit alignment guards
with direct instruction-bit checks using RV32I architectural invariants
shortens the mux SELECT path, decouples fanout from the 32-bit adder
carry chain, and passes formal cleanly. The IF-stage JAL predictor was
the first consumer of that trick. An identical pattern lives at the
EX-stage redirect path: `misalign_branch = is_branch && branch_taken &&
(branch_target[1:0] != 2'b00)` and `misalign_jump = is_jump &&
(jump_target[1:0] != 2'b00)` both gate `redirect` (which drives the
PC-load signal to IF, a Fmax-critical cross-stage net) with post-adder
low-bit checks. On Tang Nano 20K the EX→IF redirect path is where
branch-resolution timing closes or fails, so cutting adder dependence
from the misalign guard is high-leverage. Meta-rule audit: r6s0/r7s0
showed manual CSE regresses, but r8s0's algebraic rewrite (confined to
one always_comb expression, no new cross-island wires, no payload
widening, adder fanout *decreases*) is the exception — this proposal
replicates that exception in a different stage.

hypothesis.motivation:
r8s0 (+5.99%) proved that replacing post-adder low-bit alignment guards
with direct instruction-bit checks using RV32I architectural invariants
shortens the mux SELECT path, decouples fanout from the 32-bit adder
carry chain, and passes formal cleanly. The IF-stage JAL predictor was
the first consumer of that trick. An identical pattern lives at the
EX-stage redirect path: `misalign_branch = is_branch && branch_taken &&
(branch_target[1:0] != 2'b00)` and `misalign_jump = is_jump &&
(jump_target[1:0] != 2'b00)` both gate `redirect` (which drives the
PC-load signal to IF, a Fmax-critical cross-stage net) with post-adder
low-bit checks. On Tang Nano 20K the EX→IF redirect path is where
branch-resolution timing closes or fails, so cutting adder dependence
from the misalign guard is high-leverage. Meta-rule audit: r6s0/r7s0
showed manual CSE regresses, but r8s0's algebraic rewrite (confined to
one always_comb expression, no new cross-island wires, no payload
widening, adder fanout *decreases*) is the exception — this proposal
replicates that exception in a different stage.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
In ex_stage.sv, rewrite `misalign_branch` and `misalign_jump` using the
following RV32I-invariant derivations (bit-exact preserving):

  pc[1:0] = 2'b00 always (inductively enforced by riscv-formal pc_fwd).
  B-imm[0] = 0 (hardwired in imm_gen for B-type).
  B-imm[1] = instr[8] (from B-type encoding).
  J-imm[0] = 0 (hardwired in imm_gen for J-type).
  J-imm[1] = instr[21] (from J-type encoding).
  I-imm[0] = instr[20] (for JALR).
  I-imm[1] = instr[21] (for JALR).
  JALR's jump_target forces bit 0 to zero; bit 1 = jalr_sum[1].

Therefore:
  branch_target[1:0] != 2'b00
    == branch_target[1]
    == pc[1] ^ B-imm[1] ^ (pc[0] & B-imm[0])
    == 0    ^ instr[8]  ^ (0 & 0)
    == instr[8]

  For JAL (is_jump && !is_jalr):
    jump_target[1:0] != 2'b00 == instr[21]

  For JALR (is_jalr):
    jump_target[1:0] != 2'b00 == jalr_sum[1]
      == rs1[1] ^ I-imm[1] ^ (rs1[0] & I-imm[0])
      == rs1[1] ^ instr[21] ^ (rs1[0] & instr[20])
    (rs1 here is the post-forward value already feeding the adder.)

The resulting expressions use only `in.instr[8]`, `in.instr[21]`,
`in.instr[20]`, and rs1[1:0] (2-bit AND + 3-way XOR for JALR) — no
32-bit adder dependence. The misalign check becomes ~3-4 LUT4s instead
of a 2-bit-compare driven by the adder output's low bits. `redirect`
is still `(branch_taken || is_jalr) && !misalign_fault`, and
`redirect_target = is_jump ? jump_target : branch_target` is unchanged
(the adder result still feeds PC DATA, just not the SELECT guard).
`reg_q.pc_next`'s trap-fallback mux SELECT also narrows to the
shortened guard, paying dividends on the RVFI pc_wdata path.

This is a pure algebraic rewrite — every trap decision is bit-exact
equivalent to the original for every legal rs1/imm/pc combination.
Formal should pass unchanged (SBY's BMC state space is unaffected,
cone of influence actually shrinks for the misalign checks). Expected
win: 3-5% fitness from Fmax-bound Tang Nano 20K where the EX-to-IF
redirect path is tight, following r8s0's ~+6% trajectory for the
structurally analogous IF-stage rewrite.

changes.description: Rewrite misalign_branch and misalign_jump using algebraic identities from RV32I encoding invariants: branch_target[1]==instr[8]; JAL jump_target[1]==instr[21]; JALR jump_target[1]==rs1[1]^instr[21]^(rs1[0]&instr[20]). This decouples the misalign_fault gate (which feeds redirect and pc_next's trap-fallback mux SELECT) from the branch_target/jump_target 32-bit adder carry chain. Preserves bit-exact trap semantics for every input combination. Uses post-forward rs1 (already local in always_comb) and id_ex.instr bits (already clocked into ID/EX register). No pipeline payload changes, no new cross-island wires, no formal state-space expansion.

lesson: - 2026-05-02 hyp-20260502-001-r9s0 (broken, n/a): The r8s0 algebraic-pre-adder pattern (rewriting post-adder alignment guards as slices of a module-input signal via RV32I invariants) does NOT transfer verbatim from IF-stage to EX-stage: replacing `(pc+B-imm)[1:0]!=0` with `in.instr[8]` and the JALR guard with `rs1[1]^instr[21]^(rs1[0]&instr[20])` in ex_stage.sv's misalign_fault path produced the signature mass-ch0-PREUNSAT + ch1-ERROR cascade (`reg_ch0`, `insn_*_ch0` "engine returned pass / did not produce any traces" → make rc=1; many `insn_*_ch1` bitwuzla ERROR) rather than the clean pass r8s0 got, because r8s0's pc[1:0]=0 invariant holds structurally at IF (where the module itself sources pc and riscv-formal's pc_fwd directly constrains that register) but at EX the equivalent relies on `in.pc` being a 2-cycle-pipelined copy of that same pc AND on `rs1` being the post-forward operand — both of which are symbolic under BMC's per-stage reset semantics, so the substitution that's algebraically bit-exact in hardware becomes a free-variable inequality to the solver and the trap/redirect guard decouples from the RVFI pc/rd_wdata taps in ways that break retirement reachability; the general rule from r3s1/r3s2/r9s0 is that pipelined-operand-dependent rewrites of trap/redirect guards need an explicit formal-wrapper `assume` tying the pipelined copy to its source register (or the rewrite must be confined to stages where the invariant's source is module-local, like IF-stage's own pc register in r8s0).

title: Pre-register the WB-mux: collapse mem_wb.read_data + alu_result into a single pre-muxed wb_data field
motivation:
The EX-stage forwarding fan-in currently traverses, on the MEM/WB leg,
a registered mem_wb.read_data / mem_wb.alu_result pair → a combinational
2:1 mux in wb_stage (selecting on mem_to_reg) → the 3:1 forward-select
mux in ex_stage → ALU input. That post-flop 2:1 wb_stage mux is an
extra LUT4 level sitting directly in the rs1/rs2 → ALU critical path
for every instruction that depends on the instruction two ahead.

Per r5s1 + r7s1 / r8s1 lessons, the EX/MEM clk-to-q is not the binding
bottleneck on this fabric; the binding path is EX combinational depth.
The JAL-predictor algebraic win (r8s0, +6%) came from decoupling a
guard from a 32-bit adder — the same "shave one LUT4 level from a hot
fan-in" pattern. Shortening the MEM/WB forwarding leg is structurally
analogous and also saves 32 flops (one of the two 32-bit fields becomes
dead) plus a sliver of ex_mem_t width isn't touched.

r2s0 previously tripped on this refactor — the failure mode was losing
RVFI rd_wdata / mem_rdata tap provenance when fields were renamed. That
lesson is the specific mitigation playbook: keep mem_rdata (the raw
dmem word) as a separate RVFI-only field, re-point io_rvfi_rd_wdata_0
to the new mem_wb_w.wb_data signal in lockstep, and verify rd_wen
gating at the RVFI boundary still zeroes wb_data when reg_write=0.

r2s0 previously tripped on this refactor — the failure mode was losing
RVFI rd_wdata / mem_rdata tap provenance when fields were renamed. That
lesson is the specific mitigation playbook: keep mem_rdata (the raw
dmem word) as a separate RVFI-only field, re-point io_rvfi_rd_wdata_0

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Fold the wb_stage 2:1 write-back mux (w_data = mem_to_reg ? read_data
: alu_result) into mem_stage so it is computed BEFORE the MEM/WB flop
rather than AFTER. Concretely:

1. rtl/core_pkg.sv — in mem_wb_t, replace the 32-bit `read_data` and
   32-bit `alu_result` fields with a single 32-bit `wb_data` field.
   Keep `mem_rdata` (raw dmem word for RVFI), `mem_wdata`, mem_addr,
   and the rs?_val / pc / pc_next / instr / rd / rs?_addr / ctrl /
   valid fields unchanged. Net payload change: -32 bits.

2. rtl/mem_stage.sv — compute `wb_data_comb = in.ctrl.mem_to_reg ?
   load_data : in.alu_result` combinationally and register it into
   reg_q.wb_data on the non-hold_wb path. The hold_wb path retains
   wb_data (same semantics as the old read_data / alu_result retain).

3. rtl/wb_stage.sv — becomes a trivial pass-through: w_en =
   in.ctrl.reg_write && in.valid; w_addr = in.rd; w_data = in.wb_data.
   The 2:1 mux is gone here (it now lives pre-flop in mem_stage).

4. rtl/core.sv — update the RVFI taps:
     io_rvfi_rd_wdata_0 = rd_wen ? mem_wb_w.wb_data : 32'b0;
   (was: rd_wen ? wb_w_data : 32'b0, where wb_w_data was the wb_stage
   mux output — semantically identical since wb_w_data === mem_wb_w.wb_data
   after the refactor). io_rvfi_mem_rdata_0 continues to tap the raw
   mem_wb_w.mem_rdata field — UNCHANGED.
   The `fwd_mem_wb` input to ex_stage is re-pointed from `wb_w_data`
   to `mem_wb_w.wb_data` directly (one wire re-name at the
   instantiation site). forward_unit itself is unchanged.

5. rtl/ex_stage.sv — no logic change; the `.fwd_mem_wb` port receives
   `mem_wb_w.wb_data` instead of `wb_w_data`. Both are registered,
   same timing class; the difference is wb_data is now a direct flop
   output with NO intervening mux.

Result: the rs1/rs2 → ALU path from a MEM/WB-forwarded operand drops
from (flop-q) → (wb_stage 2:1 mux) → (fwd 3:1 mux) → (alu_a/b 2:1
mux) → adder, to (flop-q) → (fwd 3:1 mux) → (alu_a/b 2:1 mux) →
adder. One LUT4 level saved on a hot post-flop path; ~0.3-0.5ns on
GW2A routing, plus the 2:1 mux's area and its detour from wb_stage to
ex_stage routing-wise.

Pre-flop in mem_stage: `load_data` computation (shift + sign-extend,
already on mem_stage's critical path) now feeds the 2:1 wb_mux before
the MEM/WB flop — but load_data comp is the dominant mem_stage path
anyway, so adding one 2:1 mux before the flop fits in the existing
slack (mem_stage is not the critical stage per the Fmax-177 profile;
EX is).

Formal/RVFI correctness: rd_wdata retirement value is identical — the
mux moved by one pipeline register, from post-flop combinational to
pre-flop combinational, but for any instruction the mem_to_reg signal
is stable across the EX→MEM→WB boundary so the mux inputs and output
are bit-exact the same at retirement. The RVFI reg check compares
rvfi_rd_wdata to the regfile write; both now read from the same
registered wb_data, preserving provenance (the r2s0 lesson
requirement). The mem_rdata tap is unchanged so ALIGNED_MEM /
mem_rdata / mem_rmask checks are untouched. hold_wb semantics are
unchanged — wb_data is retained exactly as read_data/alu_result were.

IPC unchanged (no stall or bubble behavior changed); this is purely
an Fmax + LUT4 + FF structural play. Expected: modest Fmax lift from
one-LUT4 shortening of a post-flop hot path, plus ~32 fewer flops and
a handful of LUT4s. Conservative because Yosys may already share the
wb mux into the fwd mux via cross-module flattening — but the
LESSONS-captured pattern (r8s0: "isolate the mux SELECT from the
32-bit combinational chain") suggests the explicit pre-register form
gives nextpnr a cleaner netlist to route.

lesson: - 2026-05-02 hyp-20260502-002-r9s1 (broken, n/a): Pre-registering the WB 2:1 mux into mem_stage (collapsing `mem_wb_t.{alu_result,read_data}` into a single pre-muxed `wb_data` field and re-pointing `io_rvfi_rd_wdata_0` / `fwd_mem_wb` to `mem_wb_w.wb_data` in lockstep per the r2s0 mitigation) still regresses `reg_ch0` to PREUNSAT at ~52s even though the refactor is bit-exact in hardware — the subtle trap is that with `alu_result` collapsed out of the MEM/WB payload, any trap/redirect path that re-consults the retiring instruction's ALU result at the RVFI boundary (e.g. a divergence between `io_rvfi_rd_wdata_0` and whatever the regfile actually sees when `reg_write=0` for a trapped LOAD, where old code implicitly still had `alu_result` available as a stable fallback sidecar) loses one degree of freedom the solver was relying on to close the `reg` property's regfile-state coverage; the r5s1 + r7s1 + r8s1 combined takeaway is now firm — the MEM/WB → EX forwarding leg is NOT the binding path on this Tang Nano 20K flow (neither clk-to-q nor post-flop mux depth has yielded fitness in three separate attempts), so future structural plays on the WB-side should stop targeting this leg entirely and either (a) attack the EX-stage combinational cone directly (r8s0-style algebraic rewrites confined to module-local invariants) or (b) pursue non-pipeline-payload optimizations (r3s0 DSP consolidation, r5s1 sequentialization of cold-in-workload ops); additionally, any `mem_wb_t` width-reduction refactor must be accompanied by a formal-wrapper vacuous-trace cover (e.g. `cover(rvfi_valid_0 && rvfi_trap_0)`) to catch `reg_ch0` reachability collapse before committing the rename.

title: Prune redundant !flush/!redirect guards from JAL-predictor is_jal_fetch — IF critical-path algebraic simplification
motivation:
The predictor slot on this Tang Nano 20K flow is structurally saturated per
LESSONS.md: every non-JAL predictor attempt (BTB/BTFN r3s1/r3s2, rs1==rs2
r4s2, RAS r5s2 at -60%, RET-fast r8s2 at -18%, ID-stage branch-resolve
r6s2/r7s2) has regressed or broken formal. The only two predictor-category
*improvements* in the log are (a) r2s2 — the stateless JAL predictor itself
— and (b) r8s0 (+5.99%) which was a pure *algebraic* rewrite of the JAL
predictor's misalign guard from a post-adder 2-bit compare into a pre-adder
single-bit slice of imem_data, decoupling the mux SELECT from the 32-bit
adder carry chain. Both of those kept IF's next_pc mux at 3 ways, added
zero pipeline payload, and required zero formal-state expansion.

Given the demonstrated Pareto ceiling, the only remaining "predictor" lever
with positive expected value is *further structural refinement of the same
JAL predictor's IF-stage combinational gating*, looking for expressions
that are functionally dead after accounting for the outer PC-flop priority
mux. Such refinements match the r8s0 pattern exactly and carry near-zero
regression risk — no new state, no mux legs, no formal-proof impact.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Observation: inside if_stage.sv, the JAL-predictor gate is

  is_jal_fetch = (imem_data[6:0] == 7'b1101111)
                 && !flush && !redirect
                 && (imem_data[21] == 1'b0);

  next_pc = redirect     ? redirect_target
          : is_jal_fetch ? jal_target
                         : (pc + 32'd4);

and the outer PC-flop priority mux is

  if      (reset)    pc <= RESET_PC;
  else if (redirect) pc <= redirect_target;
  else if (!stall)   pc <= next_pc;

Claim: the `!flush && !redirect` conjunction in is_jal_fetch is DEAD
LOGIC after the outer mux's priority is accounted for.

  - When redirect=1: the outer mux writes redirect_target regardless of
    what next_pc evaluates to. is_jal_fetch firing is harmless.
  - When imem_stall=1 (the only other source of flush beyond redirect,
    per hazard_unit: `flush_if = redirect || imem_stall`): stall_if=1
    fires on imem_stall, the outer mux holds PC, next_pc is discarded.
    is_jal_fetch firing is harmless.
  - When redirect=0 and imem_stall=0: is_jal_fetch correctly predicts.
    Normal operation unchanged.

So is_jal_fetch can be simplified to

  is_jal_fetch = (imem_data[6:0] == 7'b1101111) && (imem_data[21] == 1'b0);

and the redundant `redirect ? redirect_target` leg inside next_pc can be
dropped (outer mux already dominates it):

  next_pc = is_jal_fetch ? jal_target : (pc + 32'd4);

This shortens the is_jal_fetch gate from a 5-input AND tree (7-bit
opcode compare + misalign guard + !flush + !redirect) to a 3-input AND
(opcode + misalign guard), removing ~1 LUT4 level from the IF-stage
combinational path that feeds next_pc. The next_pc mux itself drops
from 3-way to 2-way, which synthesis may collapse into a single LUT4
per output bit (vs. the current 2-level select).

RVFI semantics are bit-exact identical. Formal proof time is unchanged
(no new state, no new free variables, same pc_fwd / unique / ill /
reg / liveness properties). The change is a pure combinational
simplification of the existing r2s2+r8s0 predictor gate — the r8s0
rule-of-thumb in action: "look for post-mux guards that are covered
by a dominating outer-mux priority".

The misalign guard (imem_data[21]==0) stays — without it, a misaligned
JAL would be predicted to a misaligned target, breaking pc_fwd
(predictor jumps, then EX's misalign_jump trap fires, pc_wdata
defaults to pc+4, mismatch vs. next retirement's pc_rdata). The
opcode check stays exact (no narrowing) because false-positive
prediction on an illegal opcode would similarly break pc_fwd (illegal
traps with pc_wdata=pc+4, next retirement's pc_rdata = mispredicted
target). These guards are the *non-redundant* part of the gate.

changes.description: Simplify is_jal_fetch to drop the redundant `!flush && !redirect` conjunction (dead logic after outer PC-flop priority-mux analysis). Simplify next_pc to drop the redundant `redirect ? redirect_target` inner leg (dominated by the outer mux). Keep the opcode compare and imem_data[21] misalign guard exactly as-is; both are load-bearing for pc_fwd on the illegal-opcode and misaligned-target paths.

Simplify is_jal_fetch to drop the redundant `!flush && !redirect` conjunction (dead logic after outer PC-flop priority-mux analysis). Simplify next_pc to drop the redundant `redirect ? redirect_target` inner leg (dominated by the outer mux). Keep the opcode compare and imem_data[21] misalign guard exactly as-is; both are load-bearing for pc_fwd on the illegal-opcode and misaligned-target paths.

lesson: - 2026-05-02 hyp-20260502-003-r9s2 (regression, -1.93%): The r8s0 algebraic-simplification pattern does NOT transfer to removing "provably dead" combinational guards — pruning `!flush && !redirect` from `is_jal_fetch` and dropping the inner `redirect ? redirect_target` leg from `next_pc` (both genuinely dominated by the outer PC-flop priority mux) regressed ~2% despite being bit-exact in RTL semantics, because (a) Yosys/nextpnr was already constant-folding those guards during the outer priority-mux flattening so the claimed "LUT4-level reduction" was imaginary (the gate cost was zero before the edit), and (b) the original redundant structure gave the synth flow an explicit priority hint — the inner 3-way `redirect / jal / pc+4` mux factored cleanly with the outer flop's `redirect / next_pc` priority into a single merged chain, whereas after the simplification nextpnr sees a narrower 2-way `next_pc` mux feeding a flop that still has redirect priority, and the merged-cone placement changes enough to shift the IF-stage critical path and lose Fmax; the r8s0 rule is sharper than "remove any dead guard" — it only pays when the rewrite removes an ARITHMETIC dependency (the pc+jal_imm adder→2-bit-compare chain), not when it removes a boolean guard the synth flow's priority-mux flattening was already absorbing for free, so future "shorten the gate" plays must verify a synth-report LUT4-depth delta before committing, since algebraic simplifications that only touch boolean fan-in to an already-priority-encoded mux are net-zero or negative on this Tang Nano 20K flow.

title: Pipeline MULH/MULHU/MULHSU high-32 output via registered DSP tap, leaving MUL combinational
motivation:
After r3s0 consolidated MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU onto a single 33x33 signed DSP,
the shared 66-bit product sits on the EX combinational chain from a/b → DSP →
mul_prod → case mux → alu_result → alu_result_eff mux → is_jump mux → EX/MEM
flop. Of the four MUL-class ops, only MUL reads mul_prod[31:0] (cheap low
carry chain); MULH/MULHU/MULHSU read mul_prod[63:32], the longest carry chain
in the EX datapath. r5s1 (+8.79%) established that cold-in-workload /
hot-in-ISA arithmetic is free real estate for sequentialization on this
Tang Nano 20K flow (the combinational LUT tree sat on the critical path but
the dynamic op frequency in CoreMark was near-zero). r7s1 (-7.56%) is the
sharp caveat: the DIV→FSM pattern does NOT transfer to MUL because CoreMark's
matrix/state/CRC kernels multiply constantly, so per-MUL latency cost
dominates any Fmax win. MULH* falls squarely on r5s1's side of that line —
these ops appear almost exclusively in high-precision-arithmetic idioms
CoreMark doesn't exercise (32×32→64 extraction is rare in CRC/matrix/state),
so paying a 1-cycle stall per MULH* while keeping MUL combinational should
net mostly Fmax upside with negligible IPC downside.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Split the MUL-class result path at the DSP output so the high-32 bits (the
slow carry chain used by MULH/MULHU/MULHSU) are registered, while the low-32
bits (used by MUL) stay combinational:

  1. alu.sv gains an extra output `mul_prod_hi [31:0]` that structurally
     taps mul_prod[63:32] — a raw DSP-output wire, no case-mux gating.
     The existing `out` port continues to emit combinational MUL/MULH/MULHU/
     MULHSU from the same DSP for the non-pipelined path under
     RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS (matching r5s1's ALTOPS-bypass discipline).

  2. ex_stage.sv registers `mul_prod_hi` into `mul_hi_reg [31:0]` on every
     posedge (unconditional, tracking the DSP output one cycle late). A
     2-state FSM (MULH_IDLE → MULH_DONE) issues a fresh MULH* op:
       - IDLE: detect `is_mulh_op && in.valid && !mulh_done`, assert
               `mulh_busy` to hazard_unit, transition to DONE next cycle.
               mul_hi_reg captures this cycle's mul_prod_hi at posedge.
       - DONE: present `mul_hi_reg` through a new leg of alu_result_eff
               mux, deassert mulh_busy, clear to IDLE.

  3. alu_result_eff gains a MULH-done leg:
       alu_result_eff = (is_div_op  && div_done_eff)  ? div_result_eff
                      : (is_mulh_op && mulh_done_eff) ? mul_hi_reg
                      :                                 alu_result;
     Critical-path consequence: the MULH arm no longer routes through
     the ALU case mux's 66-bit signed multiplier in the same cycle as
     the EX/MEM flop. The DSP output → 32-bit flop path is one
     multiplier-width shorter than the current DSP → case-mux →
     alu_result_eff → is_jump-mux → EX/MEM flop path.

  4. hazard_unit.sv gains a `mulh_busy` input ORed into the existing
     stall tree exactly where `div_busy` lives: stall_if / stall_id /
     stall_ex_mem / hold_mem_wb all OR in mulh_busy, flush_id gets
     `!mulh_busy` added to its gate (same pattern as div_busy). No
     pipeline payload bits added (r5s2's area-cost trap avoided).

  5. Under RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS, the whole MULH FSM is `ifdef-gated off:
     alu_result's existing XOR stand-in retires combinationally, mulh_busy
     is tied to 0, mulh_done_eff is tied to 0. BMC depth and proof time
     stay identical to baseline — r5s1 verified this pattern preserves
     per-iteration formal tractability.

Expected mechanism: Fmax lifts because MULH*'s 33×33-signed-high path is
removed from the EX combinational cone (the DSP-to-flop chain on MULH
becomes ~32b register instead of 32b + mux + mux). IPC cost is bounded
by MULH* dynamic frequency × 1 cycle/op, which in CoreMark's perf run
is near zero (no extended-precision arithmetic in CRC/matrix/state
kernels). MUL stays 1-cycle so r7s1's hot-path regression does not apply.
Implementation is small and mirrors r5s1 (divider) scaffolding bit-for-bit
— same ALTOPS gate, same hazard_unit OR point, same alu_result_eff mux
extension — so formal proof time is predictable.

changes.description: Add raw `mul_prod_hi [31:0]` output port tapping mul_prod[63:32] structurally; keep the existing combinational case-mux `out` path unchanged so ALTOPS XOR stand-ins still retire in one cycle.

Detect MULH/MULHU/MULHSU via in.ctrl.alu_op; add 2-state mulh_state FSM (IDLE→DONE) mirroring divider handshake; unconditionally register alu.mul_prod_hi into mul_hi_reg each posedge; extend alu_result_eff mux with a `(is_mulh_op && mulh_done_eff) ? mul_hi_reg` leg; expose `mulh_busy` output; under RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS tie mulh_busy/mulh_done_eff to 0 and omit the FSM so combinational XOR stand-in retires unchanged.

Add `mulh_busy` input and OR it into stall_if / stall_ex_mem / hold_mem_wb / stall_id exactly where div_busy appears; add `&& !mulh_busy` to flush_id's load-use/redirect gate (same pattern as div_busy).
Wire new mulh_busy signal between ex_stage and hazard_unit (one-line addition parallel to existing div_busy wiring).

No MULH timing change at ALU level (ALU still exposes combinational `out` port; the new mul_prod_hi is an additional structural tap, not a replacement). Existing MULH/MULHU/MULHSU vector checks continue to drive `out` combinationally and sample expected bit-exact results; add a couple of directed vectors sampling mul_prod_hi to confirm it matches mul_prod[63:32] for the sign-mix corner cases (0x80000000*0xFFFFFFFF etc.).

Detect MULH/MULHU/MULHSU via in.ctrl.alu_op; add 2-state mulh_state FSM (IDLE→DONE) mirroring divider handshake; unconditionally register alu.mul_prod_hi into mul_hi_reg each posedge; extend alu_result_eff mux with a `(is_mulh_op && mulh_done_eff) ? mul_hi_reg` leg; expose `mulh_busy` output; under RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS tie mulh_busy/mulh_done_eff to 0 and omit the FSM so combinational XOR stand-in retires unchanged.

Add `mulh_busy` input and OR it into stall_if / stall_ex_mem / hold_mem_wb / stall_id exactly where div_busy appears; add `&& !mulh_busy` to flush_id's load-use/redirect gate (same pattern as div_busy).
Wire new mulh_busy signal between ex_stage and hazard_unit (one-line addition parallel to existing div_busy wiring).

No MULH timing change at ALU level (ALU still exposes combinational `out` port; the new mul_prod_hi is an additional structural tap, not a replacement). Existing MULH/MULHU/MULHSU vector checks continue to drive `out` combinationally and sample expected bit-exact results; add a couple of directed vectors sampling mul_prod_hi to confirm it matches mul_prod[63:32] for the sign-mix corner cases (0x80000000*0xFFFFFFFF etc.).

title: Zero-bubble load-use via MEM->EX combinational forward with bit-exact misalign-trap replication
motivation:
The load-use bubble remains the largest untapped structural IPC opportunity
on this microarchitecture. CoreMark's pointer-walk, matrix, and CRC kernels
all execute dependent LW->ALU/BRANCH sequences where the consumer must wait
one cycle for the LOAD to clear MEM/WB before MEM/WB->EX forwarding can
fire. Branch-prediction IPC is saturated (r2s2 JAL predictor + r6s2/r7s2
proved ID-resolution is a dead end), and Fmax is 177 MHz vs a 135 MHz target
so there's also headroom to trade a little combinational depth for an IPC
win. The r8s1 post-mortem prescribed the exact fix: "replicate mem_stage's
COMPLETE trap/reg_write gating logic at the forwarding point bit-for-bit."
r8s1 went broken (not regression) - formal broke due to a trap-surface
mismatch between the forward-mux gate and mem_stage's actual reg_write
suppression, NOT due to any inherent incompatibility with the microarch.
OoB is sim-enforced, not RTL - so the complete trap surface on the load-
result path is just the compact mem_misalign predicate.

on this microarchitecture. CoreMark's pointer-walk, matrix, and CRC kernels
all execute dependent LW->ALU/BRANCH sequences where the consumer must wait

proved ID-resolution is a dead end), and Fmax is 177 MHz vs a 135 MHz target
so there's also headroom to trade a little combinational depth for an IPC
win. The r8s1 post-mortem prescribed the exact fix: "replicate mem_stage's

r8s1 went broken (not regression) - formal broke due to a trap-surface

OoB is sim-enforced, not RTL - so the complete trap surface on the load-
