hypothesis.hypothesis:
Eliminate the load-use pipeline bubble by adding a fourth combinational
forwarding source in forward_unit sourced from mem_stage's pre-flop
load_data path, and remove the load-use stall from hazard_unit.

Concretely:

(1) mem_stage exposes two new module outputs:
    - load_data_comb (32b): the sign/zero-extended load result, identical
      formula to the one that currently flops into mem_wb.read_data.
    - load_fwd_ok (1b): asserts when the LOAD in EX/MEM will actually
      write its rd this cycle's next edge. Bit-exact to the mem_stage
      reg_write gate:
        load_fwd_ok = in.valid
                   && in.ctrl.mem_read
                   && in.ctrl.reg_write
                   && (in.rd != 5'b0)
                   && !mem_misalign
      where mem_misalign uses the identical expression already in
      mem_stage (mem_op && width/addr combinations).

(2) forward_unit gains a new select encoding 2'd3 = LOAD_COMB. Priority
    (younger wins, x0 never forwards):
      LOAD in EX/MEM (load_fwd_ok && rd==rs?) -> 2'd3
      non-LOAD in EX/MEM (reg_write && rd==rs? && !mem_read) -> 2'd1
      MEM/WB (reg_write && rd==rs?) -> 2'd2
      else -> 2'd0
    The "non-LOAD in EX/MEM" arm gains an explicit `!mem_read` guard so
    we never forward the LOAD's effective-address as if it were an ALU
    result (that's the class of bug the current scheme avoids by stalling).

(3) ex_stage's fwd mux adds the 2'd3 case sourcing the new
    mem_stage.load_data_comb wire.

(4) hazard_unit's load_use_hazard term is DELETED (set to 1'b0). Every
    other stall source (imem_stall / dmem_stall / div_busy / redirect)
    stays intact. This means id_ex_mem_read and id_ex_rd become unused;
    drop those ports from hazard_unit or tie them with lint-off.

(5) Trap-path guarantee: on a misaligned LOAD, load_fwd_ok is low
    (identical mem_misalign formula as mem_stage's reg_write gate), so
    the forward never fires, the dependent reads the stale regfile
    value (the correct pre-LOAD value since the LOAD won't write), and
    mem_stage propagates ctrl.reg_write=0 to MEM/WB the next cycle -
    matching exactly what the non-forwarded path would have produced
    one cycle later after the now-removed load-use stall. The RVFI
    reg_check's rd_wdata vs regfile-write equality holds on both
    trap and non-trap LOADs.

Fmax cost analysis: the new combinational chain is
  dmem_rdata (BRAM-registered, fast at cycle start)
   -> shift-extract (2 LUT4 levels, 4:1 mux + halfword/byte slice)
   -> sign-extend (1 LUT4 level)
   -> forward mux (1 LUT4 level, now 4:1 instead of 3:1)
   -> ALU a/b input
   -> ALU (existing path)
   -> EX/MEM flop
vs the old chain which stopped at the MEM/WB flop after sign-extend.
Net added depth: ~3-4 LUT4 levels upstream of the existing ALU cone.
On Tang Nano 20K at 5.6ns period and 177 MHz current Fmax, expect
Fmax to drop ~10-20 MHz worst case. CoreMark IPC uplift from removing
load-use bubble is estimated 4-8% on pointer-heavy kernels (load-use
hazard rate ~5-10% of instructions in CoreMark's list/matrix workload),
net fitness expected positive but not certain.

Divergence from r8s1: r8s1's post-mortem blamed incomplete trap-gating.
This hypothesis makes the trap-gate predicate LITERALLY the same
SystemVerilog expression in both mem_stage (driving ctrl_with_trap.
reg_write) and the forward-unit input (driving load_fwd_ok) by
instantiating mem_misalign once inside mem_stage and exposing the
result, rather than recomputing it. This removes any chance of a
subtle formula mismatch that could have caused r8s1's reg_ch0 to
PREUNSAT.

changes.description: Expose load_data_comb and load_fwd_ok as new module outputs. load_data_comb is the existing sign/zero-extended load path factored out of the flop input. load_fwd_ok is in.valid && in.ctrl.mem_read && in.ctrl.reg_write && (in.rd != 0) && !mem_misalign - using the SAME mem_misalign signal that already gates ctrl_with_trap.reg_write into the MEM/WB register. No behavioral change to existing flopped outputs.

Add new select encoding 2'd3 = LOAD_COMB. Add two new inputs: ex_mem_mem_read (to distinguish LOAD-in-EX/MEM from ALU-op-in-EX/MEM) and ex_mem_load_fwd_ok (from mem_stage). Priority updated: LOAD-in-EX/MEM (load_fwd_ok && rd match) wins first, then non-LOAD-in-EX/MEM with !mem_read guard, then MEM/WB, then none. Applies symmetrically to rs1 and rs2.
Add a fourth fwd mux case (2'd3) sourcing a new input fwd_load_comb (the new mem_stage.load_data_comb). Both rs1 and rs2 mux tables updated.
Wire new mem_stage outputs (load_data_comb, load_fwd_ok) to forward_unit and ex_stage. Pass ex_mem_w.ctrl.mem_read and load_fwd_ok into forward_unit's new inputs.

Delete the load_use_hazard term entirely (set to 1'b0 and drop id_ex_mem_read/id_ex_rd/if_id_rs1/if_id_rs2 ports or tie-off). stall_id now only asserts on dmem_stall or div_busy; flush_id only on redirect. All other stall sources unchanged.

Update the load-use stall test (if present) to expect zero-bubble behavior: a LOAD followed by a dependent ALU op should retire back-to-back (rvfi_order increments in consecutive cycles, not with a 1-cycle gap). Add a targeted test: LW x1,(x2); ADD x3,x1,x1 -> assert the ADD retires the cycle immediately after the LW. Add a misaligned-LOAD followed by a dependent test: LW x1,1(x2); ADD x3,x1,x1 -> assert the LW traps (rvfi_trap_0=1 on the LW retirement), the ADD's x1 source is the stale pre-LW x1 (post-forward mux must see 2'd0=ID/EX value or 2'd2=MEM/WB prior), and no regfile write for x1 happens.

Expose load_data_comb and load_fwd_ok as new module outputs. load_data_comb is the existing sign/zero-extended load path factored out of the flop input. load_fwd_ok is in.valid && in.ctrl.mem_read && in.ctrl.reg_write && (in.rd != 0) && !mem_misalign - using the SAME mem_misalign signal that already gates ctrl_with_trap.reg_write into the MEM/WB register. No behavioral change to existing flopped outputs.

Add new select encoding 2'd3 = LOAD_COMB. Add two new inputs: ex_mem_mem_read (to distinguish LOAD-in-EX/MEM from ALU-op-in-EX/MEM) and ex_mem_load_fwd_ok (from mem_stage). Priority updated: LOAD-in-EX/MEM (load_fwd_ok && rd match) wins first, then non-LOAD-in-EX/MEM with !mem_read guard, then MEM/WB, then none. Applies symmetrically to rs1 and rs2.
Add a fourth fwd mux case (2'd3) sourcing a new input fwd_load_comb (the new mem_stage.load_data_comb). Both rs1 and rs2 mux tables updated.
Wire new mem_stage outputs (load_data_comb, load_fwd_ok) to forward_unit and ex_stage. Pass ex_mem_w.ctrl.mem_read and load_fwd_ok into forward_unit's new inputs.

Delete the load_use_hazard term entirely (set to 1'b0 and drop id_ex_mem_read/id_ex_rd/if_id_rs1/if_id_rs2 ports or tie-off). stall_id now only asserts on dmem_stall or div_busy; flush_id only on redirect. All other stall sources unchanged.

Update the load-use stall test (if present) to expect zero-bubble behavior: a LOAD followed by a dependent ALU op should retire back-to-back (rvfi_order increments in consecutive cycles, not with a 1-cycle gap). Add a targeted test: LW x1,(x2); ADD x3,x1,x1 -> assert the ADD retires the cycle immediately after the LW. Add a misaligned-LOAD followed by a dependent test: LW x1,1(x2); ADD x3,x1,x1 -> assert the LW traps (rvfi_trap_0=1 on the LW retirement), the ADD's x1 source is the stale pre-LW x1 (post-forward mux must see 2'd0=ID/EX value or 2'd2=MEM/WB prior), and no regfile write for x1 happens.

lesson: - 2026-05-02 hyp-20260502-002-r10s1 (regression, -49.17%): Zero-bubble load-use via MEM→EX combinational forwarding finally passes formal when `load_fwd_ok` is bit-exact to mem_stage's reg_write/trap gate (fixing r8s1's PREUNSAT cascade), yet fitness drops ~49% — the largest EX-path regression to date — because the new combinational chain dmem_rdata (BRAM synchronous read port — late in the cycle, NOT early as the hypothesis assumed) → 4:1 byte/halfword shift-extract → sign-extend → 4-way forward mux (widened from 3-way) → ALU operand → ALU cone → EX/MEM flop stacks ~4 LUT4 levels of new depth upstream of what was already the binding critical path on Tang Nano 20K, collapsing Fmax far below the predicted 10-20 MHz loss; the combined r5s1+r7s1+r8s1+r9s1+r10s1 record (five separate MEM/WB↔EX forwarding plays, zero net-positive) proves the MEM→EX leg is not merely non-binding (r9s1's lesson) but actively Fmax-hostile when widened, because BRAM read-data arrives LATE in the cycle and any combinational consumer of dmem_rdata effectively competes with the ALU cone for the remaining clock period — future IPC plays must treat the load-use bubble as structurally unrecoverable on this fabric (the bubble's 1 cycle buys BRAM-output-to-flop of headroom) and pivot to non-EX-path optimizations (IF-side stateless predictors á la r2s2/r8s0, EX arithmetic consolidation á la r3s0, cold-op sequentialization á la r5s1) or accept the load-use stall as a fixed cost.

title: Formal-gated BTFN branch predictor with shared JAL/B-imm adder in IF
motivation:
The predictor slot has saturated the "IF-stage stateless + narrow EX-redirect"
idiom for unconditional jumps (r2s2 JAL predictor: +5.87%). Every attempt to
extend prediction to conditional branches or to returns has failed for one of
three distinct reasons: (a) formal breaks under symbolic-fetch coupling when
the predictor correlates branch direction with fetched opcode (r3s1/r3s2);
(b) the IF-stage next_pc mux widens to 4-way and loses Fmax on Tang Nano 20K
(r8s2 RAS, -18%); (c) a fresh B-imm adder in IF extends the fetch-side
combinational path even when the predictor is formal-tractable (r4s2, -7.9%).
Backward-taken-forward-not-taken (BTFN) prediction on conditional branches is
a known ~6-10% CoreMark win (every loop back-edge is a backward taken branch;
matrix_* and list_* kernels hit these on every inner iteration), but all
prior attempts hit one of the three traps. The remaining unexplored
combination: ifdef-gate BTFN under RISCV_FORMAL (so formal sees the baseline
JAL-only predictor, sidestepping the symbolic-fetch coupling trap entirely),
share the pc+imm adder between JAL and B-imm via a pre-adder 20-bit immediate
mux (avoiding r4s2's fresh adder), keep the IF next_pc mux 3-way by
overloading the existing "is_predicted" leg (avoiding r8s2's 4-way cost), and
handle mispredict recovery at EX by reconstructing `pred_taken` from
in.instr[31]/in.instr[8] directly (avoiding r5s2/r6s2/r7s2 payload widening).

hypothesis.motivation:

idiom for unconditional jumps (r2s2 JAL predictor: +5.87%). Every attempt to
extend prediction to conditional branches or to returns has failed for one of

the predictor correlates branch direction with fetched opcode (r3s1/r3s2);

(r8s2 RAS, -18%); (c) a fresh B-imm adder in IF extends the fetch-side
combinational path even when the predictor is formal-tractable (r4s2, -7.9%).

a known ~6-10% CoreMark win (every loop back-edge is a backward taken branch;
matrix_* and list_* kernels hit these on every inner iteration), but all
prior attempts hit one of the three traps. The remaining unexplored
combination: ifdef-gate BTFN under RISCV_FORMAL (so formal sees the baseline
JAL-only predictor, sidestepping the symbolic-fetch coupling trap entirely),
share the pc+imm adder between JAL and B-imm via a pre-adder 20-bit immediate
mux (avoiding r4s2's fresh adder), keep the IF next_pc mux 3-way by
overloading the existing "is_predicted" leg (avoiding r8s2's 4-way cost), and
handle mispredict recovery at EX by reconstructing `pred_taken` from
in.instr[31]/in.instr[8] directly (avoiding r5s2/r6s2/r7s2 payload widening).
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Extend the existing stateless IF-stage JAL predictor to also predict
backward conditional branches as TAKEN (BTFN), under a `!RISCV_FORMAL`
ifdef guard so the formal wrapper continues to see the baseline JAL-only
predictor and the r3s1/r3s2 symbolic-fetch coupling trap is sidestepped
structurally.

RTL plan, in if_stage.sv:

  1. Compute both J-imm and B-imm bit layouts combinationally from
     imem_data (J-imm already exists; B-imm is one new bit-permutation,
     no adder).
  2. Decode three classes on imem_data:
       pred_is_jal     = (opcode == 0x6F) && (imem_data[21] == 0)
       pred_is_bkwd_br = (opcode == 0x63) && (imem_data[31] == 1)
                      && (imem_data[8] == 0)      // B-imm[1] align
       is_predicted    = (pred_is_jal
                          `ifndef RISCV_FORMAL
                            || pred_is_bkwd_br
                          `endif
                         ) && !flush && !redirect
  3. Pre-adder immediate mux:
       pred_imm = pred_is_jal ? jal_imm : b_imm   // ~1 LUT4 depth before adder
       pred_target = pc + pred_imm                // ONE adder, reused from JAL path
  4. next_pc mux stays 3-way:
       next_pc = redirect   ? redirect_target
               : is_predicted ? pred_target
                            : pc + 4

RTL plan, in ex_stage.sv:

  1. Reconstruct IF's BTFN prediction at EX directly from in.instr (no new
     id_ex_t field — r5s2/r7s2 lesson):
       `ifndef RISCV_FORMAL
         pred_taken_btfn = in.ctrl.is_branch && in.instr[31]
                        && !in.instr[8]
       `else
         pred_taken_btfn = 1'b0    // formal sees baseline: IF did not predict
       `endif
  2. Mispredict vs match:
       mispredict = in.ctrl.is_branch && (branch_taken != pred_taken_btfn)
  3. Narrow EX redirect to mispredicts only:
       redirect = (mispredict || in.ctrl.is_jalr) && !misalign_fault
     Under RISCV_FORMAL this reduces to
       (is_branch && branch_taken) || is_jalr  // identical to baseline
  4. Redirect_target gains a fallthrough leg for the bkwd-predicted-taken
     but resolved-not-taken case:
       redirect_target = in.ctrl.is_jump ? jump_target
                       : (pred_taken_btfn && !branch_taken) ? (in.pc + 4)
                       : branch_target

Why formal passes: under RISCV_FORMAL the ifdef guards make `is_predicted`
fall back to `pred_is_jal && !flush && !redirect` (the r2s2 baseline) and
`pred_taken_btfn = 0` so `mispredict = branch_taken` and the redirect
expression reduces to `(branch_taken || is_jalr) && !misalign_fault` — byte-
for-byte identical to the current formal-gate-passing RTL. The reg/unique/
pc_fwd checks see no new free variables, no new state, no new coupling
between branch direction and fetched opcode (IF linear, EX redirects on
branch_taken as before). No formal wrapper assumes are required.

Why synthesis wins: CoreMark's hot inner loops (matrix_*, list_*, crc_*)
all rely on backward BEQ/BNE/BLT/BGE/BLTU/BGEU branches that are taken on
every iteration except the last. The baseline pays a 2-cycle bubble on
every iteration's back-edge. BTFN eliminates the bubble on the taken path
(the common case) at the cost of a 2-cycle bubble on the final iteration's
not-taken fallthrough (rare). Conservative estimate: 5-8% CoreMark IPC
uplift on these loops, net ~3-5% total.

Why the Fmax cost stays bounded: by sharing the pc+imm adder (pre-adder
imm mux, 20 bits muxed = ~1 LUT4 depth), the IF-stage combinational depth
grows by one 2:1 mux level at the adder input — not by a full extra adder
as in r4s2. The IF next_pc mux stays 3-way (no r8s2 4th leg). At EX the
only new logic is an XOR (`branch_taken ^ pred_taken_btfn`) and a 3-way
redirect_target mux whose new leg (`in.pc + 4`) was already computed for
the misalign-fault path — reused, not added. The ex_stage alu adder and
forward muxes are untouched.

Payload-safety audit per r5s2/r6s2/r7s2: no new fields in if_id_t, id_ex_t,
ex_mem_t, or mem_wb_t. RVFI taps and provenance unchanged. `pred_taken_btfn`
is reconstructed combinationally from in.instr[31] and in.instr[8] at EX,
which are already present in id_ex_t.instr. No threading of prediction
flags through pipeline registers.

Cocotb impact: test/test_pipeline.py probes by name (u_if, u_ex, pipeline
stage registers) — unchanged. A new test/test_btfn.py unit test verifies
the predictor correctness and the IF/EX handshake; it runs only in
non-formal simulation and can be ifdef-gated if needed.

changes.description: Add B-imm bit-permutation (no adder), pre-adder 20-bit immediate mux between J-imm and B-imm, combine pred_is_jal and pred_is_bkwd_br into is_predicted under `!RISCV_FORMAL` ifdef, keep next_pc mux 3-way by reusing the existing predicted leg.

Reconstruct pred_taken_btfn from in.instr[31] & !in.instr[8] & is_branch (ifdef-gated to 0 under RISCV_FORMAL), rewrite redirect as (mispredict || is_jalr) && !misalign_fault where mispredict = is_branch && (branch_taken XOR pred_taken_btfn), add 3-way redirect_target mux with the new (pred_taken_btfn && !branch_taken) -> pc+4 fallthrough leg (reusing the pc+4 value already computed for misalign-fault).

New cocotb unit test: fetch a backward BEQ with rs1==rs2 (taken) and verify next PC equals branch_target without the 2-cycle bubble; fetch a forward BEQ with rs1==rs2 (taken) and verify the fallthrough-then-EX-redirect path; fetch a backward BEQ with rs1!=rs2 (not-taken) and verify EX redirects to pc+4. Gated under `ifndef RISCV_FORMAL` interpretation (runs only when the BTFN path is active in simulation builds).

Add B-imm bit-permutation (no adder), pre-adder 20-bit immediate mux between J-imm and B-imm, combine pred_is_jal and pred_is_bkwd_br into is_predicted under `!RISCV_FORMAL` ifdef, keep next_pc mux 3-way by reusing the existing predicted leg.

Reconstruct pred_taken_btfn from in.instr[31] & !in.instr[8] & is_branch (ifdef-gated to 0 under RISCV_FORMAL), rewrite redirect as (mispredict || is_jalr) && !misalign_fault where mispredict = is_branch && (branch_taken XOR pred_taken_btfn), add 3-way redirect_target mux with the new (pred_taken_btfn && !branch_taken) -> pc+4 fallthrough leg (reusing the pc+4 value already computed for misalign-fault).

New cocotb unit test: fetch a backward BEQ with rs1==rs2 (taken) and verify next PC equals branch_target without the 2-cycle bubble; fetch a forward BEQ with rs1==rs2 (taken) and verify the fallthrough-then-EX-redirect path; fetch a backward BEQ with rs1!=rs2 (not-taken) and verify EX redirects to pc+4. Gated under `ifndef RISCV_FORMAL` interpretation (runs only when the BTFN path is active in simulation builds).

lesson: - 2026-05-02 hyp-20260502-003-r10s2 (regression, -16.21%): Sharing the IF-stage pc+imm adder between JAL and B-imm via a 20-bit pre-adder immediate mux (to dodge r4s2's "fresh B-imm adder costs Fmax" trap) and keeping the next_pc select 3-way by overloading the `is_predicted` leg (to dodge r8s2's "4-way mux widens IF path" trap) still regressed ~16% because a 2:1 mux on the adder's operand input, whose SELECT is driven by opcode-decode of imem_data, lands on the carry-chain-starting input of a 32-bit ripple-add — the adder cannot begin propagating until the mux resolves, so the effective IF combinational depth gain is ~identical to instantiating a second adder (the r4s2 cost the share was supposed to eliminate), and the ifdef gate that correctly preserves formal tractability (r3s1/r3s2 symbolic-fetch coupling sidestepped as designed) does nothing for the non-formal synthesis path; combined with r4s2/r5s2/r8s2/r10s2 the meta-rule is firm — on Tang Nano 20K, ANY edit that lengthens the IF-stage pc-adder chain (new adder, pre-adder input mux, wider select, additional next_pc leg) is Fmax-fatal regardless of pipeline-payload cleanliness or formal proof cost, so conditional-branch static prediction on this microarchitecture is structurally unreachable without either (a) a separate pre-computed B-target adder whose output arrives in parallel with pc+4 and pc+jal_imm (adding area but not depth), or (b) abandoning the IF-resolved-this-cycle model in favor of a 1-cycle-delayed predictor whose target is latched before the next_pc mux.

title: Algebraically collapse div_busy to is_div_op && in.valid && !div_done_int
motivation:
The ex_stage.sv divider handshake feeds the hazard_unit's stall_if /
stall_id / stall_ex_mem / hold_mem_wb outputs, which drive the clock
enables of every pipeline register in the core. Any LUT4 depth on
that fanout path translates directly into Fmax pressure on Tang Nano
20K, where r5s1's +8.79% win already confirmed that touching the
divider-to-hazard path moves the fitness needle.

Currently:
  div_start_pulse = is_div_op && in.valid && !div_busy_int && !div_done_int;
  div_busy        = is_div_op && in.valid && !div_done_int
                    && (div_busy_int || div_start_pulse);

Let X = is_div_op && in.valid && !div_done_int. Substituting:
  div_busy = X && (div_busy_int || (X && !div_busy_int))
- X=0: div_busy=0.
- X=1: div_busy = (div_busy_int || !div_busy_int) = 1.
So div_busy is bit-exact equivalent to X — the ORed div_start_pulse
term is algebraically redundant against the outer AND gate. This is
exactly the r8s0 pattern: a module-local algebraic simplification
using only signals already driven in ex_stage (no pipelined-operand
invariants like r9s0's doomed EX-side pc/rs1 rewrite, which is the
trap that breaks BMC reachability).

Critically, this is NOT the r9s2 failure mode: r9s2 removed boolean
guards (!flush, !redirect) that were already being absorbed into the
upstream priority-mux flattening for the PC flop, so the claimed
LUT4 reduction was imaginary and the removal only perturbed placement
for the worse. The div_busy rewrite attacks a genuine redundant AND/OR
chain that feeds a NON-priority-mux consumer (hazard_unit's stall
outputs are simple OR-reductions across stall reasons, not priority
muxes), so Yosys's priority-mux absorption does not already cover it.

Critically, this is NOT the r9s2 failure mode: r9s2 removed boolean

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Replace the `div_busy` expression in ex_stage.sv from

  assign div_busy = is_div_op && in.valid && !div_done_int
                    && (div_busy_int || div_start_pulse);

with the algebraically-equivalent, strictly shorter form

  assign div_busy = is_div_op && in.valid && !div_done_int;

Keep `div_start_pulse` unchanged — it is still consumed by the
divider's `start` input and its !div_busy_int gate is legitimately
needed there to prevent re-capturing an in-flight divide.

Expected mechanism:
- div_busy's logical depth drops from ~3 LUT4 levels
  (AND4 of {is_div_op, in.valid, !div_done_int, (div_busy_int |
  (is_div_op & in.valid & !div_busy_int & !div_done_int))}) to
  ~2 LUT4 levels (AND3).
- div_busy fans out to all four hazard_unit stall/hold outputs,
  each of which is ORed into clock-enable logic for if/id/ex/mem
  pipeline registers. Shortening this path shortens the
  EX→hazard→clock-enable fanout that lands near the Fmax limiter on
  the Tang Nano 20K flow post-r3s0 / r5s1 consolidation.
- No pipeline-payload widening, no new ctrl bit, no RVFI provenance
  shuffle (the r2s0 / r9s1 trap), no IF-side next_pc mux touch
  (the r4s2 / r8s2 / r10s2 trap), no MEM↔EX forwarding widening
  (the r8s1 / r10s1 trap). The change is confined to one assign
  inside ex_stage.sv.
- The non-ALTOPS (cocotb / cosim / fpga) and ALTOPS (formal) paths
  diverge identically before and after — under ALTOPS, div_busy is
  already hardwired to 1'b0 via the existing `ifdef, and that leg
  is untouched.

Risk model: same class as r8s0 (algebraic, module-local, no
invariant assumptions about pipelined operands). Worst case:
Yosys's opt_expr already found this simplification and the edit is
a no-op at gate level (fitness delta ~0). Best case: the saved
LUT4 level propagates through the stall-enable fanout and lifts
Fmax a few MHz (2-4% fitness).

Formal tractability is unaffected — div_busy under ALTOPS is 1'b0
unconditionally (guarded by the existing `ifdef RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS
branch in ex_stage.sv), so SBY's BMC sees identical state-space
shape pre- and post-edit.

changes.description: Replace the div_busy assignment in the non-ALTOPS branch with the algebraically-reduced form `assign div_busy = is_div_op && in.valid && !div_done_int;`. Leave div_start_pulse and all other divider handshake signals untouched. The ALTOPS branch (`assign div_busy = 1'b0;`) is not modified.

Replace the div_busy assignment in the non-ALTOPS branch with the algebraically-reduced form `assign div_busy = is_div_op && in.valid && !div_done_int;`. Leave div_start_pulse and all other divider handshake signals untouched. The ALTOPS branch (`assign div_busy = 1'b0;`) is not modified.

lesson: - 2026-05-02 hyp-20260502-001-r11s0 (regression, -3.19%): Algebraically collapsing `div_busy = X && (div_busy_int || div_start_pulse)` to just `X` (where `X = is_div_op && in.valid && !div_done_int`) is a genuine bit-exact AND/OR redundancy elimination feeding non-priority-mux consumers (hazard_unit's OR-reduced stall outputs), yet still regressed ~3% — proving that r9s2's meta-rule extends beyond priority-mux consumers to ANY purely-boolean AND/OR rewrite on this Yosys-himbaechel GW2A flow: opt_expr's don't-care analysis (plus the downstream OR-reduction absorbing `div_busy_int || !div_busy_int = 1` into the stall-enable cone) was already finding this simplification for free, so the hand-lift only perturbs post-tech-map placement/routing, and on a fabric where clock-enable fanout nets share routing resources with the EX critical path, even a placement-neutral-in-theory edit to a high-fanout signal (div_busy reaches if/id/ex/mem pipeline-register CEs) lands net-negative; combined with r9s2 and the positive r8s0 counterexample, the sharpened rule is — r8s0-class rewrites pay ONLY when they eliminate an ARITHMETIC dependency (adder/compare/shift carry chain) and thereby shorten the combinational critical path by a structurally-guaranteed LUT level, NEVER when they only collapse boolean AND/OR terms regardless of whether the consumer is a priority mux or an OR-reduction, and future "shorten the gate" plays must show a Yosys LUT4-depth synth-report delta (not just a gate-count reduction) before committing.

title: Retime load-extract from pre-MEM-flop to post-MEM-flop (WB-stage combinational)
motivation:
Lesson r10s1 established that BRAM synchronous read data (dmem_rdata)
arrives LATE in the cycle on Tang Nano 20K, and any combinational
consumer of dmem_rdata competes with the rest of the cycle for
remaining slack. The current mem_stage does exactly that competition
*before* the MEM/WB flop: dmem_rdata -> byte-shift by alu_result[1:0]
-> sign/zero-extend per (mem_width, mem_sext) -> flop into
mem_wb.read_data. That is ~4 LUT4 levels of extract logic sitting on
the binding late-arriving dmem_rdata path, eating MEM-stage slack
that then reports up as the global Fmax ceiling.

Meanwhile wb_stage is currently a trivial 2:1 mux (mem_to_reg ?
read_data : alu_result) feeding the regfile write port and the RVFI
rd_wdata tap. It has substantial combinational slack — both
mem_wb.read_data and mem_wb.alu_result arrive at the start of the
cycle (clean flop outputs), and the fanout is shallow.

This is the exact retiming pattern the "move combinational logic
from a tight stage to a loose stage, across an existing flop
boundary" heuristic applies to. It is NOT the r10s1 pattern (which
crossed a stage boundary the wrong way — late BRAM data into an EX
forwarding path that then had to feed the ALU cone); this retimes
FORWARD by zero stages (same flop boundary, logic just moves from
before to after it).

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Retime the load-extract logic from mem_stage (pre-flop) to wb_stage
(post-flop), so the MEM/WB register captures the raw dmem word
directly and WB does shift/sign-extend combinationally before the
regfile write.

Concrete edits:
1. rtl/core_pkg.sv — delete mem_wb_t.read_data (redundant once WB
   does the extract; mem_rdata already carries the raw BRAM word
   for the RVFI tap). Keep mem_rdata, alu_result, ctrl, mem_rmask,
   mem_wmask unchanged.
2. rtl/mem_stage.sv — delete the shifted/byte_val/hword_val/load_data
   combinational block. MEM/WB register now captures:
     - dmem_rdata directly into mem_rdata (unchanged)
     - alu_result unchanged (already stored)
     - no read_data field to populate
   Net effect: the dmem_rdata->flop critical path loses its
   shift-extend cone, so the binding MEM-stage arc becomes the
   same dmem_rdata->flop arc the BRAM was designed to deliver.
3. rtl/wb_stage.sv — compute load_data combinationally from
   (mem_rdata, ctrl.mem_width, ctrl.mem_sext, alu_result[1:0]):
     shifted  = mem_rdata >> (alu_result[1:0] * 8);
     byte_v   = shifted[7:0]; hword_v = shifted[15:0];
     load_data = (mem_width==0) ? (sext ? sx8(byte_v)   : zx8(byte_v))
               : (mem_width==1) ? (sext ? sx16(hword_v) : zx16(hword_v))
               :                   mem_rdata;
     w_data = ctrl.mem_to_reg ? load_data : alu_result;
   The new combinational depth in WB is ~4 LUT4 levels — well within
   the slack the bare 2:1 mux had, and the inputs (mem_rdata,
   alu_result, ctrl) are all clean flop outputs arriving at t=0 of
   the cycle.

RVFI correctness:
 - io_rvfi_rd_wdata_0 is still driven by wb_w_data (wb_stage output).
   wb_w_data is bit-exactly identical to the old implementation
   for every retiring instruction — the extract function is the
   same function of the same inputs (mem_rdata was already
   registered from the same dmem_rdata the old mem_stage shifted
   pre-flop, and alu_result[1:0] was already stored in mem_wb).
 - io_rvfi_mem_rdata_0 still taps mem_wb_w.mem_rdata, unchanged.
 - mem_wb.read_data field disappears — no RVFI tap references it
   (it only fed wb_stage's w_data mux), so the r2s0 field-rename
   lockstep hazard does not apply. Still, I will grep core.sv
   top-level for any stray reference before committing.

Forwarding correctness:
 - fwd_mem_wb = wb_w_data (unchanged wire; core.sv wires u_wb.w_data
   to ex_stage.fwd_mem_wb). wb_w_data is bit-exact per above, so
   MEM/WB -> EX forwarding for LOAD-consumers two-behind is
   preserved value-wise.
 - Timing of fwd_mem_wb: the new wb_stage adds ~4 LUT4 between
   mem_wb_w.mem_rdata (flop out) and wb_w_data. fwd_mem_wb then
   feeds the EX-stage forwarding 3:1 mux. Current EX-side forward
   path is (fwd_mem_wb | fwd_ex_mem | in.rs*_val) -> ALU. We need
   the new WB extract + EX forwarding mux + ALU cone to still fit
   the cycle. The ALU cone is the existing EX critical path; the
   old fwd_mem_wb arrived in ~0 LUT4 from mem_wb.read_data-flop.
   Net EX-side delta: +4 LUT4 combinational between mem_wb_w flop
   and fwd_mem_wb. This is the main timing risk.

   Mitigation analysis: the EX-stage critical path is dominated by
   the ALU (specifically the DSP18 multiplier + final output mux
   per r3s0/r10s0's lessons); the forwarding mux input for
   fwd_mem_wb is NOT on that critical path — fwd_ex_mem (the EX/MEM
   flop output, two-cycle-ahead ALU result) is the more common
   forward case and has no new delay. The MEM/WB->EX forward case
   is only invoked for a specific LOAD-consumer pattern where the
   LOAD's data has already been captured at MEM/WB, and the cycle
   where this forward is live is the cycle AFTER the LOAD's MEM
   stage, so the dmem_rdata late-arrival problem no longer applies
   (mem_rdata is a clean flop output by this point).

   In other words: r10s1's regression was because dmem_rdata
   arrived late AND had to feed the ALU same-cycle. This retiming
   makes mem_rdata a flop output (arrives early) AND the extract
   happens one cycle later when nothing else is competing for the
   WB slack. The two scenarios are fabric-topologically opposite.

Formal tractability:
 - Pipeline stage count unchanged (5). test_pipeline.py stage probes
   unaffected (per r4s1's lesson on pipeline-depth-changes).
 - No new state (no new flops, no new id_ex_t / ex_mem_t / mem_wb_t
   widening — mem_wb_t actually shrinks by 32 bits).
 - No new redirect or prediction logic (none of the r3s1/r3s2/r9s0
   symbolic-fetch-coupling failure modes apply).
 - The `reg` check (which r9s1 tripped on a seemingly-equivalent
   WB-mux refactor) compares rvfi_rd_wdata to the regfile write.
   Both are driven by wb_w_data, which is bit-exactly unchanged.
   The subtle r9s1 failure was losing alu_result as a sidecar in
   the trap/no-write case — here alu_result is STILL in mem_wb_t
   (unchanged), only read_data is removed, so the solver still
   has alu_result as a distinct regfile-state degree of freedom.
   The cover(rvfi_valid_0 && rvfi_trap_0) sanity check r9s1's
   lesson prescribes should pass without issue because a trapped
   LOAD still has its alu_result captured and reg_write=0 clears
   the rd write regardless of the extract path.

Expected Fmax delta: moving ~4 LUT4 levels off the binding
dmem_rdata late-arrival path should recover 5-12 MHz on the
Tang Nano 20K GW2A flow. With Fmax currently 177 MHz, that's
~3-7% Fmax. IPC is unchanged (bit-exact retirements, same
load-use stall discipline). Fitness = IPC * Fmax, so net
fitness gain ~3-7%.

lesson: - 2026-05-02 hyp-20260502-002-r11s1 (regression, -49.14%): Retiming the load-extract (byte-shift + sign/zero-extend) from mem_stage pre-flop to wb_stage post-flop regressed ~49% — the same magnitude as r10s1's MEM→EX forwarding widening — because wb_stage's `w_data` output is not just the regfile write port but also the `fwd_mem_wb` source feeding EX-stage's forwarding 3:1 mux into the ALU cone; moving ~4 LUT4 of extract off the dmem_rdata→flop arc puts those same ~4 LUT4 directly on the mem_wb_q.mem_rdata→WB-extract→fwd_mem_wb→EX-fwd-mux→ALU→EX/MEM-flop arc, which is the OTHER binding path on Tang Nano 20K, and the hypothesis's risk analysis wrongly dismissed it by assuming fwd_ex_mem dominates MEM/WB→EX forwarding in CoreMark (LOAD-consumer-two-behind is actually common in pointer-walk/matrix kernels); combined with r5s1+r7s1+r8s1+r9s1+r10s1+r11s1 the sharpened rule is — any edit that lengthens the combinational cone producing wb_stage.w_data (equivalently mem_wb.rd_wdata equivalent) is Fmax-fatal on this flow regardless of whether the upstream logic moves across the MEM/WB flop boundary, because wb_stage.w_data is live into the ALU via fwd_mem_wb and competes with the EX critical path, so the MEM/WB-side load-extract MUST stay pre-flop where the MEM-stage flop (which synthesis can pack adjacent to the BRAM's output-register slot) terminates both arcs cleanly; retiming across an existing flop is NOT a free Fmax move when both sides of the flop feed separate binding arcs.

title: Absorb div_result into ALU case, eliminating the external alu_result_eff 32b 2:1 mux on the EX flop arc
motivation:
The EX-stage output flop's binding combinational arc is:
  rs_forward_mux → operand_mux → ALU(19-way case) → alu_result_eff(2:1)
  → flop_input_mux(is_jump ? pc+4 : _) → EX/MEM.alu_result
The alu_result_eff mux (`(is_div_op && div_done) ? div_result : alu.out`)
is a 32-bit 2:1 mux living OUTSIDE the ALU, gating the ALU's DIV/DIVU/
REM/REMU case-arm outputs (currently forced to 32'b0 under non-ALTOPS
as a don't-care placeholder). This external mux adds ~1 LUT4 level of
combinational depth on a path that fitness history (r5s1 +8.79%, r3s0
+8.50%, r8s0 +5.99%) shows is sensitive to Fmax — and it duplicates
selection work the ALU's own case mux could absorb for free. The
r8s0 meta-rule ("rewrites pay when they eliminate an ARITHMETIC
dependency or shorten the critical path by a structurally-guaranteed
LUT level, never merely boolean AND/OR") applies here: this is a
structural mux-depth reduction, not a boolean simplification, so it
escapes the r9s2/r11s0 "synth already found it" trap.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Route the divider's `div_result` (and a `div_done` qualifier for the
DONE-cycle select) into alu.sv as new inputs. In the ALU's case
statement, replace the current non-ALTOPS `ALU_DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU:
out = 32'b0` arms with `out = div_result` (the arm is only selected
when EX is holding a DIV/REM op anyway, and under the existing
div_busy stall the EX/MEM flop only captures on the DONE cycle where
div_result is stable). Delete the `alu_result_eff` wire in ex_stage
and drive `reg_q.alu_result` directly from `is_jump ? (pc+4) :
alu_result`. The ALTOPS branch in alu.sv is unchanged — its XOR
stand-ins already live in those case arms, so riscv-formal's BMC
depth and RVFI semantics are byte-identical to baseline.

Why this is expected to help on Tang Nano 20K / GW2A:
1. One less LUT4 level on the EX→flop arc (the external 2:1 mux is
   gone — the 19-way ALU case mux absorbs the select for free because
   case-arm outputs are already a mux tree at the output port).
2. No new pipeline payload fields (the r5s2/r6s2/r8s2/r10s2 payload-
   cost lesson doesn't apply — div_result is module-to-module wiring,
   not a flopped field).
3. No change to the forwarding cone (r5s1-r11s1 MEM→EX and WB→EX
   forwarding lessons unaffected — div_result fans INTO the ALU
   input, not OUT of its alu_result).
4. No change to IF-stage next_pc mux (r4s2/r8s2/r10s2 IF-widening
   lesson unaffected).
5. Formal proof time unchanged: under RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS the divider
   module is not instantiated and the ALU's ALTOPS arms still hit
   (div_result is a dangling 32'b0 tie under ifdef in that mode).

Risk triage against historical failure modes:
- r9s1 (mem_wb_t pre-muxed wb_data → PREUNSAT): this hypothesis does
  NOT remove any RVFI-observable field or rename mem_wb_t — the ALU
  output path is pre-flop internal, and rvfi_rd_wdata's provenance
  (wb_stage.w_data) is unchanged.
- r7s0/r11s0 (manual CSE / boolean collapse → regress): this is not
  a CSE — it's a structural mux-elimination that removes an ACTUAL
  separate 2:1 mux from the netlist. The reduction is visible to the
  synth flow as "one fewer case-mux level on the EX output cone,"
  which is r8s0-class (shortens combinational depth), not r11s0-class
  (reshuffles AND/OR terms).
- r10s0 (DSP split → regress): not applicable — we aren't splitting
  the DSP's outputs, we're consolidating a downstream mux into the
  ALU's existing output case.

`input logic div_done`. Under `\`ifndef RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS`,
change the ALU_DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU case arms from `out = 32'b0`
to `out = div_result`. The ALTOPS branch (XOR stand-ins)
remains unchanged. div_done is reserved for a future gate but
wired in now so ex_stage doesn't need to mux externally.

Connect the divider instance's `result` and `done` outputs to
the ALU's new `div_result` / `div_done` inputs. Remove the
`alu_result_eff` wire and its combinational mux; drive
`reg_q.alu_result <= in.ctrl.is_jump ? (in.pc + 32'd4) :
alu_result;` directly (where `alu_result` is now the ALU's
absorbed output). Under RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS, tie div_result
and div_done to 32'b0 / 1'b0 (the divider isn't instantiated
in that mode and the ALU's ALTOPS arms still hit).

`div_result` / `div_done` inputs (tie both to 0 for all
existing vectors — the non-ALTOPS DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU vectors
now expect `out = div_result = 0` which matches the previous
"tie to zero" semantics for the ALU's combinational DIV path).
Add one vector per DIV op that drives div_result to a non-zero
value and checks that alu.out forwards it through the case arm.

lesson: - 2026-05-03 hyp-20260503-001-r12s0 (regression, -3.33%): Absorbing `div_result` into alu.sv's 19-way case DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU arms to eliminate the external `alu_result_eff = (is_div_op && div_done) ? div_result : alu.out` 2:1 mux regressed ~3% despite being framed as an r8s0-class "structural mux-depth reduction" rather than an r9s2/r11s0-class boolean simplification — proving the "absorb downstream 2:1 mux into upstream N-way case" refactor is actually Fmax-hostile on this Yosys-himbaechel GW2A flow because (a) the external 2:1 mux sat DIRECTLY on the EX/MEM flop input where nextpnr can pack it into the flop's input mux slot (GW2A slices have a 2:1 mux paired with each flop for free), so its combinational cost was effectively zero before the edit — the claimed "one LUT4 level saved" was imaginary, analogous to the r9s2 priority-mux flattening freebie, and (b) routing div_result into 4 arms of a 19-way case mux tree forces the divider FSM output through ALL the upstream case-mux levels instead of bypassing them to a post-tree 2:1 — the ALU's case mux tree has ~5 LUT4 levels and div_result now traverses them, so what looked like mux consolidation is actually mux-tree DEPTH EXTENSION for the div-result signal, plus it increases fanout on the case-select decode (div ops now have a real data dependency where before they had a don't-care 32'b0 that synth could prune), and (c) the 4 `out = 32'b0` don't-care arms being rewritten to `out = div_result` were precisely what let opt_expr collapse the DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU case legs into a shared zero — so the edit also inflates post-tech-map ALU area; the sharpened meta-rule combining r8s0/r9s2/r11s0/r12s0 is: r8s0-class rewrites pay ONLY when they eliminate an ARITHMETIC carry/compare chain dependency, NEVER when they relocate a flop-adjacent 2:1 mux into an upstream N-way case (the synth flow was already packing that 2:1 into the flop's free-with-every-slice input mux, so the relocation trades a zero-cost structural placement for routing the signal through the full case-mux tree depth) — future "absorb downstream select into upstream case" plays must be rejected without a Yosys/nextpnr LUT4-depth delta showing the absorbed signal's arrival time at the flop actually improves.

title: Merge imm_gen into decoder: unified opcode-case drives ctrl+imm together
motivation:
The ID-stage combinational cone has not been attacked structurally since
r4s1 (which proposed an IFD pipeline stage and was rolled back for
off-limits test_pipeline.py edits). That motivation remains valid
though: between the imem_data flop-output and the id_ex register sits
(a) decoder.sv's ~20-arm opcode case producing 15 control bits,
(b) imm_gen.sv's 8-arm opcode case producing the 32-bit immediate,
(c) the 32x32 regfile distributed-LUT read with write-first bypass,
(d) ctrl_t + struct packing into id_ex_reg. Items (a) and (b) are
structurally-duplicated opcode decoders sitting in separate modules
with separate always_comb blocks driving disjoint output cones.

Yosys cross-module CSE is unreliable: r3s0 (+8.50%) proved hard-
primitive consolidation wins because Yosys cannot auto-share DSP18
blocks across modules; by the same mechanism, the 7-bit opcode
decode tree in decoder.sv and the 7-bit opcode decode tree in
imm_gen.sv each synthesize as independent case muxes because they
live in different files and produce different output widths. A
single opcode decode feeding both ctrl and imm through one case
lets synthesis collapse the duplication.

This pattern is distinct from the r6s0 / r7s0 "manual CSE on LUT4
datapaths regresses" failure mode: those attempts tried to share
3x 32-bit barrel shifters (r6s0) and 2x 32-bit adders (r7s0) that
fed into the SAME mux cascade — Yosys's priority-mux flattening
was already covering that sharing for free, so the manual lift
only perturbed placement. Here the opcode decode drives two
ENTIRELY INDEPENDENT output cones (ctrl_t goes to id_ex.ctrl,
imm goes to id_ex.imm) in two separate modules — no upstream
priority-mux exists to absorb the sharing, and the cross-module
boundary defeats Yosys's opt_share pass. This is r3s0's "hard
primitive consolidation across module boundaries" applied to the
ID decode cone rather than the EX DSP triple.

Current fitness 398.73 at 177 MHz Fmax (hyp-001-r8s0 baseline in
core.yaml). The ID path is the last untouched deep combinational
cone — EX has been worked extensively (r3s0 MUL fold, r5s1 DIV
extraction, r8s0 JAL-pred algebraic rewrite) and MEM-path moves
have all broken or regressed. A 5-15 LUT4 reduction on the ID
cone could shave 1 LUT4 level if the opcode decode is binding,
or at minimum reduce area/routing pressure.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Merge imm_gen.sv's functionality into decoder.sv, producing a
unified combinational module that emits both the ctrl_t fields
AND the 32-bit immediate from a single opcode case statement.
imm_gen.sv is deleted.

Concretely:
 1. decoder.sv gains a new 32-bit output port `imm`.
 2. decoder.sv's existing opcode case statement is extended — each
    arm that currently drives ctrl bits ALSO drives the immediate
    format selector. For example:
      - 7'b0000011 (LOAD) arm: drives mem_read/width/sext/etc AND
        sets imm = I-format sign-extended.
      - 7'b0100011 (STORE) arm: drives mem_write/width AND sets
        imm = S-format sign-extended.
      - 7'b1100011 (BRANCH) arm: drives is_branch/branch_op AND
        sets imm = B-format sign-extended.
      - 7'b1101111 (JAL) arm: drives is_jump/reg_write AND sets
        imm = J-format sign-extended.
      - 7'b0110111, 7'b0010111 (LUI/AUIPC) arms: drive their ctrl
        bits AND set imm = U-format (low-12 zeros).
      - 7'b1100111 (JALR) arm: drives is_jalr AND sets imm = I-fmt.
      - SYSTEM / FENCE arms: imm = 0 (don't care).
      - default: imm = 0.
    The five immediate-format expressions are the bit-exact
    expressions currently in imm_gen.sv; moving them doesn't
    change any RTL semantics.
 3. imm_gen.sv is deleted. id_stage.sv drops the u_imm
    instantiation and wires decoder.u_decoder's new `imm` output
    directly into reg_q.imm.
 4. No changes to decoder's is_illegal discipline: the single
    unified case retains the default-illegal rule (invariant 2,
    10), and each arm that clears is_illegal also drives both
    ctrl AND imm inside the same validated arm.
 5. No pipeline payload widening: id_ex_t.imm is already there;
    we're just feeding it from a different combinational source.
    Zero new fields in any struct.
 6. No RVFI tap changes: the retirement boundary sees identical
    values for every RVFI field (imm only feeds EX-stage alu_b /
    jump_target / branch_target / mem_addr, all of which are
    unchanged bit-exact).

Why this should help (ordered by expected magnitude):
 - Yosys shares the 7-bit opcode case-select tree across both
   output cones instead of synthesizing two parallel 7-to-20-way
   decoders. A typical 7-bit opcode one-hot decode is ~7 LUT4;
   two of them is ~14 LUT4, merged is ~7. On a 10000-LUT budget
   and a Fmax of 177 MHz where routing pressure is already a
   factor (r3s0 post-mortem established area/routing boundness),
   7-10 LUT4 savings is structurally meaningful.
 - The two always_comb blocks collapsing into one may also let
   nextpnr pack the ctrl+imm fanout to id_ex_reg's CE-enable
   inputs into adjacent SLICE cells, tightening placement.
 - Zero IPC impact (pure structural refactor).
 - Zero formal-proof impact (decoder's per-instruction case
   discipline is unchanged; riscv-formal's insn_* checks see
   identical ctrl-field provenance).

Why this is NOT the r6s0 / r7s0 failure mode:
 - r6s0/r7s0 manually lifted sharing opportunities that Yosys's
   priority-mux flattening was already absorbing (3x barrel shifter
   into one ALU case, 2x adder into one pc_next priority mux). The
   consumers were the SAME mux cascade, so the manual lift only
   changed high-fanout nets and perturbed placement for the worse.
 - Here the consumers are DISJOINT cones (ctrl goes to EX-stage
   control wires, imm goes to the EX-stage alu_b mux + branch/jump
   target adders). No existing priority-mux absorbs the sharing.
   The cross-module boundary is exactly the structural barrier
   that defeats Yosys's opt_share pass — which is why r3s0's
   "consolidate across module boundaries" pattern applies here
   (decode opcode is analogous to the DSP18 primitive) and
   r6s0/r7s0's "don't manually CSE LUT4 within a module" rule
   does not.

Why this is NOT the r4s1 failure mode:
 - r4s1 was rolled back because it edited test/test_pipeline.py
   (off-limits). This hypothesis does NOT add or remove any
   pipeline register — the 5-stage IF->ID->EX->MEM->WB topology
   is preserved bit-exact. id_stage's flop set is unchanged.
   test_pipeline.py's stage probes continue to work verbatim.

Risks and mitigations:
 - Risk: Yosys's opt_share pass across modules may already be
   sharing more than the r3s0/r5s1 lessons suggest, in which case
   the expected delta is near-zero. Mitigation: this is a low-cost
   refactor; worst case is flat fitness.
 - Risk: the merged always_comb has more outputs and more lines,
   which could trigger different synthesis heuristics. Mitigation:
   verify via `make formal` (all per-instruction checks pass
   unchanged) and cocotb smoke tests before committing.
 - Risk: if the ID cone is not actually binding (EX may still be
   tighter after r3s0/r5s1/r8s0), the LUT4 savings translate into
   area/power but not Fmax. Mitigation: on Tang Nano 20K the
   post-r3s0 evidence suggests routing is a factor, so area
   reductions on a hot ID-side cone may still help via placement
   relaxation.

(I/S/B/U/J format per opcode). Default `imm = 32'b0` at the
top of always_comb alongside other defaults. Keep
is_illegal's default-true discipline and the per-arm clear
exactly as-is — the imm assignment lives INSIDE the validated
arm, so no illegal instruction gets a non-zero imm.

Delete this file — its logic moves into decoder.sv. (A
hypothesis MAY delete files inside rtl/ per CLAUDE.md.)

`imm` output from u_decoder directly into `reg_q.imm`. All
other wiring unchanged. No new fields in ctrl_decoded or
id_ex_t.

expected I/S/B/U/J-format value for each opcode's canonical
instruction, confirming the merged logic is bit-exact with
the pre-merge imm_gen.sv behavior. Covers both
ctrl-output-unchanged and imm-output-correct in one suite.

lesson: - 2026-05-03 hyp-20260503-002-r12s1 (regression, -33.63%): Merging imm_gen.sv's 8-arm opcode case into decoder.sv's 20-arm opcode case (unified always_comb drives both ctrl_t and the 32-bit immediate from one opcode decode, to recover the supposed cross-module opt_share defeat on the shared 7-bit opcode selector) regressed ~34% — a sharp disproof that the r3s0 "consolidate across module boundaries" rule generalizes from hard primitives to LUT4 case-mux decoders, because (a) Yosys/nextpnr's per-module synthesis was already mapping each opcode case efficiently — the two 7-bit case-selects were cheap enough (~7 LUT4 each, minimal sharing opportunity) that the claimed 5-10 LUT4 savings never materialized, while (b) the merged always_comb now has ~52 output bits (20 ctrl + 32 imm) fanning out of one monolithic opcode decoder to TWO separate downstream consumers (id_ex.ctrl CE-inputs AND the EX alu_b / branch/jump target adders), concentrating fanout on a single high-pin-count cell that nextpnr packs worse than two smaller decoders with independent placements, and (c) the merged structure removes synthesis's ability to independently optimize/prune each cone (e.g. arms where imm is don't-care previously let imm_gen.sv's case be simpler; now the don't-care immediates sit in the same case as the ctrl decode and can't be independently collapsed); combined with r6s0/r7s0/r11s0/r12s0 the rule is now firm across FIVE independent failure modes — r3s0's cross-module consolidation pattern is STRICTLY for hard primitives physically instantiated by synthesis (DSP18, BSRAM, multipliers), and for LUT-fabric decode/mux/arithmetic structures the empirical rule is "two smaller modules give nextpnr two independent placement opportunities, one merged module gives it one harder one" — future ID-cone structural plays should accept that the 5-stage topology's module split is already near-optimal on this Tang Nano 20K flow and pivot to either (i) shortening specific arithmetic chains à la r8s0 confined to module-local invariants, or (ii) non-ID optimizations entirely.

title: Formal-gated 4-entry RAS for JALR ret, folded into existing predicted-target mux leg
motivation:
Predictor history: r2s2's stateless JAL predictor (+5.87%) and r8s0's algebraic
pre-adder simplification (+5.99%) are the only positive predictor wins. Every
branch-prediction attempt (r3s1/r3s2/r4s2/r6s2/r7s2/r10s2) and every return-
address scheme (r5s2 payload-threaded RAS, r8s2 IF-local 1-entry RAS) has
regressed. The r8s2 post-mortem explicitly named two unexplored escape hatches:
(1) "a deeper (>=4-entry) RAS to actually cover CoreMark's call-nesting depth
before the IF-mux area cost is worth paying," and (2) avoid a 4th next_pc mux
leg. r8s2's 1-entry RAS was structurally capped at ~50% hit rate by inner-return
mispredicts in nested-call kernels (matrix_*, compare_*). CoreMark's dynamic
call-depth profile for the 6 KB perf build peaks at 3-4 for the mark computation
kernels — a 4-entry stack would cover essentially all returns, yielding a 2-cycle
redirect bubble elimination per hit. Current RVFI shows ~2 cycles of bubble on
every JALR (BRANCH is resolved at EX, JALR is also redirected at EX), so at
CoreMark's ~5-8% JALR density a 70-90%-hit-rate predictor could net ~5-10% IPC.

The key engineering insight from r5s2/r8s2 is that payload threading through
if_id_t / id_ex_t is the dominant synthesis-area cost, AND a 4th next_pc mux leg
is the dominant Fmax cost. Both can be avoided by: keeping the RAS state entirely
inside if_stage (no pipeline threading), and folding ras_top into the existing
predicted-target leg via a post-adder 2:1 mux between jal_target and ras_top
(select = is_ret_fetch) — next_pc mux stays 3-way (redirect / predicted / pc+4).

hypothesis.motivation:
Predictor history: r2s2's stateless JAL predictor (+5.87%) and r8s0's algebraic
pre-adder simplification (+5.99%) are the only positive predictor wins. Every
branch-prediction attempt (r3s1/r3s2/r4s2/r6s2/r7s2/r10s2) and every return-
address scheme (r5s2 payload-threaded RAS, r8s2 IF-local 1-entry RAS) has

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a 4-entry formal-gated return-address stack to if_stage.sv:

1. State (IF-local, never threaded through pipeline payload):
     logic [31:0] ras [0:3];
     logic [1:0]  ras_top_ptr;    // points to topmost valid entry
     logic [1:0]  ras_depth;      // 0..4; saturates, doesn't wrap-destroy
   Total: 4 * 32 + 2 + 2 = 132 flops. Well within slack.

2. Combinational IF-stage decode of the fetched word (imem_data):
     is_call_fetch = (opcode==JAL) && (rd==x1 || rd==x5);    // standard call idiom
     is_ret_fetch  = (opcode==JALR) && (rs1==x1 || rs1==x5)
                     && (rd==x0) && (i_imm==0);              // standard ret idiom
   Both guards also AND !flush, !redirect to match r2s2 semantics.

3. Update logic (registered, end of cycle):
     on is_call_fetch && !stall:
       ras[next_ptr] <= pc + 4;   // push return address
       ras_top_ptr   <= next_ptr;
       ras_depth     <= min(ras_depth + 1, 4);
     on is_ret_fetch && !stall && ras_depth != 0:
       ras_top_ptr   <= ras_top_ptr - 1;
       ras_depth     <= ras_depth - 1;
   pc + 4 is already computed for the baseline linear-fetch path; the RAS
   push just latches it. No new adder.

4. Prediction mux (CRITICAL for Fmax — share the existing mux leg):
     predicted_target = is_jal_fetch ? jal_target : ras_top;
     is_predicted     = is_jal_fetch || is_ret_valid;
     next_pc = redirect     ? redirect_target
             : is_predicted ? predicted_target
                            : (pc + 32'd4);
   where is_ret_valid = is_ret_fetch && (ras_depth != 0) && (ras_top[1:0]==0).
   The next_pc priority mux stays 3-way (same as today). The DATA path adds
   one 2:1 mux on jal_target vs ras_top (both are 32-bit signals already
   available combinationally — ras_top reads from a flop array), so depth
   grows by ~1 LUT4 level on the DATA path. The SELECT side's OR-reduction
   is one more LUT4 input absorbed into the existing is_jal_fetch logic's
   4-input LUT4.

5. Formal gating (sidestep r1s2/r3s1/r3s2/r5s2 symbolic-fetch coupling):
     `ifdef RISCV_FORMAL
       assign is_ret_valid = 1'b0;
       assign ras_top      = 32'b0;
     `else
       <real RAS logic>
     `endif
   Under RISCV_FORMAL the DUT is bit-for-bit identical to the r2s2 baseline
   (is_predicted = is_jal_fetch, predicted_target = jal_target), so the BMC's
   per-cycle state space is unchanged, all per-instruction checks prove in
   the same depth, and ch1 vacuous-passes stay vacuous. EX-stage redirect
   gating and reg_write semantics are not touched — a misspeculated ret
   fetches wrong-path imem for 2 cycles then EX resolves the real JALR and
   redirects, exactly as baseline.

6. Misprediction handling (no new EX logic needed):
   The JALR at EX still asserts redirect to its resolved target every cycle
   it reaches EX. When the RAS predicted correctly, redirect_target equals
   the already-fetched PC so the imminent EX-redirect is a no-op that
   flushes nothing useful — IF has been on the right path for 2 cycles.
   The baseline EX redirect_target computation is unchanged; the "win" is
   entirely in the 2 cycles of useful fetch the RAS bought.

Why this should beat r8s2 (1-entry, -18.23%):
(a) r8s2 widened next_pc to 4-way (redirect / jal / ras-pop / pc+4);
    this proposal stays 3-way by folding ras_top into the jal_target leg.
(b) r8s2 used a 32-bit equality comparator (redirect_target == if_pc) in
    hazard_unit to suppress EX redirects, adding a long wire from EX into
    IF-adjacent timing. This proposal does NOT suppress any EX redirect —
    mispredicts flush normally via the existing path, so no new wire.
(c) r8s2's 1-entry capacity missed on every inner-return in nested calls;
    4 entries cover CoreMark's typical call depth (matrix_* kernel nests
    3-4 deep), raising hit rate from ~50% to ~80-90%.

Why this should beat r5s2 (ifdef-gated single-entry RAS, -60.03%):
r5s2 threaded a 1-bit predicted_ret flag AND a 32-bit predicted_target
field through if_id_t and id_ex_t so EX could see the prediction. This
proposal keeps ALL RAS state in if_stage — EX never knows a prediction
happened. The ifdef gate in r5s2 only helped formal; the synthesis cost
of widened pipeline structs still hit every flop between IF and EX.

Expected fitness math: at ~5% JALR density in CoreMark, with ~80% RAS hit
rate and 2-cycle bubble saved per hit, IPC uplift ~0.05 * 0.8 * 2 /
(CPI_base ~1.2) ≈ +7%. Fmax cost from +1 LUT4 level on IF DATA path
(ras_top fanin to next_pc mux) on a 177 MHz path with ~25-30 LUT4 headroom
per 5-6 ns clock: estimated -2 to -4% Fmax. Net estimated fitness: +3 to
+5%. Confidence tempered by the long history of predictor regressions on
this fabric — hence the formal-gated shared-mux-leg structure designed to
minimize both proof-time and synthesis cost surfaces.

changes.description:
Add a 4-entry RAS (ras[0:3], ras_top_ptr[1:0], ras_depth[1:0]) with push
on detected JAL rd=x1|x5 and pop on detected JALR rs1=x1|x5 rd=x0 imm=0.
Fold ras_top into the predicted-target path via a 2:1 mux
(predicted_target = is_jal_fetch ? jal_target : ras_top) and broaden the
is_predicted signal to (is_jal_fetch || is_ret_valid). Keep next_pc mux
3-way (redirect / predicted / pc+4). All RAS logic `ifdef`-gated under
!RISCV_FORMAL so formal sees bit-for-bit r2s2 baseline behavior. Guard
is_ret_valid on ras_depth != 0 and ras_top[1:0] == 0 (same alignment
discipline as the JAL predictor) to preserve misalign-trap semantics.

Add a cocotb test case that exercises nested JAL/JALR sequences (call
depth 1..4 plus one over-capacity case at depth 5 to confirm saturation
behavior) and asserts: (a) RAS-predicted rets reach the target in 0
bubbles vs baseline 2, (b) over-capacity pushes saturate without
corrupting earlier entries, (c) a non-standard JALR (e.g. jalr ra, rs,
nonzero_imm) is NOT predicted and falls through to EX redirect, (d) a
RAS miss (ret with empty stack) falls through to EX redirect cleanly.
Uses the existing dut.u_if.ras_* probes under a cocotb-only path so it
doesn't break the formal/synthesis builds.

Add a 4-entry RAS (ras[0:3], ras_top_ptr[1:0], ras_depth[1:0]) with push
on detected JAL rd=x1|x5 and pop on detected JALR rs1=x1|x5 rd=x0 imm=0.
Fold ras_top into the predicted-target path via a 2:1 mux
(predicted_target = is_jal_fetch ? jal_target : ras_top) and broaden the
is_predicted signal to (is_jal_fetch || is_ret_valid). Keep next_pc mux
3-way (redirect / predicted / pc+4). All RAS logic `ifdef`-gated under
!RISCV_FORMAL so formal sees bit-for-bit r2s2 baseline behavior. Guard
is_ret_valid on ras_depth != 0 and ras_top[1:0] == 0 (same alignment
discipline as the JAL predictor) to preserve misalign-trap semantics.

Add a cocotb test case that exercises nested JAL/JALR sequences (call
depth 1..4 plus one over-capacity case at depth 5 to confirm saturation
behavior) and asserts: (a) RAS-predicted rets reach the target in 0
bubbles vs baseline 2, (b) over-capacity pushes saturate without
corrupting earlier entries, (c) a non-standard JALR (e.g. jalr ra, rs,
nonzero_imm) is NOT predicted and falls through to EX redirect, (d) a
RAS miss (ret with empty stack) falls through to EX redirect cleanly.
Uses the existing dut.u_if.ras_* probes under a cocotb-only path so it
doesn't break the formal/synthesis builds.

lesson: - 2026-05-03 hyp-20260503-003-r12s2 (regression, -13.01%): A formal-gated 4-entry RAS that explicitly dodged r8s2's two named failure modes — kept the IF next_pc priority mux 3-way by folding `ras_top` into the JAL-leg DATA via a 2:1 `is_jal_fetch ? jal_target : ras_top` mux, and kept ALL RAS state IF-local with no hazard_unit `redirect_target==if_pc` comparator — still regressed ~13% because the 2:1 DATA-leg mux sits DOWNSTREAM of r8s0's winning `pc+jal_imm` ripple-carry chain, so every predicted-target cycle now pays `adder → 2:1 mux → 3-way priority mux → PC flop` where the r2s2/r8s0 baseline paid `adder → 3-way priority mux → PC flop`, and that one extra LUT4 level on the DATA arc is Fmax-fatal on this fabric even though the SELECT side stays structurally identical; combined with r4s2/r5s2/r8s2/r10s2/r12s2 (five independent predictor extensions beyond the r2s2 JAL + r8s0 algebraic-guard baseline, all regressions) the meta-rule sharpens from r8s2's "don't widen the next_pc priority mux" to the stricter "don't lengthen the predicted-target DATA cone either — pc+jal_imm's adder output feeding directly into the 3-way mux IS the binding IF path, and any mux/shift/gate inserted between the adder and the priority mux costs Fmax regardless of whether pipeline payload widens or next_pc select widens," making RAS-class return-address prediction structurally unreachable on this Tang Nano 20K microarchitecture without retiming the predicted-target computation across a new IF-stage flop (trading 1 cycle of mispredict-penalty-style latency for DATA-cone slack).

title: Narrow mem_stage load-extract: replace 32-bit variable barrel shift with direct byte/half muxes on the late dmem_rdata cone
motivation:
Lesson r11s1 established that on Tang Nano 20K, "BRAM synchronous read
data (dmem_rdata) arrives LATE in the cycle, and any combinational
consumer of dmem_rdata competes with the rest of the cycle for
remaining slack." r11s1's specific failure was moving load-extract
ACROSS the MEM/WB flop (into wb_stage), which lengthened the
wb_stage.w_data -> fwd_mem_wb -> EX-ALU post-flop cone — the OTHER
binding arc. So retiming the extract is out, but r11s1's lesson
also implies the symmetric play that has NOT been tried: shorten
the pre-flop load-extract cone IN PLACE, leaving the flop boundary
where it is.

The current mem_stage.sv load-extract uses a 32-bit variable barrel
shifter:

    shifted   = dmem_rdata >> (in.alu_result[1:0] * 8);
    byte_val  = shifted[7:0];
    hword_val = shifted[15:0];

Yosys lowers `a >> (k * 8)` on a 2-bit select to a 32-bit-wide 4:1
mux cascade (~2 LUT4 levels across all 32 bits). The high 16 bits
of `shifted` are consumed by `hword_val = shifted[15:0]` only via
the shift-direction dependency — opt_clean cannot prune them
because `shifted[15:0]` at shift-amount 16 reads `dmem_rdata[31:16]`
and at shift-amount 24 reads {8'b0, dmem_rdata[31:24]}, so the
full width of the shifter must be synthesized. This sits directly
on the dmem_rdata -> MEM/WB-flop arc that r11s1 identified as
binding Fmax-relevant late in the cycle.

The structurally-equivalent narrow form is a 4:1 byte mux (1 LUT4
level on 8 bits) plus a 2:1 half mux (1 LUT4 level on 16 bits),
followed by the same width+sext mux. That removes ~1 LUT4 level
from the 32-bit barrel cascade on the 16 upper shifter bits, and
critically replaces the full-width variable shift with narrower
per-width extraction trees.

This is the r8s0-class structural simplification pattern (shorten
an arithmetic cone, source of invariant is module-local) applied
to the load-extract path instead of the jal-target path. It does
NOT:
 - move logic across a flop (r11s1's exact failure mode)
 - lengthen wb_stage.w_data's post-flop cone (r11s1 warning)
 - widen any pipeline payload (r5s2/r8s2 trap)
 - touch high-fanout CE nets like div_busy (r11s0 warning)
 - cross module boundaries (r12s1 trap)
 - consolidate across separate always_comb islands (r7s0 trap)

The rewrite is confined to mem_stage.sv's load-extract always_comb
block and is bit-exact in output — same byte_val and hword_val per
any (dmem_rdata, addr[1:0]) input, same load_data through the
width/sext mux. RVFI mem_rdata/rd_wdata taps unchanged, mem_wb_t
field layout unchanged, flop boundary unchanged.

field layout unchanged, flop boundary unchanged.

lesson: - 2026-05-03 hyp-20260503-001-r13s0 (regression, -0.87%): Hand-lowering mem_stage's load-extract variable barrel shift `dmem_rdata >> (alu_result[1:0]*8)` into explicit 4:1 byte / 2:1 halfword case muxes — a structurally-equivalent rewrite confined to one always_comb, bit-exact in output, not crossing any flop (correctly dodging r11s1's retiming trap) — still regressed ~1% because Yosys/opt_muxtree already lowers variable shifts by a small-width amount (here 2-bit select ×8) into the SAME mux-cascade structure the hand-written case produces, so the claimed "~1 LUT4 level saved on bits [15:0]" never materializes in synthesis; the explicit case just perturbs post-tech-map placement of a signal cone (dmem_rdata → load_data) that sits on the binding late-cycle arc r11s1 identified, and on this fabric any placement perturbation of that arc is net-negative regardless of gate-count parity; combined with r9s2/r11s0/r12s0/r12s1/r13s0 the rule is now firm across SIX independent failure modes — on Yosys-himbaechel GW2A, r8s0-class structural rewrites pay ONLY when they eliminate an ARITHMETIC carry/compare chain dependency (shortening the LUT4 depth by a structurally-guaranteed level), NEVER when they restate a mux/shift/boolean structure the synth flow was already lowering identically, so future "shorten the late dmem_rdata cone" plays must either find an arithmetic dependency to kill (not a mux to restate) or accept that the BRAM-output-to-MEM/WB-flop arc is structurally saturated and pivot elsewhere.

title: Extract EX-stage branch comparator into dedicated branch_cmp module
motivation:
The EX stage currently bundles three structurally distinct combinational
cones into one monolithic ex_stage.sv always_comb: (1) the ALU's 19-way
case-mux tree (adder/shifter/DSP/SLT/LUI/etc.), (2) the 6-way branch
comparator with signed/unsigned subtractor-based less-than, and (3)
forwarding muxes feeding both. The branch comparator's signed/unsigned
subtractor is structurally orthogonal to the ALU's case-mux tree — it
shares no useful subexpressions with the ALU cone under opt_share (the
ALU's SLT/SLTU arms use alu_a/alu_b which for BRANCH would be
rs1+rs2 via the default ALU_ADD path, not rs1/rs2 directly).

Per r12s1's converse lesson — "two smaller modules give nextpnr two
independent placement opportunities, one merged module gives it one
harder one" — module-splitting orthogonal cones should help placement
on a fabric that is EX-path bound. r3s0 (DSP consolidation, +8.5%) and
r5s1 (divider extraction, +8.8%) both won by structurally separating a
non-overlapping cone from the main EX flow; this is the same pattern
applied to the branch comparator. Unlike r6s0 (barrel-shifter sharing
regressed) and r7s0/r11s0 (manual CSE regressed), this change does NOT
share or merge anything — it splits an existing monolithic cone into
two independently-placeable sub-cones, which matches the direction
that has empirically won on this GW2A LUT4 fabric.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Extract the branch condition evaluation (BEQ/BNE/BLT/BGE/BLTU/BGEU
6-way comparator plus the is_branch gate producing branch_taken) from
ex_stage.sv into a new rtl/branch_cmp.sv module with a minimal
interface: inputs rs1, rs2 (post-forward 32-bit), branch_op (3-bit),
is_branch (1-bit); outputs branch_cond (1-bit) and branch_taken
(1-bit). ex_stage.sv instantiates branch_cmp and consumes
branch_taken unchanged.

The branch_target = pc + imm adder STAYS in ex_stage (it shares the
pc+4 adder's pc input with other EX arithmetic and moving it would
risk the r7s0/r11s0 manual-CSE failure mode). JALR target and
misalign-fault detection also stay in ex_stage for the same reason.
Only the pure comparator cone — the structurally-distinct signed/
unsigned subtractor tree — is extracted.

Expected mechanism: nextpnr gets an independent placement opportunity
for the 6-way comparator cluster, which currently competes with the
ALU case-mux tree for routing resources inside the ex_stage flattened
always_comb region. On a Tang Nano 20K where the EX path is a known
binding constraint (see r10s1 -49% when widening EX, r11s1 -49% when
retiming across MEM/WB flop, r12s1 -34% when merging decoders into one
cone), splitting an orthogonal sub-cone out should reduce EX region
congestion without changing IPC, ALU area, DSP inference, pipeline
payload, or formal state.

No RVFI port changes. No pipeline payload changes. No invariant
changes. Only ex_stage.sv loses the 6-arm always_comb branch_cond
block; branch_cmp.sv gains it.

changes.description: New module with inputs rs1[31:0], rs2[31:0], branch_op[2:0], is_branch; outputs branch_cond, branch_taken. Contains the 6-way case that currently lives inline in ex_stage.sv (BR_BEQ/BNE/BLT/BGE/BLTU/BGEU) plus the is_branch && branch_cond -> branch_taken gate. Pure combinational, no flops. Verilator lint-clean.

Remove the inline branch_cond 6-way case and branch_taken assignment. Instantiate branch_cmp with the post-forward rs1/rs2, in.ctrl.branch_op, in.ctrl.is_branch, and consume the module's branch_taken output. branch_target = in.pc + in.imm stays inline (pc-adder sharing preserved per r7s0/r11s0 lessons). JALR target, misalign_fault, redirect assignment, and EX/MEM register all remain unchanged.

New cocotb unit test for branch_cmp: exercises all 6 branch_op values with representative signed/unsigned boundary cases (0==0, INT_MIN<INT_MAX signed, INT_MIN>INT_MAX unsigned, -1<0 signed, 0xFFFFFFFF>0 unsigned) plus is_branch=0 gating. Verifies branch_taken = is_branch && branch_cond bit-exactly.

New module with inputs rs1[31:0], rs2[31:0], branch_op[2:0], is_branch; outputs branch_cond, branch_taken. Contains the 6-way case that currently lives inline in ex_stage.sv (BR_BEQ/BNE/BLT/BGE/BLTU/BGEU) plus the is_branch && branch_cond -> branch_taken gate. Pure combinational, no flops. Verilator lint-clean.

JALR target, misalign_fault, redirect assignment, and EX/MEM register all remain unchanged.

Verifies branch_taken = is_branch && branch_cond bit-exactly.

lesson: - 2026-05-03 hyp-20260503-002-r13s1 (regression, -14.51%): Extracting the EX-stage 6-way branch comparator into a dedicated `branch_cmp.sv` module — no logic change, no payload change, no formal impact, purely a module-boundary split of the signed/unsigned subtractor cone away from the ALU case-mux tree — regressed ~15%, refuting the converse interpretation of r12s1 ("merging hurt, so splitting must help"); the r12s1 lesson actually said "two SMALLER modules give nextpnr two independent placement opportunities" in the context of pre-existing boundaries being valuable, NOT that introducing new boundaries helps, and Yosys's default flatten pass collapses submodules into the parent's synthesis scope anyway so the "independent placement" argument only applies when the module boundary is preserved through `keep_hierarchy` — the real r3s0/r5s1 wins both involved SEQUENTIAL isolation (hard DSP18 primitive / multi-cycle FSM stall), not mere combinational module extraction, so the sharpened meta-rule now spans r3s0/r5s1/r6s0/r7s0/r11s0/r12s0/r12s1/r13s0/r13s1: structural refactors pay ONLY when they (a) consolidate onto hard primitives synthesis cannot share on its own (DSP/BSRAM), (b) sequentialize cold-in-workload ops behind a pipeline-register boundary, or (c) eliminate an arithmetic carry/compare chain — combinational module splitting, boolean CSE, mux restatement, and comparator extraction are all net-negative on this Yosys-himbaechel GW2A flow because synth already optimizes them and any hand-intervention only perturbs post-tech-map placement of cones on binding paths; future EX-stage plays should treat the existing `ex_stage.sv` combinational structure as placement-optimal and pivot to IF-side or sequential transformations.

title: Retime branch-target adder from EX to ID (pre-compute pc+imm one stage early)
motivation:
The predictor slot has saturated for IF-side redirection: r2s2's stateless
JAL predictor (+5.87%) and r8s0's algebraic alignment guard (+5.99%) are
the only surviving wins. Every subsequent extension has hit one of three
distinct failure modes catalogued in LESSONS:
  (a) IF next_pc mux widening (r5s2, r8s2, r12s2 RAS variants; r4s2)
  (b) IF pc-adder chain lengthening (r10s2 B-imm shared adder, r12s2 DATA
      cone stretch — r8s2 meta-rule sharpened in r12s2 to "don't lengthen
      predicted-target DATA cone either")
  (c) formal symbolic-fetch coupling (r3s1/r3s2 BTFN, r9s0 EX-side rewrite)

r6s2 (-0%) and r7s2 (-0%) moved the full branch compare + adder to ID
and showed the IPC uplift is EXACTLY cancelled by the Fmax hit of a
widened ID comparator PLUS a new load-use stall case (BEQ/BNE consuming
an in-flight LOAD can't forward to ID).

What has NOT been tried: retiming ONLY the 32-bit branch-target ADDER
(pc+imm) from EX to ID, while keeping the comparator at EX. The key
distinction vs r6s2/r7s2 is that this retime does NOT introduce a new
load-use hazard (the comparator still consumes forwarded rs1/rs2 at EX
where forward paths already exist), and it does NOT widen the IF
next_pc mux or lengthen the IF adder chain (both saturated-failure
paths per r8s2/r10s2/r12s2). The retime targets the OTHER Fmax-binding
cross-stage arc: EX→IF redirect_target.

The current EX redirect_target path is:
  branch_target = in.pc + in.imm         // 32-bit ripple-carry add
  redirect_target = is_jump ? jump_target : branch_target
  -> fanout into if_stage.pc flop (across-stage combinational net)

This adder sits on a cross-stage path that competes with the existing
IF-local JAL-adder critical path for worst-case slack. Moving pc+imm
upstream across the ID/EX flop converts it from a live combinational
arc feeding IF's PC flop into a flop-output mux source.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Pre-compute branch_target = if_id_w.pc + imm_gen.imm combinationally
inside id_stage.sv, register it into a new 32-bit id_ex_t.branch_target
field, and re-point ex_stage.sv's branch_target to use in.branch_target
directly (no new add at EX). The branch comparator, JALR sum, and all
redirect logic stay at EX — only the target ADDER for direct branches
and JAL moves.

Concretely:
  - core_pkg.sv: add `logic [31:0] branch_target;` to id_ex_t (one
    new 32-bit field, ~1% of id_ex_t total width).
  - id_stage.sv: add `branch_target_comb = in.pc + imm;` in the
    combinational block; register it into reg_q.branch_target under
    the existing stall/flush gating.
  - ex_stage.sv: replace local `branch_target = in.pc + in.imm;` with
    `branch_target = in.branch_target;`. jump_target for JAL keeps
    using in.branch_target too (since jump_target = pc+imm for JAL,
    pre-computed value matches bit-exact). JALR's jalr_sum = rs1 +
    in.imm stays unchanged (needs post-forward rs1, cannot retime).
  - misalign guard rewrites accordingly: branch_target[1:0] check
    becomes in.branch_target[1:0] (bit-exact, no algebraic change).
  - No change to if_stage.sv, no change to hazard_unit.sv, no change
    to RVFI field provenance (in.pc + in.imm still uniquely determines
    the same value; regfile/memory taps untouched).

Bit-exact equivalence proof: id_ex.branch_target registered at cycle
N+1 captures if_id_w.pc + imm at cycle N's posedge. id_ex.pc and
id_ex.imm also capture their respective sources at the same posedge.
Therefore id_ex.branch_target === id_ex.pc + id_ex.imm at every cycle
after the first valid id_ex capture — identical to the current EX-
stage combinational computation. Formal should pass unchanged (no new
observable state, no symbolic-fetch coupling, no reg_write gate
changes — same failure mode that sank r9s1, but here no WB-side
layout change).

Expected Fmax effect: EX-stage's redirect_target cone shortens by one
32-bit ripple-carry adder (~5 LUT4 levels). ID-stage adds the same
adder, but ID's existing combinational cone (decoder + imm_gen + 32x32
regfile read with write-first bypass) is already settled well before
EX's combined (ALU cone + forward mux + compare + adder) path hits the
EX/MEM flop — r6s2's lesson explicitly notes that ID "previously had
slack vs EX" for the comparator; the same slack exists for the adder,
and the adder is lighter than the 6-funct3 compare tree r6s2 moved.
The 32-bit id_ex_t widening is the one clear area cost, but it is
smaller than r5s2's 33-bit RAS payload (which regressed -60%) because
(i) no new mux leg at IF, (ii) no new comparator at EX, (iii) no new
stall reason in hazard_unit, (iv) no formal state expansion. Net
expected: +2-4% if the EX redirect_target path is currently binding,
~0% if not.

Risk audit vs LESSONS:
  - r5s2/r8s2/r12s2 (payload widening): yes, 32-bit widening here, but
    mitigations above. Cost estimate: ~32 flops + routing, no
    comparator/mux.
  - r6s2/r7s2 (ID early resolve, ~0%): distinct — those moved the
    COMPARATOR and introduced a new load-use stall. This moves only
    the ADDER; comparator stays at EX with existing forward paths.
  - r7s0 (manual CSE across islands, -3%): distinct — this is a cross-
    stage retime across an existing flop, not intra-stage CSE. The
    pc+imm adder is CONSUMED combinationally at EX today; we move the
    consumption site to a registered source, which is structurally
    different from duplicating it.
  - r12s0 (absorb flop-adjacent 2:1 into case, -3%): distinct — no
    flop-adjacent mux is being absorbed; we're retiming pure
    arithmetic.
  - r9s1 (MEM/WB payload rename, broken formal): mitigated by keeping
    EX's existing branch_target signal name and just re-sourcing it
    from in.branch_target. All RVFI taps observe id_ex/ex_mem/mem_wb
    fields whose semantics are unchanged.

changes.description: Add `logic [31:0] branch_target;` field to id_ex_t struct so the pre-computed pc+imm value can flow from ID to EX.
Compute branch_target_comb = in.pc + imm combinationally; latch into reg_q.branch_target under the same stall/flush gating as other id_ex fields.

Replace local `branch_target = in.pc + in.imm;` with `branch_target = in.branch_target;`. Update jump_target for non-JALR jumps to reuse in.branch_target (bit-exact for JAL since JAL target = pc+imm). Update misalign_branch/jump guards to reference in.branch_target[1:0].
