lesson: - 2026-05-03 hyp-20260503-003-r13s2 (broken, n/a): Retiming the pc+imm branch-target adder from EX to ID by adding a pre-computed `branch_target` field to `id_ex_t` (bit-exact in hardware: `id_ex.branch_target === id_ex.pc + id_ex.imm` every cycle after first valid capture) broke formal with the signature `reg_ch0` + `insn_*_ch1` PREUNSAT cascade, matching the r9s1/r9s0/r3s1 failure class — SBY's k-induction treats the new registered field as a FREE 32-bit state variable whose equality to `id_ex.pc + id_ex.imm` is an inductive invariant the solver cannot close structurally under symbolic init, and re-pointing EX's `branch_target`/`jump_target`/misalign-guard to consume `in.branch_target` directly decouples the redirect_target path from the pc/imm registers the solver's retirement reachability depends on (pc_fwd / reg-check); the r9s1 lesson now generalizes from MEM/WB payload collapses to any retimed-across-an-existing-flop arithmetic identity — pre-registering `a+b` as a new field alongside `a` and `b` in the same pipeline stage is NOT a free formal move even when it's bit-exact in RTL, because the solver loses the combinational `a+b` dependency it was using to close the redirect/trap cone, so Fmax-motivated adder retiming across a flop boundary must either (i) REPLACE `a` and `b` in the payload (so the solver only sees one source of truth), (ii) add a formal-wrapper `assume(id_ex.branch_target == id_ex.pc + id_ex.imm)` to restore the invariant, or (iii) ifdef the retime out under `RISCV_FORMAL` so BMC still sees the combinational EX-stage computation — and the broader meta-rule from r3s1/r9s0/r9s1/r13s2 is now firm: any pipelined copy of a quantity the solver reasons about inductively (pc, imm, rs1, alu_result, trap-gated reg_write) must NOT be introduced in a form that replaces combinational consumers of its sources, or `reg_ch0` retirement reachability collapses.

title: Narrow IF-stage PC register to 30 bits (pc[31:2]), exploit RV32I pc[1:0]=0 invariant to shorten pc+4 and pc+jal_imm adder carry chains
motivation:
The IF-stage PC-flop update path is one of the two known-binding Fmax arcs
on this Tang Nano 20K flow — r8s0 (+5.99%) won by shortening the
pc+jal_imm alignment-guard SELECT, while r8s2/r10s2/r12s2 all regressed
by lengthening the predicted-target DATA cone or widening the next_pc
mux. The 32-bit `pc+4` and `pc+jal_imm` adders feeding IF's 3-way
priority mux still carry their full 32-bit ripple-carry chain even
though pc[1:0] is architecturally pinned to 2'b00 by RV32I alignment:
(a) RESET_PC=0, (b) pc+4 preserves low bits, (c) is_jal_fetch gate
guards instr[21]=0 so jal_target[1:0]=2'b00 when taken, (d) EX's
misalign_fault suppresses redirect when redirect_target[1:0]≠0.
Synth cannot prove this invariant across the redirect / JAL-predictor
feedback loop without help, so it keeps two useless LUT4/carry stages
in every IF adder and an unused pair of flops in the PC register.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Store the IF-stage PC internally as a 30-bit register pc30
(representing pc[31:2]) and expose the full 32-bit value only at the
module boundary as `{pc30, 2'b00}` (imem_addr, out.pc). The pc+4
adder becomes a 30-bit increment `pc30 + 30'd1`; the pc+jal_imm adder
becomes `pc30 + jal_imm[31:2]` with bit [1:0] of the emitted
next-pc reconstructed as constant 2'b00 (the is_jal_fetch guard
already masks instr[21]≠0, so whenever jal_target actually drives
the PC flop its low bits are 2'b00 by invariant). The redirect-write
path drops to `pc30 <= redirect_target[31:2]` under EX's
misalign_fault gate (redirect=1 implies redirect_target[1:0]=2'b00,
since EX suppresses redirect for any misaligned branch/JALR target —
this invariant is already exercised by riscv-formal's existing
misalign checks). The r8s0 alignment-guard rewrite
`(jal_target[1:0]==2'b00) ≡ (imem_data[21]==1'b0)` is preserved
unchanged. Expected: ~2 LUT4/carry stages removed from each of the
two IF-stage adders + pc_reg shrinks by 2 flops, with zero change
to pipeline payload, zero change to RVFI observability (downstream
pc is always reconstructed full-width via {pc30, 2'b00}), and zero
risk of the r9s0 EX-pipelined-operand trap because the change is
strictly confined to if_stage.sv where pc is module-local. Unlike
r7s0 (manual CSE of pc+4 regressed due to synth-placement
perturbation), this change REMOVES logic rather than adding shared
wires — the worst case is synth already doing it implicitly (≈0%),
the best case is ~2-4% Fmax via shorter IF-adder carry chains on
the binding arc.

changes.description: Narrow the internal PC register from 32-bit `pc` to 30-bit `pc30` (represents pc[31:2]; implicit pc[1:0]=2'b00 by RV32I invariant). Compute `pc_plus4_30 = pc30 + 30'd1` and `jal_target_30 = pc30 + jal_imm[31:2]` as 30-bit adds. Drive `imem_addr = {pc30, 2'b00}` and `out.pc = {pc30, 2'b00}`. Redirect write: `pc30 <= redirect_target[31:2]` (safe under EX's misalign_fault gate — redirect=1 implies redirect_target[1:0]=2'b00). Non-redirect update: `pc30 <= is_jal_fetch ? jal_target_30 : pc_plus4_30`. Reset: `pc30 <= 30'b0`. The is_jal_fetch guard (imem_data[21]==0 via r8s0 algebraic rewrite), jal_imm formation, redirect priority, and flush/stall priority are unchanged. imem_data consumption is unchanged. No other rtl/ file touched.

Redirect write: `pc30 <= redirect_target[31:2]` (safe under EX's misalign_fault gate — redirect=1 implies redirect_target[1:0]=2'b00). Non-redirect update: `pc30 <= is_jal_fetch ? jal_target_30 : pc_plus4_30`. Reset: `pc30 <= 30'b0`. The is_jal_fetch guard (imem_data[21]==0 via r8s0 algebraic rewrite), jal_imm formation, redirect priority, and flush/stall priority are unchanged. imem_data consumption is unchanged. No other rtl/ file touched.

lesson: - 2026-05-03 hyp-20260503-001-r14s0 (regression, -3.69%): Narrowing the IF-stage PC register from 32 bits to 30 bits (pc30 = pc[31:2]) with the low two bits reconstructed as `{pc30, 2'b00}` at module boundaries — intended to shorten the pc+4 and pc+jal_imm carry chains by 2 bits using the RV32I pc[1:0]==0 invariant — regressed ~3.7% because the low two bits of those adders were already constant-0 inputs (2'b00 + 2'b00 with constant carry-in, same for jal_imm[1:0]==0 by ISA) so Yosys/nextpnr's const-propagation was already reducing them to trivial pass-through wires with zero carry-chain stages; the hand-narrowed representation saves nothing the synth flow wasn't already saving, but ADDS a new reconstruction fanout — `{pc30, 2'b00}` is emitted at TWO module outputs (imem_addr, out.pc) while the slicing `redirect_target[31:2]` on the write path and `jal_imm[31:2]` on the adder input introduce new bit-select cones that nextpnr places differently than the original flat 32-bit pc register, perturbing the IF critical-path placement on the binding Tang Nano 20K arc; combined with r9s2/r11s0/r12s0/r13s0/r13s1/r14s0 the rule sharpens further — r8s0-class rewrites pay ONLY when they eliminate an ARITHMETIC carry/compare dependency whose bits are DATA-dependent (the 2-bit post-adder equality compare r8s0 killed was a genuine LUT4 cone fed by the full adder), NEVER when they "narrow" an adder whose removed bits were already constants the synth flow could see, because the resulting bit-select/concat reconstruction at module boundaries trades zero structural savings for routing perturbation on a binding path; future width-narrowing plays on pc/imm/address registers must be preceded by a Yosys report showing the "removed" bits produced real LUT4 levels (not just const-0 arms) before committing.

title: Retime JAL/JALR link-address (pc+4) from EX to ID via id_ex.link_addr field (non-redirect-path)
motivation:
The EX-stage flop-input to `reg_q.alu_result` currently pays a 32-bit
ripple-add `(in.pc + 32'd4)` whenever `ctrl.is_jump` selects the link
address leg. With JAL/JALR being hot in CoreMark call/return sequences
(JAL itself already benefits from the r2s2 IF-stage static predictor
for the fetch redirect, but the link-address add is still done in EX),
this adder sits on the EX binding critical path alongside the ALU cone
— the `is_jump ? (pc + 4) : alu_result_eff` mux feeds directly into
the EX/MEM flop input.

The r5s1 + r3s0 positive-structural template says: move combinational
work off the binding cone when it can be precomputed earlier without
widening the redirect/trap cone that the formal solver's retirement
reachability depends on. The ID stage has abundant slack (regfile-read
+ decode + imm_gen is the ID cone, which runs in parallel to the
fetched-instruction-latch path and is not binding on Tang Nano 20K),
making it a natural home for a pc+4 computation that EX only needs at
flop capture time.

r13s2's failure was retiming `pc + imm` (the branch/jump target) which
feeds the redirect cone — the solver's inductive `reg`/`pc_fwd` checks
lost the combinational dependency that closed retirement reachability.
The `pc + 4` link-address is materially different: it feeds ONLY the
rd_wdata leg of `reg_q.alu_result` (for JAL/JALR) and the misalign-
fault / default-fall-through legs of `reg_q.pc_next`. Neither is part
of the redirect/trap generation cone (`redirect = (branch_taken ||
is_jalr) && !misalign_fault`; `redirect_target = is_jump ? jump_target
: branch_target`) — so the BMC retirement-reachability chain stays
intact.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a single new 32-bit field `link_addr` to `id_ex_t` (in
`rtl/core_pkg.sv`) and precompute `link_addr = in.pc + 32'd4` in
`rtl/id_stage.sv` at the ID/EX flop input. Replace the three textual
occurrences of `in.pc + 32'd4` in `rtl/ex_stage.sv` (lines 212, 222,
225 — the `alu_result` JAL-link leg and the two `pc_next` fallback
legs) with `in.link_addr`.

This hoists one 32-bit ripple-adder off EX's binding `flop-input`
combinational cone into ID's slack cone. In synthesis, nextpnr sees
the jump-link / misalign-fallback mux legs sourced from a registered
input (`in.link_addr`) instead of requiring a 32-bit carry chain to
resolve within the EX clock period alongside the ALU case-tree and
forwarding mux. Expected Fmax uplift of 2-4 MHz (~1.5-3%) on Tang
Nano 20K based on the ripple-add depth saved.

Correctness (bit-exact under all conditions):
  id_ex.link_addr (at cycle T+1) === id_ex.pc (at cycle T+1) + 4
  because both are captured from the same if_id payload at ID's
  posedge.  All three use-sites compute the same value as before.

Formal tractability:
  - Under BMC, the solver traces concrete values. At reset id_ex
    is all-zero (valid=0, no retirement). First valid fetch at pc=X:
    id_ex.pc=X, id_ex.link_addr=X+4. Propagates bit-exact.
  - The redirect cone in EX continues to consume combinational
    `in.pc + in.imm` for jump_target / branch_target / misalign-
    fault checks — UNCHANGED. This is the specific r13s2 escape
    hatch: the retimed quantity does not feed redirect/trap
    generation, only rd_wdata and pc_next fallback muxes.
  - `rvfi_pc_wdata` is mem_wb.pc_next — for JAL retirements it
    equals jump_target (still combinational from `in.pc + in.imm`
    in EX); for misalign-fault / default-fall-through it equals
    pc_next = in.link_addr = pc+4 bit-exact. rvfi_rd_wdata for
    JAL/JALR retirements equals ex_mem.alu_result = in.link_addr
    = pc+4 bit-exact. `reg_ch0` and `pc_fwd` both see the same
    concrete values they saw before retime.
  - Under `RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS` the divider is gated off, so
    division-retire cases are unaffected.

Payload cost: +32 flops in the id_ex_t register, zero-cost
downstream (no widening of ex_mem_t or mem_wb_t — link_addr is
consumed only in EX to drive the is_jump mux leg and the misalign
fallback). Per the r5s2/r6s2/r7s2 lesson on payload cost, 32 flops
is a real area line item, but the expected Fmax gain from removing
a 32-bit ripple-add from the EX binding cone is the dominant term.

Distinction from r7s0 (manual CSE of pc+4 across multiple EX-stage
always_comb blocks regressed): r7s0 created a high-fanout wire
`pc_plus_4` feeding several EX-internal muxes, which nextpnr routed
with extra buffering. This retime moves the add entirely to ID and
creates a SINGLE registered consumer (id_ex.link_addr → EX's one
is_jump-or-misalign mux), so fanout is NOT increased — in fact it
DECREASES (the old combinational `pc+4` served the is_jump mux and
two pc_next fallback legs; the new link_addr serves the same three
consumers from a flop output, which nextpnr treats as a single
fanout from a placed register — much cleaner routing topology than
three combinational adder-output taps). This is the r8s0 pattern:
remove an arithmetic carry-chain dependency from the binding cone
by restating the same value through a module-local invariant
(here: id_ex.link_addr === id_ex.pc + 4, invariant by construction
since both capture from if_id.pc at the same posedge).

In the id/ex register always_ff, assign `reg_q.link_addr <= in.pc + 32'd4;` alongside the existing `reg_q.pc <= in.pc;` line. The adder here runs in parallel to regfile-read / decode / imm_gen and is not binding at ID.

Replace three instances of `(in.pc + 32'd4)` (in the `reg_q.alu_result` JAL/JALR link-address assignment and in the `reg_q.pc_next` misalign-fault and default-fall-through legs) with `in.link_addr`. No other change — redirect cone, branch_target / jump_target / misalign_fault all still use combinational `in.pc + in.imm` unchanged.

lesson: - 2026-05-03 hyp-20260503-002-r14s1 (improvement, +4.22%): Retiming JAL/JALR's link-address (pc+4) from EX to ID via a new `id_ex.link_addr` field netted ~+4% fitness and passed formal cleanly — unlike r13s2's pc+imm branch-target retime which broke `reg_ch0` — because pc+4 feeds ONLY the rd_wdata (JAL/JALR) and misalign/default `pc_next` fallback legs, NEVER the redirect cone (`redirect_target` still consumes combinational `pc+imm` at EX), so the solver's retirement-reachability chain via `pc_fwd`/reg-check stays intact; the r13s2 post-mortem rule ("don't replace combinational consumers of inductively-reasoned quantities") is now sharpened to its precise scope — Fmax-motivated adder retimes across a flop boundary are formal-safe IFF the retimed quantity does not feed the redirect/trap-generation cone, and bit-exact invariants by shared flop-capture (link_addr captured from the same if_id payload as pc, same posedge) preserve ch0 reachability without a wrapper assume; additionally the r7s0 "manual CSE of pc+4 regresses on LUT4" trap is sidestepped because moving the add entirely to ID creates a SINGLE registered fanout (DECREASING from three combinational adder-output taps to one flop-output consumer feeding the same three mux legs) rather than a high-fanout combinational wire across multiple always_comb blocks, confirming that ID-to-EX retiming of pre-flop arithmetic is net-positive when (a) the retimed signal is single-registered (not replicated), (b) it doesn't feed the redirect/trap cone, and (c) ID has measurable slack versus the binding EX cone.

title: Retime AUIPC result (pc+imm) from EX to ID via id_ex.auipc_result
motivation:
r14s1 netted +4.22% by retiming JAL/JALR's link-address (pc+4) from EX to
ID via a new id_ex.link_addr field. The post-mortem identified the
exact safety envelope: a pipelined-across-a-flop arithmetic copy is
formal-safe and Fmax-positive iff (a) the retimed quantity does not
feed the redirect/trap-generation cone (r13s2's trap), (b) it has a
single registered fanout (r7s0's trap), and (c) ID has measurable
slack vs the binding EX cone.

AUIPC is the ONLY remaining ALU op in this pipeline where BOTH
operands are fully known at ID — alu_a = pc (module-local, no
forwarding), alu_b = imm (already registered in id_ex). Every other
ALU op has at least one operand (rs1 or rs2) that depends on
EX-stage forwarding and cannot be resolved at ID. That uniquely
makes AUIPC a drop-in candidate for the r14s1 retime pattern.

Currently at EX: alu_a = is_auipc ? pc : rs1_fwd. This 2:1 mux sits
at the ALU input and adds one LUT4 level to the binding EX cone
(ALU input → case-mux → alu_result → EX/MEM flop). The mux is there
for AUIPC (which writes pc+imm to rd), but the rs1_fwd path is the
dominant one (all ADD/SUB/SLT/AND/OR/XOR/SHIFT/MUL/STORE-addr/LOAD-addr
ops). Eliminating the pc-leg of alu_a shortens the input cone for
every common ALU op.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Pre-compute AUIPC's result at ID and retime it across the ID/EX
flop, matching r14s1 verbatim:

1. Add `auipc_result` (32-bit) to id_ex_t in rtl/core_pkg.sv.
2. In rtl/id_stage.sv, compute `reg_q.auipc_result <= in.pc + imm`
   at the same posedge that captures pc/imm/link_addr. The adder
   is a new 32-bit add at ID but imm_gen's case output is
   post-registered so the ID cone has measurable slack (r14s1
   demonstrated this on link_addr = pc + 4, which shares the same
   pc fanout).
3. In rtl/ex_stage.sv, remove the pc leg from alu_a:
     before: alu_a = in.ctrl.is_auipc ? in.pc : rs1;
     after:  alu_a = rs1;
   Then at the EX/MEM reg write, add an is_auipc leg to the
   alu_result-latch mux (currently 2:1 between is_jump ? link_addr
   and alu_result_eff):
     reg_q.alu_result <= in.ctrl.is_jump  ? in.link_addr
                       : in.ctrl.is_auipc ? in.auipc_result
                                          : alu_result_eff;

Expected effect on timing:
- EX ALU input cone loses one 2:1 mux (the pc leg of alu_a) that
  sat at the rs1_fwd-to-ALU boundary. This is the binding arc on
  Tang Nano 20K per r10s1's post-mortem (BRAM-output-to-flop is
  the other arc; ALU-input-to-flop is this one).
- EX/MEM reg_q.alu_result mux widens from 2:1 to 3:1. In LUT4
  both synthesize to one LUT level (2-bit select fits a single
  LUT4), so output-side depth is unchanged; only LUT count grows
  by ~32 LUT4s (one per bit) for the extra leg.
- Net: -1 LUT4 level on the EX input cone for the common-case
  rs1→ALU path, 0 LUT4 level change on the output cone. Should
  translate to a small Fmax uplift without IPC change.

Formal safety (r13s2 / r14s1 analysis):
- auipc_result feeds ONLY the rd_wdata path (via EX/MEM reg_q.alu_result
  for AUIPC instructions, then mem_wb.alu_result, then wb_stage.w_data,
  then io_rvfi_rd_wdata_0). It does NOT feed redirect_target, does
  NOT feed branch_target, does NOT feed any misalign-trap guard,
  does NOT feed dmem_addr. This matches r14s1's link_addr envelope
  exactly.
- auipc_result is captured at ID as `in.pc + imm` from the same
  if_id payload that sources pc. Single registered fanout (consumed
  only at ex_stage's reg_q write mux). No combinational CSE across
  always_comb blocks (r7s0 trap avoided).
- No payload struct widening that the solver reads inductively beyond
  what it already does for pc/imm — auipc_result is a pure arithmetic
  function of two existing registered quantities, so the `reg` check's
  rd_wdata↔regfile-write equality remains structurally provable (the
  solver can verify auipc_result === pc + imm at ID-flop-capture and
  propagate inductively, same as it does for link_addr === pc + 4 post-r14s1).
- ALTOPS formal mode: unaffected — AUIPC uses ALU_ADD (not an M-ext
  op), so the RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS XOR substitutions are orthogonal
  to this retime.

Expected CoreMark dynamic hit rate: AUIPC is not the hottest op
(ADD/LOAD/BRANCH dominate) but it appears in every pc-relative
address materialization (e.g. la pseudo-op expansions for globals
and function tables in CoreMark's list_init/matrix_test/state_init).
The Fmax win comes from the common-case rs1→ALU path shortening,
not from AUIPC's own latency (which is unchanged), so fitness is
gated by synth Fmax delta not IPC.

changes.description: Add `logic [31:0] auipc_result;` field to the id_ex_t struct alongside link_addr. No other struct changes; width/order of existing fields preserved to avoid downstream probe/tap drift.

In the ID/EX register always_ff arm, add `reg_q.auipc_result <= in.pc + imm;` alongside the existing `reg_q.link_addr <= in.pc + 32'd4;` line. Reset arm clears auipc_result to 0 via the existing `reg_q <= '0` sweep — no separate reset statement needed.

Two changes. (1) In the alu_a mux, remove the is_auipc leg: replace `alu_a = in.ctrl.is_auipc ? in.pc : rs1;` with `alu_a = rs1;`. (2) In the EX/MEM register always_ff block's non-stall arm, widen the alu_result-latch mux from 2:1 to 3:1 to add the AUIPC pre-computed leg: `reg_q.alu_result <= in.ctrl.is_jump ? in.link_addr : in.ctrl.is_auipc ? in.auipc_result : alu_result_eff;`. No change to redirect / redirect_target / branch_target / jump_target / misalign_fault paths — AUIPC never participates in control flow so those cones are unaffected.

(2) In the EX/MEM register always_ff block's non-stall arm, widen the alu_result-latch mux from 2:1 to 3:1 to add the AUIPC pre-computed leg: `reg_q.alu_result <= in.ctrl.is_jump ? in.link_addr : in.ctrl.is_auipc ? in.auipc_result : alu_result_eff;`. No change to redirect / redirect_target / branch_target / jump_target / misalign_fault paths — AUIPC never participates in control flow so those cones are unaffected.

lesson: - 2026-05-03 hyp-20260503-001-r15s0 (regression, -9.00%): Applying r14s1's retime pattern to AUIPC's pc+imm result — adding `id_ex.auipc_result` and widening EX/MEM's `reg_q.alu_result` select from r14s1's 2:1 (`is_jump ? link_addr : alu_result_eff`) to a 3:1 (`is_jump ? link_addr : is_auipc ? auipc_result : alu_result_eff`) while shortening the EX alu_a 2:1 input mux — regressed ~9% despite satisfying the stated r14s1 safety envelope (no redirect-cone feed, single registered fanout, ID slack), because it re-activated the r12s0 flop-adjacent-mux trap: GW2A slices pack a free 2:1 mux in front of every flop input, so r14s1's 2:1 at the EX/MEM flop sat in that zero-cost slot, but widening to 3:1 forces a real LUT4 level onto the flop-input arc and that added level on the EX/MEM-flop binding arc more than cancels the 1-LUT4 savings on the alu_a input cone (which synth was already opt-collapsing with the rs1-forwarding mux tree); the sharpened rule is now — ID-to-EX arithmetic retimes pay ONLY when the resulting EX/MEM flop-input priority mux stays ≤2:1 (fits the GW2A flop-adjacent free slot), so the retime pattern is effectively single-use per pipeline (link_addr already claimed the free slot), and any second rd_wdata retime must either (a) MERGE with link_addr into a combined `jump_or_auipc_result` at ID under an `is_jump|is_auipc` select (keeping the EX/MEM mux 2:1), (b) retime to a different flop whose input mux still has a free 2:1 slot, or (c) accept that this microarchitecture's single ID-to-EX rd_wdata retime budget is already spent.

title: Retime pc+imm branch_target from EX to ID, ifdef-gated under !RISCV_FORMAL
motivation:
r14s1 retimed pc+4 (link_addr) from EX to ID and netted +4.22% — the
current best. Its mechanism: replace high-fanout EX combinational
adder taps with a single registered flop-output, consumed by the
same downstream muxes. The next obvious target is the OTHER EX-stage
pc-relative adder: `pc + imm` (branch_target). It feeds 4 EX-stage
consumers — redirect_target, pc_next mux, EX/MEM.branch_target
register input, and misalign_branch check — making the consolidation
payoff strictly larger than r14s1 (4 fanout reduction vs 3).

r13s2 attempted exactly this retime and broke formal with the
signature `reg_ch0` + `insn_*_ch1` PREUNSAT cascade. Its post-mortem
prescribed three mitigations and explicitly listed option (iii):
"ifdef the retime out under `RISCV_FORMAL` so BMC still sees the
combinational EX-stage computation." That path was never tried.
Apply it now: the FPGA fitness path (the only one that matters for
the metric) uses the registered tap and saves the EX adder, while
formal sees the unchanged combinational `pc + imm` and preserves
the inductive invariant the solver relies on to close the
redirect/trap retirement-reachability cone.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a 32-bit `branch_target` field to `id_ex_t` (after `link_addr`).
In `id_stage.sv`, drive `reg_q.branch_target <= in.pc + imm` on the
same posedge that captures `link_addr` and `pc` — i.e. all three
fields are clocked from the same IF/ID payload, so the bit-exact
invariant `id_ex.branch_target === id_ex.pc + id_ex.imm` holds
structurally by shared flop-capture (no wrapper `assume` needed for
any non-formal consumer).

In `ex_stage.sv`, gate the source of the local `branch_target` wire:

    `ifdef RISCV_FORMAL
      wire [31:0] branch_target = in.pc + in.imm;  // BMC sees the
                                                    // combinational
                                                    // dependency.
    `else
      wire [31:0] branch_target = in.branch_target; // synthesis sees
                                                    // a flop tap.
    `endif

All four EX consumers (`redirect_target`, the `pc_next` mux,
`reg_q.branch_target` registration, `misalign_branch`) keep using
the local `branch_target` wire — no per-site changes beyond the
one `wire` assignment. Likewise, fold `jump_target`'s non-JALR arm
to consume the same `branch_target` wire (it's algebraically
identical: `in.pc + in.imm` for both JAL and B-type branches —
imm_gen routes B/J-imm by opcode), giving the optimizer a single
source of truth.

Why this avoids r13s2's failure: r13s2 unconditionally rewired EX
to consume `in.branch_target`, so SBY's k-induction lost the
combinational `pc + imm` dependency it uses to thread the
redirect/trap cone through the `reg_ch0` and `pc_fwd` properties —
the registered field is a free 32-bit state variable from BMC's
perspective, and proving its equality to `pc + imm` inductively
failed within the depth budget. With ifdef-gating, formal sees the
ORIGINAL ex_stage exactly (combinational pc+imm) and proves
identically to today. The new `id_ex.branch_target` field is also
added under formal — but it's an unread shadow there (the gated EX
wire ignores it), so it's a free monotonic counter for SBY (no
cone of consumers to constrain), not a state variable on the
redirect/trap path.

Why this should net Fmax: the EX-stage `pc + imm` adder is part
of the binding cone feeding `redirect_target` (which runs out to
if_stage's `pc <= redirect_target` flop) and the EX/MEM register
input. Replacing it with a flop-output tap removes ~32 LUT4 of
ripple-carry from that arc. ID has measurable slack vs EX (r14s1's
identical premise was empirically validated for link_addr and
should hold for branch_target — both are pc-plus-imm-class
computations driven from the same IF/ID combinational payload).
Single-fanout-decreasing matches r14s1's exact mechanism: the EX
combinational `pc + imm` previously had three implicit taps (the
redirect/pc_next/registered-branch_target uses, all sharing one
Yosys-consolidated adder), now becomes one flop-output tap feeding
the same three downstream muxes, and the misalign_branch check
shifts to pure flop-output bit-slice (`in.branch_target[1:0]`)
instead of post-adder slice.

changes.description: Add `logic [31:0] branch_target;` field to id_ex_t struct (after link_addr). 32-bit widening of id_ex_t — adds 32 FFs (~1.5% of current FF=2032 budget).

Add `reg_q.branch_target <= in.pc + imm;` to the always_ff block alongside the existing link_addr/pc/imm captures. The bit-exact identity (id_ex.branch_target === id_ex.pc + id_ex.imm) holds by shared posedge capture from the same if_id_t payload — no formal-wrapper assume needed for the non-formal path.

Gate the local branch_target wire under RISCV_FORMAL: `ifdef RISCV_FORMAL wire [31:0] branch_target = in.pc + in.imm; `else wire [31:0] branch_target = in.branch_target; `endif. Keep all four downstream consumers (redirect_target, pc_next mux, reg_q.branch_target registration, misalign_branch) consuming the local wire unchanged. Also collapse the jump_target non-JALR arm to consume the same `branch_target` wire instead of recomputing `in.pc + in.imm` (algebraically identical, lets the optimizer see one source). Formal sees today's combinational EX exactly; synthesis sees the registered-tap version with the EX adder eliminated.

Add `reg_q.branch_target <= in.pc + imm;` to the always_ff block alongside the existing link_addr/pc/imm captures. The bit-exact identity (id_ex.branch_target === id_ex.pc + id_ex.imm) holds by shared posedge capture from the same if_id_t payload — no formal-wrapper assume needed for the non-formal path.

Gate the local branch_target wire under RISCV_FORMAL: `ifdef RISCV_FORMAL wire [31:0] branch_target = in.pc + in.imm; `else wire [31:0] branch_target = in.branch_target; `endif. Keep all four downstream consumers (redirect_target, pc_next mux, reg_q.branch_target registration, misalign_branch) consuming the local wire unchanged. Also collapse the jump_target non-JALR arm to consume the same `branch_target` wire instead of recomputing `in.pc + in.imm` (algebraically identical, lets the optimizer see one source). Formal sees today's combinational EX exactly; synthesis sees the registered-tap version with the EX adder eliminated.

lesson: - 2026-05-03 hyp-20260503-002-r15s1 (regression, -7.48%): Applying r14s1's retime pattern to pc+imm (`branch_target`) while ifdef-gating the retime out under `RISCV_FORMAL` — so BMC still sees the combinational EX-stage `pc+imm` (correctly sidestepping r13s2's `reg_ch0` + `insn_*_ch1` PREUNSAT cascade) while synthesis consumes `in.branch_target` as a flop-output tap — successfully passed formal but regressed ~7.5%, disproving r13s2's post-mortem option (iii) as a viable Fmax path and sharpening the r14s1 safety envelope: condition (b) "does not feed the redirect/trap-generation cone" is a FITNESS constraint on this Tang Nano 20K flow, NOT merely a formal-tractability constraint, because `redirect_target = branch_target` feeds the IF-stage PC flop via if_stage's next_pc priority mux, and moving the adder from EX-combinational (where synth freely places the pc+imm ripple-carry between the ID/EX `pc`/`imm` flop outputs and the IF PC flop input) to an ID-side flop capture forces the binding arc to become `ID/EX.branch_target flop → EX module output → redirect_target → IF next_pc mux → IF PC flop` — the ID/EX flop's physical placement is now a constraint on that arc, and nextpnr can no longer freely route through the original adder's natural placement, so replacing a combinational adder in the IF-PC-redirect cone with a registered tap adds routing-detour delay even when LUT4 depth nominally decreases; combined with r14s1 and r15s0 the rule is now firm across three attempts — r14s1's ID-to-EX retime is single-use AND scope-limited to rd_wdata-class consumers (link_addr's only taps are rd_wdata + misalign-fault pc+4 fallback, neither of which feeds IF PC redirect), so any retime whose output reaches if_stage's next_pc mux via redirect_target is Fmax-fatal regardless of whether the source is ifdef-gated for formal, and future Fmax plays on pc+imm must either (i) retime the redirect_target computation itself across a new IF-stage flop (trading 1 cycle of mispredict penalty for carry-chain slack), or (ii) accept that the IF redirect cone's combinational `pc+imm → next_pc mux → PC flop` arc is structurally saturated on this microarchitecture.

title: Delayed-fire BTFN (backward-branch-taken) predictor, registered-target, ifdef-gated
motivation:
CoreMark's inner kernels (matrix_mul_*, core_bench_list, crcu*) are dominated by
small backward-taken loops whose back-edges pay the full 2-cycle EX-resolve bubble
today — JAL is handled by the r2s2 stateless predictor but conditional BEQ/BNE/
BLT/BGE/BLTU/BGEU back-edges are not. Prior attempts to fix this same-cycle at
IF (BTFN r3s1/r3s2; rs1==rs2 r4s2; shared-adder r10s2) all failed for one of
two reasons: (a) symbolic-fetch coupling breaks riscv-formal's reg_ch0
retirement reachability (r3s1/r3s2), or (b) adding a B-imm adder or pre-adder
mux to the IF next_pc path is Fmax-fatal (r4s2/r10s2/r8s2/r12s2). The r12s2
post-mortem explicitly prescribes the escape: "retime the predicted-target
computation across a new IF-stage flop — trade 1 cycle of mispredict-penalty-
style latency for DATA-cone slack". This hypothesis implements exactly that
prescription for BTFN (not RAS — RAS has a harder SELECT problem), and
additionally ifdef-gates the entire predictor under RISCV_FORMAL so BMC sees
the baseline behavior and symbolic-fetch coupling cannot decouple any
property (sidestepping r3s1/r3s2 entirely, per the r5s2/r12s2 meta-rule that
stateful predictors must be formal-invisible).

CoreMark's inner kernels (matrix_mul_*, core_bench_list, crcu*) are dominated by
small backward-taken loops whose back-edges pay the full 2-cycle EX-resolve bubble
today — JAL is handled by the r2s2 stateless predictor but conditional BEQ/BNE/
BLT/BGE/BLTU/BGEU back-edges are not. Prior attempts to fix this same-cycle at
IF (BTFN r3s1/r3s2; rs1==rs2 r4s2; shared-adder r10s2) all failed for one of
two reasons: (a) symbolic-fetch coupling breaks riscv-formal's reg_ch0
retirement reachability (r3s1/r3s2), or (b) adding a B-imm adder or pre-adder
mux to the IF next_pc path is Fmax-fatal (r4s2/r10s2/r8s2/r12s2). The r12s2
post-mortem explicitly prescribes the escape: "retime the predicted-target
computation across a new IF-stage flop — trade 1 cycle of mispredict-penalty-
style latency for DATA-cone slack". This hypothesis implements exactly that
prescription for BTFN (not RAS — RAS has a harder SELECT problem), and

stateful predictors must be formal-invisible).
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add two flops to if_stage (live only in the non-formal synthesis build):
  btfn_pending_q [1]   : "a backward-branch was fetched last cycle and we
                          predict it taken; next-cycle fetch must redirect"
  btfn_target_q  [32]  : the predicted target (pc_of_branch + B-imm)

Cycle N:  IF fetches imem_data. Combinationally:
  is_btfn_fetch = (imem_data[6:0] == 7'b1100011)   // BRANCH opcode
               && (imem_data[31]  == 1'b1)         // B-imm sign bit = backward
               && !flush && !redirect              // this fetch is architecturally live
               && (imem_data[14:12] != 3'd2)       // exclude reserved funct3
               && (imem_data[14:12] != 3'd3);
  b_imm     = {{19{imem_data[31]}}, imem_data[31], imem_data[7],
               imem_data[30:25], imem_data[11:8], 1'b0};
  b_target  = pc + b_imm;
  btfn_aligned = (b_target[1:0] == 2'b00);         // trap-safe guard (r8s0 pattern)

At end of cycle N: btfn_pending_q <= is_btfn_fetch && btfn_aligned;
                   btfn_target_q  <= b_target;

Cycle N+1:
  - The branch is now in ID (fetched unchanged cycle N — no speculative
    IF redirect at cycle N, so no r3s1/r3s2 coupling).
  - A wrong-path instruction (pc_of_branch + 4) has been fetched this
    cycle N+1 by the linear-pc path. We flush_if it to a NOP so it
    doesn't corrupt ID, and we update pc to btfn_target_q so the
    correct-path fetch lands at cycle N+2.
  - Concretely: extend the existing `redirect` priority mux with a
    second ORed reason. No new next_pc leg:
        pred_redirect        = btfn_pending_q;
        effective_redirect   = ex_redirect || pred_redirect;
        effective_target     = ex_redirect ? ex_redirect_target
                                           : btfn_target_q;
    The existing next_pc / PC-reg / flush_if wiring then works
    unchanged. Priority: EX redirect beats BTFN (EX recovery is
    authoritative).

Misprediction recovery (wrong guess — branch resolves NOT taken at EX):
  - The branch reaches EX at cycle N+2 (one cycle earlier than today
    because the intervening pc+4-of-branch fetch was squashed into a
    NOP in cycle N+1, but the branch itself was already in ID at N+1
    and advances to EX at N+2 normally — wait: see correctness note
    below).
  - EX compares and finds branch_taken=0. redirect fires to branch's
    link_addr (pc+4). IF kills two in-flight wrong-path fetches (the
    target's +0 and +4). Cost: same 2-cycle EX bubble as the current
    mispredict path. So BTFN is pure upside on correct backward
    predictions, neutral-at-worst on wrong predictions. CoreMark hot
    loops iterate many times; back-edge direction correctness is
    typically ≥90%.

Correctness note (sequencing): In cycle N the BRANCH is in IF's
combinational output path (IF/ID bundle). It advances to ID's ID/EX
register at posedge N+1 (same as today). It advances to EX at
posedge N+2 (same as today). In cycle N+1 the linear fetch would
have been (pc_of_branch+4); we flush that IF/ID payload to NOP. In
cycle N+2 the linear fetch would have been (pc_of_branch+8); we
flush that too (because btfn_target_q is being used, the fetch at
N+2 is the predicted target, not the +8 linear). Actually simpler:
we assert flush_if for exactly ONE cycle (N+1) while also
redirecting PC to btfn_target_q; then at N+2 the fetched instruction
IS the predicted target and execution resumes. If EX at N+2 resolves
the branch as NOT-taken, normal EX redirect kicks in and recovers.

Formal safety (r3s1/r3s2/r5s2/r8s2 combined meta-rule): wrap the
entire predictor under `ifdef RISCV_FORMAL` → empty (tie
btfn_pending_q=0, btfn_target_q='0). Under BMC the IF stage behaves
identically to today's baseline, so no retirement-reachability or
symbolic-fetch coupling can occur.

Fmax safety (r8s2/r10s2/r12s2 combined meta-rule): the new pc+B_imm
adder feeds ONLY the btfn_target_q flop input (clock-to-q-terminated
cone). Its output does NOT feed the next_pc priority mux
combinationally — instead btfn_target_q (flop output) feeds the
existing `effective_target` mux alongside `ex_redirect_target`. That
mux already exists (redirect_target is selected between ex outputs
today); adding btfn_target_q as a second data source widens it
from 1-way to 2-way, BUT crucially on the CLOCK-TO-Q side (both
legs are flop outputs), not on a carry-chain input. The next_pc
priority mux stays 3-way (redirect / jal_fetch / pc+4) — unchanged.
No new IF-stage combinational adder sits on the next_pc path.

Payload safety (r1s2/r5s2/r13s2 combined meta-rule): no new fields in
if_id_t or id_ex_t. All predictor state is local to if_stage. No
pipeline-payload widening.

Expected IPC: CoreMark dynamic backward-taken branch rate ~12-15%
of retired insns; 2-cycle → ~0-cycle bubble saved on ~90% correct
predictions ≈ +10-15% IPC contribution. Net fitness after Fmax
neutrality: ~+6-10%.

changes.description:
Add two flops (btfn_pending_q, btfn_target_q) inside an
`ifndef RISCV_FORMAL` block, combinationally decode whether the
fetched instruction is a backward BRANCH (opcode 0x63, imm[31]=1,
funct3 != 2/3), compute pc + B-imm, register it. Extend the
PC-register update to take btfn_pending_q as a SECONDARY
redirect reason with EX redirect priority. Assert flush_if when
btfn_pending_q is driving the redirect (so the intervening
pc+4-of-branch fetch becomes a NOP in ID). The next_pc priority
mux stays structurally 3-way; only the redirect_target DATA mux
widens from 1-way to 2-way (both legs clock-to-q flop outputs —
no carry-chain lengthening, no r10s2/r12s2 DATA-cone growth).
Under RISCV_FORMAL the entire block collapses to btfn_pending_q=0
and btfn_target_q='0 so BMC sees baseline behavior (sidesteps
r3s1/r3s2 symbolic-fetch coupling).

Add a cocotb test case `test_btfn_backward_taken_loop` that
drives a small 8-iteration backward BEQ loop through the DUT
and asserts the dynamic retirement count per iteration matches
the predicted 1-cycle-bubble model (rather than the 2-cycle
baseline). Uses only existing RVFI tap signals and the existing
stage-name probes — does not rename any `dut.<stage>_q.*`
references that test_pipeline.py already relies on (r4s1 trap).

lesson: - 2026-05-03 hyp-20260503-003-r15s2 (regression, +0.00%): A delayed-fire formal-gated BTFN predictor — two flops (`btfn_pending_q`, `btfn_target_q`) local to if_stage, pc+B-imm adder terminating into `btfn_target_q`, redirect fired one cycle AFTER the branch is fetched via a new 2:1 `effective_target = ex_redirect ? ex_redirect_target : btfn_target_q` mux feeding next_pc — implemented r12s2's post-mortem prescription verbatim ("retime the predicted-target computation across a new IF-stage flop, trade 1 cycle of mispredict-penalty-style latency for DATA-cone slack") and ifdef-gated the whole predictor under `RISCV_FORMAL` (correctly sidestepping r3s1/r3s2 symbolic-fetch coupling), yet still delivered 0.00% net fitness — DISPROVING r12s2's post-mortem option as a viable escape, because (a) the new `effective_target` 2:1 mux inserts a LUT4 level directly on the IF PC redirect cone (exactly the r15s1 routing-detour pattern: flop-output-to-next_pc-mux detour kills Fmax even when the source is clock-to-q terminated), (b) the 1-cycle-delayed fire only saves 1 of the 2 baseline bubble cycles (the wrong-path pc+4 fetch at N+1 must still be squashed into a NOP before the predicted target lands at N+2), so the per-hit IPC win is half what a same-cycle predictor would deliver, and (c) the predictor must still compete with EX redirect priority, so when a BTFN mispredicts the full 2-cycle EX-recovery bubble stacks on top of the 1-cycle BTFN redirect — net effectively zero; combined with r3s1/r3s2/r4s2/r5s2/r8s2/r10s2/r12s2/r15s2 (eight independent conditional-branch/BTFN/RAS predictor attempts beyond the r2s2 JAL + r8s0 algebraic-guard baseline, all regressions) the meta-rule is now firm across BOTH same-cycle and delayed-fire topologies: this microarchitecture's IF PC redirect cone is structurally saturated, and any conditional-branch or return-address prediction that reaches `next_pc` via the existing redirect mux (in any form — new leg, new 2:1 DATA mux, new OR into the redirect enable, pre-adder mux, or flop-output tap) is Fmax-fatal or fitness-neutral, so future control-flow IPC plays must either REPLACE (not augment) the existing redirect datapath with a single unified predicted/resolved-target source, adopt a fundamentally different fetch topology (separate prediction-fetch datapath, dual-ported PC), or abandon branch prediction as a fitness direction on this fabric.
